bills.plain_summary: The E-Access Act would require federal agencies to publish government documents and records online in accessible digital formats at no cost to the public. This bill aims to make it easier for ordinary Americans to find and read official government information without paying fees or visiting offices in person. The bill has been referred to the House Committee on Energy and Commerce, meaning it is in the early stages of the legislative process.

impacts.text: Federal agencies may face significant costs and staffing burdens to digitize and maintain large volumes of existing documents online.
Everyday Americans would gain free, convenient online access to government records that currently require fees or in-person requests.
The bill could shift how agencies allocate IT and administrative budgets, potentially affecting other government services depending on implementation.

bills.who_affected: General public, journalists, researchers, small business owners, legal professionals, people with disabilities, low-income Americans without means to pay record fees
bills.ticker_alert: 📋 New bill would make federal government documents free and online for all Americans

bills.title: Stop Illegal Alien Cops Act
bills.plain_title: Ban on Non-Citizens Serving as Police Officers
bills.deck: This bill would prohibit non-U.S. citizens from working as law enforcement officers anywhere in the United States.
bills.plain_summary: The Stop Illegal Alien Cops Act would make it illegal for anyone who is not a U.S. citizen to serve as a police officer or law enforcement official at the federal, state, or local level. Some cities and states have recently changed their laws to allow non-citizens, including undocumented immigrants, to join police forces to address officer shortages. This bill was referred to the House Judiciary Committee, meaning it is in the early stages of the legislative process and has not yet been voted on.

impacts.text: Law enforcement agencies already facing staffing shortages could lose eligible recruits, potentially leaving communities with fewer officers on patrol.
Supporters argue ensuring all officers are U.S. citizens strengthens national security and public trust in law enforcement institutions.
The bill's effect depends heavily on how many non-citizen officers currently serve and whether states would be legally compelled to comply with a federal mandate.

bills.who_affected: Non-citizen legal residents seeking law enforcement careers, local and state police departments, communities with officer shortages, undocumented immigrants, taxpayers funding public safety
bills.ticker_alert: 🚨 Bill introduced to ban non-citizens from serving as police officers nationwide

bills.title: CLEAR Path Act
bills.plain_title: Streamlining Nuclear Energy Licensing and Permitting
bills.deck: This bill reforms federal processes to make it faster and cheaper to build new nuclear power plants in the U.S.
bills.plain_summary: The CLEAR Path Act (Creating Helpful Incentives to Produce Semiconductors... no — Clearing Legislative Enhancements for Advanced Reactors) reforms the Nuclear Regulatory Commission's licensing and permitting procedures to reduce delays and costs for building new nuclear reactors, including advanced reactor designs. It aims to modernize outdated regulatory frameworks that critics say make U.S. nuclear energy uncompetitive. The bill has been placed on the Senate Legislative Calendar, meaning it awaits a floor vote.

impacts.text: Critics argue streamlined licensing could reduce safety review time for nuclear facilities, potentially increasing risks to nearby communities.
Faster nuclear plant approvals could lower energy costs long-term and help the U.S. meet clean energy goals without relying solely on fossil fuels.
The bill shifts regulatory authority and timelines at the NRC, which supporters call modernization and opponents call a weakening of oversight.

bills.who_affected: Energy ratepayers, nuclear industry workers, environmental advocates, communities near nuclear facilities, utility companies, clean energy investors
bills.ticker_alert: ⚡ Senate bill would fast-track permits for new U.S. nuclear power plants

bills.title: Carlton H. Ingram Veterans’ Benefits Improvement Act
bills.plain_title: Veterans Benefits Improvement and Modernization Act
bills.deck: This bill proposes updates to how veterans receive and appeal their federal benefits.
bills.plain_summary: The Carlton H. Ingram Veterans' Benefits Improvement Act (HR 8066) aims to improve how the Department of Veterans Affairs delivers and processes benefits to veterans, including potential changes to claims, appeals, or payment processes. The bill is named after Carlton H. Ingram, honoring a figure connected to veterans' service. It has been referred to the House Committee on Veterans' Affairs, meaning it is in the early stages of consideration and has not yet been voted on.

impacts.text: Implementation costs could divert VA funding from direct veteran healthcare or services if not separately appropriated.
Veterans who have struggled with slow or denied benefits claims could see faster decisions and improved access to earned benefits.
The bill's actual scope and specific provisions are not fully detailed in public summaries, making it difficult to assess the full range of changes.

bills.who_affected: U.S. military veterans, surviving spouses of veterans, VA benefits claimants, VA administrative staff, veterans service organizations
bills.ticker_alert: ⚖️ New bill proposes improvements to veterans' federal benefits claims process

bills.title: Taxpayer Funds Oversight and Accountability Act
bills.plain_title: Federal Spending Oversight and Accountability Act
bills.deck: This bill would strengthen oversight of how the federal government spends taxpayer money.
bills.plain_summary: The Taxpayer Funds Oversight and Accountability Act (HR 8340) aims to increase transparency and accountability in how the federal government spends public money. It was referred to the House Committee on Oversight and Government Reform, meaning it is still in the early stages of the legislative process and has not yet been voted on. The bill reflects ongoing congressional efforts to reduce waste, fraud, and abuse in federal spending.

impacts.text: Could add bureaucratic reporting requirements that slow down legitimate government programs and service delivery
May help reduce waste and fraud in federal spending, potentially saving taxpayers billions of dollars over time
The actual effectiveness depends heavily on the specific enforcement mechanisms written into the bill, which are not yet widely detailed

bills.who_affected: Federal agency employees, government contractors, taxpayers, recipients of federal grants and programs, congressional oversight staff
bills.ticker_alert: 💰 New bill aims to boost oversight of how your tax dollars are spent

bills.title: 6PPD Task Force Act
bills.plain_title: Task Force to Study Tire Chemical Harming Salmon
bills.deck: Creates a federal task force to study and address 6PPD, a tire rubber chemical killing salmon and other fish.
bills.plain_summary: This bill would establish a federal task force to study 6PPD-quinone, a toxic chemical produced when a common tire preservative reacts with ozone. The chemical washes off roads into waterways and has been linked to mass deaths of coho salmon and other fish. The bill was referred to multiple House committees and has not yet been voted on.

impacts.text: Potential costs to tire manufacturers and drivers if task force findings lead to regulations requiring reformulated tires
Could protect salmon populations and drinking water sources by identifying actionable solutions to a known toxic chemical threat
The task force only studies the problem and makes recommendations — it does not directly ban or regulate 6PPD chemicals

bills.who_affected: Commercial and recreational fishermen, Pacific Northwest communities, tire manufacturers, environmental agencies, Native American tribes with fishing treaty rights, wildlife conservationists
bills.ticker_alert: 🐟 Congress eyes toxic tire chemical devastating salmon populations nationwide

bills.title: Breast Cancer Research Stamp Reauthorization Act of 2026
bills.plain_title: Keep the Breast Cancer Awareness Stamp Going
bills.deck: This bill extends the special postage stamp that raises money for breast cancer research.
bills.plain_summary: This bill would reauthorize the Breast Cancer Research semipostal stamp, which lets Americans pay a little extra when buying postage, with the extra money going to breast cancer research funding. The stamp has raised hundreds of millions of dollars since 1998 and requires periodic reauthorization by Congress to keep selling. The bill has been referred to multiple House committees for review before any floor vote.

impacts.text: Buyers pay slightly above face value for the stamp, meaning it costs more than standard postage for everyday mailers.
Extends a proven fundraising tool that has generated over $100 million for breast cancer research, potentially saving lives.
Critics question whether disease-specific fundraising through postal stamps is the most efficient or equitable way to direct medical research dollars.

bills.who_affected: Breast cancer patients and survivors, medical researchers, everyday stamp buyers, postal customers, veterans (via Armed Services committee jurisdiction)
bills.ticker_alert: 🎗️ Congress considers extending breast cancer research stamp fundraising program

bills.title: SAFER Act of 2026
bills.plain_title: Bank Account Access Rules for Cannabis Businesses
bills.deck: This bill would allow legal cannabis businesses to access banking and financial services without federal penalties.
bills.plain_summary: The SAFER Act of 2026 (HR 8338) would protect banks and credit unions from federal punishment if they provide accounts, loans, or other financial services to cannabis businesses operating legally under state law. Currently, most banks refuse to serve cannabis companies because marijuana remains illegal federally, forcing many to operate as cash-only businesses. This bill has been referred to the House Committee on Financial Services, where it awaits further consideration.

impacts.text: Critics argue it could expand cannabis access and use by making the industry more financially stable and legitimate without fully resolving the federal-state legal conflict.
Legal cannabis businesses and their employees could gain access to checking accounts, payroll services, and loans, reducing dangerous cash-only operations and improving worker safety.
The bill does not legalize cannabis federally — it only addresses banking access, leaving the broader debate over marijuana's legal status unresolved.

bills.who_affected: Cannabis business owners, dispensary employees, banks and credit unions, law enforcement, state tax authorities, small business owners in cannabis-legal states
bills.ticker_alert: 🏦 SAFER Act would open banks to legal cannabis businesses nationwide

bills.title: FIGHT Act of 2025
bills.plain_title: Farm and Food Import Trade Enforcement Act
bills.deck: This bill strengthens enforcement against unfair foreign agricultural imports that undercut American farmers.
bills.plain_summary: The FIGHT Act of 2025 (HR 3946) aims to protect American farmers by cracking down on foreign agricultural products that enter the U.S. market through unfair trade practices, such as subsidies or dumping below cost. The bill would give federal agencies stronger tools to investigate and restrict these imports. It was referred to the House Committee on Agriculture, where it awaits review before any further action.

impacts.text: Stricter import restrictions could raise grocery prices for American consumers if cheaper foreign food products are blocked or taxed.
American farmers could see fairer competition and higher prices for their crops if foreign dumping and subsidized imports are curbed.
Stronger trade enforcement could prompt retaliatory measures from trading partners, affecting U.S. agricultural export markets as well.

bills.who_affected: American farmers and ranchers, food importers and distributors, grocery consumers, agricultural trade workers, foreign food exporters
bills.ticker_alert: 🌾 FIGHT Act targets unfair foreign farm imports to protect U.S. agriculture

bills.title: Women and Lung Cancer Research and Preventive Services Act of 2025
bills.plain_title: Lung Cancer Research and Prevention for Women Act
bills.deck: Expands Medicare coverage for lung cancer screenings and boosts research funding for women with lung cancer.
bills.plain_summary: This bill would require Medicare to cover lung cancer screenings and related preventive services for women without cost-sharing, meaning no copays or deductibles. It also directs federal agencies to increase research into why lung cancer affects women differently than men. The bill passed the House and its latest action suggests it moved through procedural steps, though Senate passage would still be needed to become law.

impacts.text: Expanded Medicare coverage could increase federal spending, potentially adding to the national deficit over time.
Women on Medicare could receive lung cancer screenings at no out-of-pocket cost, enabling earlier detection and better survival rates.
The bill focuses research dollars specifically on women, which some say corrects a historical gap while others argue it narrows broader cancer research priorities.

bills.who_affected: Women aged 50 and older, Medicare beneficiaries, lung cancer patients, oncology researchers, public health agencies
bills.ticker_alert: 🩺 House passes bill expanding free lung cancer screenings for women on Medicare

bills.title: To amend the Public Health Service Act to reauthorize the telehealth network and telehealth resource centers grant programs.
bills.plain_title: Renewing Federal Grants for Telehealth Networks
bills.deck: This bill extends federal funding for programs that help rural and underserved communities access healthcare via technology.
bills.plain_summary: This bill reauthorizes two federal grant programs under the Public Health Service Act that support telehealth networks and resource centers — organizations that help clinics, hospitals, and patients in rural or underserved areas use video and remote healthcare technology. By renewing these grants, Congress would continue funding the infrastructure and training needed to expand telemedicine access. The bill has passed its final procedural step in Congress, meaning it has been agreed to without objection.

impacts.text: Continued federal spending on telehealth grants adds to the national budget without guaranteed measurable outcomes or cost-effectiveness reviews.
Patients in rural and low-income communities gain continued access to remote medical care, reducing travel burdens and improving health outcomes.
The bill reauthorizes existing programs rather than creating new ones, so its real-world effect depends heavily on how grant funds are distributed and used by recipients.

bills.who_affected: Rural residents, low-income patients, community health centers, telehealth providers, medical professionals in underserved areas
bills.ticker_alert: 📡 Congress renews telehealth grants to expand rural and underserved healthcare access

bills.title: Improving Care in Rural America Reauthorization Act of 2025
bills.plain_title: Renewing Rural Healthcare Support Programs
bills.deck: Reauthorizes federal programs that help rural communities access better healthcare services.
bills.plain_summary: This bill renews existing federal programs designed to improve healthcare access and quality in rural areas of the United States. It extends funding and support for initiatives that help rural hospitals, clinics, and health workers serve communities that often struggle to attract medical professionals and resources. The bill passed the House and its motion to reconsider was laid on the table, meaning it has cleared an early legislative hurdle.

impacts.text: Reauthorization without program reforms may continue funding ineffective or outdated rural health initiatives without accountability improvements.
Rural residents gain continued access to federally supported health services, reducing care gaps in underserved communities.
The bill extends existing programs rather than creating new ones, so its real-world effect depends heavily on how agencies implement and fund the programs.

bills.who_affected: Rural residents, rural hospital staff, rural primary care physicians, rural community health workers, small-town patients, federally qualified health center employees
bills.ticker_alert: 🏥 Rural healthcare programs renewed — access extended for millions in small towns

bills.title: Commission on Advancing Restorative Justice in Elementary and Secondary Education Act of 2026
bills.plain_title: Study Commission on Restorative Justice in K-12 Schools
bills.deck: Creates a federal commission to study replacing school suspensions with restorative justice practices.
bills.plain_summary: This bill would establish a federal commission to study the use of restorative justice programs in K-12 public schools as alternatives to traditional discipline like suspensions and expulsions. Restorative justice focuses on repairing harm and keeping students in school rather than removing them. The bill has been referred to the House Committee on Education and Workforce, where it awaits further action.

impacts.text: Critics worry reduced suspensions could leave teachers and students less safe if disruptive or harmful behavior goes insufficiently addressed
Students — especially minorities who face disproportionate suspension rates — could benefit from approaches that keep them in school and on track to graduate
The commission would only study and recommend policies, not mandate any school discipline changes, making real-world impact uncertain

bills.who_affected: K-12 public school students, teachers and school staff, school administrators, parents, minority and low-income student communities
bills.ticker_alert: 📋 New bill proposes federal study of restorative justice in K-12 schools

bills.title: Transit Workforce Development Act
bills.plain_title: Training Program for Public Transit Workers
bills.deck: Creates federal grants to train and develop a skilled workforce for public transit systems nationwide.
bills.plain_summary: This bill would establish a federal grant program to help public transit agencies recruit, train, and retain workers, including bus drivers, mechanics, and rail operators. It aims to address staffing shortages that have reduced service in many cities. The bill was referred to the House Committee on Transportation and Infrastructure, meaning it has not yet been debated or voted on.

impacts.text: Creates new federal spending and bureaucracy that critics say duplicates existing workforce programs already funded by transit agencies.
Riders in understaffed transit systems could see more frequent and reliable bus and train service as agencies fill open positions.
Grant distribution formulas will determine which cities and transit agencies benefit most, creating winners and losers among regions.

bills.who_affected: Public transit riders, transit agency employees, job seekers in transportation sector, urban and suburban commuters, local transit agencies
bills.ticker_alert: 🚌 New bill would fund training programs for public transit workers nationwide

bills.title: Keep Public Funds in Public Schools Act
bills.plain_title: Ban Federal Tax Breaks for Private School Voucher Programs
bills.deck: This bill would eliminate federal tax deductions for donations that fund private school voucher programs.
bills.plain_summary: The Keep Public Funds in Public Schools Act would remove federal tax incentives for contributions to scholarship programs that send students to private or religious schools using public-adjacent funding. It targets so-called 'neo-voucher' programs where donors receive state tax credits for giving money to private school scholarship funds. The bill was introduced in the U.S. Senate and has been referred to the Senate Finance Committee, where most tax-related legislation is first reviewed.

impacts.text: Families relying on private school scholarships funded by donor tax incentives could lose access to those educational options if donation funding dries up.
Supporters say it would protect public school funding by closing a loophole that effectively redirects tax dollars away from public education systems.
The bill reignites a long-standing debate over school choice versus public school investment, with contested evidence on how voucher programs affect student outcomes.

bills.who_affected: Public school students and teachers, private and religious school students, low-income families using school vouchers, taxpayers, state education departments, donors to scholarship organizations
bills.ticker_alert: 🏫 Senate bill would strip federal tax breaks from private school voucher donations

bills.title: Communications, Video, and Technology Accessibility Act of 2026
bills.plain_title: Disability Access Rules for TV, Internet, and Tech Services
bills.deck: This bill updates federal rules to ensure people with disabilities can access modern communications and video technology.
bills.plain_summary: The Communications, Video, and Technology Accessibility Act of 2026 would modernize accessibility requirements for television, internet-based video, and communications services so that people with disabilities — including those who are deaf, hard of hearing, blind, or have mobility impairments — can fully use them. It aims to update outdated laws to cover newer streaming platforms and digital technologies. The bill has been referred to the House Committee on Energy and Commerce, meaning it is in the early stages of the legislative process.

impacts.text: Compliance costs for tech companies and smaller streaming services could increase, potentially raising subscription prices for all consumers.
Millions of Americans with visual, hearing, or mobility disabilities would gain improved access to streaming video, apps, and online communications tools.
The bill would expand the FCC's regulatory authority over newer digital and streaming platforms, which supporters call overdue and critics call government overreach.

bills.who_affected: Deaf and hard-of-hearing Americans, blind and low-vision Americans, people with mobility or cognitive disabilities, streaming service providers, telecom companies, app developers, seniors with age-related disabilities
bills.ticker_alert: ♿ Bill aims to modernize disability access rules for streaming and digital tech

bills.title: Clean Slate through Rehabilitation Act
bills.plain_title: Second Chance Job Training for People with Criminal Records
bills.deck: This bill would fund job training and education programs for people with criminal records to help them find stable employment.
bills.plain_summary: The Clean Slate through Rehabilitation Act would create or expand federal funding for workforce training, education, and rehabilitation programs aimed at people who have been convicted of crimes. The goal is to help formerly incarcerated individuals gain skills and find jobs, reducing repeat offenses. The bill has been referred to the House Committee on Education and Workforce, meaning it is in early stages and has not yet been voted on.

impacts.text: Critics argue federal spending on job training for convicted individuals could divert limited education funds away from law-abiding citizens seeking workforce development.
People with criminal records gain access to job training and education, improving their chances of employment and reducing likelihood of reoffending.
The effectiveness of rehabilitation-based workforce programs varies widely by implementation, making long-term outcomes difficult to predict without program specifics.

bills.who_affected: Formerly incarcerated individuals, employers hiring ex-offenders, crime victims, federal workforce training administrators, taxpayers, low-income communities with high incarceration rates
bills.ticker_alert: 📋 House bill proposes job training funding for Americans with criminal records

bills.title: DAIRY PRIDE Act
bills.plain_title: Dairy Labeling Standards: Milk Must Come From Animals
bills.deck: This bill would prohibit plant-based drinks like oat or almond milk from using the word 'milk' on their labels.
bills.plain_summary: The DAIRY PRIDE Act would require the FDA to enforce existing rules that limit the word 'milk' (and similar terms like 'cheese' and 'yogurt') to products that come from animals. Plant-based alternatives like almond, oat, or soy beverages would have to change their labeling. The bill has been referred to the House Committee on Energy and Commerce, meaning it has not yet been debated or voted on.

impacts.text: Consumers who buy plant-based beverages could face confusion and higher costs as brands are forced to relabel products they already recognize.
Dairy farmers could benefit from clearer marketplace distinctions, potentially reducing consumer confusion between animal dairy and plant-based alternatives.
The bill reignites a long-running debate over whether shared food terms mislead consumers or simply reflect common, widely understood language.

bills.who_affected: Dairy farmers, plant-based food manufacturers, grocery retailers, lactose-intolerant consumers, vegans and vegetarians, FDA regulators
bills.ticker_alert: 🥛 Bill would ban 'milk' label on oat, almond, and soy drinks

bills.title: Ban Presidential Plunder of Taxpayer Funds Act
bills.plain_title: Ban on Presidents Profiting from Taxpayer Money
bills.deck: This bill would prohibit presidents and vice presidents from personally profiting from federal government spending.
bills.plain_summary: This bill would make it illegal for a sitting president or vice president to personally benefit financially from federal taxpayer funds, such as through government contracts or payments to their businesses. It was introduced in the U.S. Senate and has been sent to the Senate Judiciary Committee for review. The bill has not yet been voted on and faces an uncertain path forward.

impacts.text: Critics say the bill's enforcement mechanisms may be difficult to implement and could invite politically motivated legal battles against executives.
Taxpayers would gain stronger legal protections ensuring their money cannot flow back to businesses owned or controlled by the sitting president or vice president.
Courts and legal experts would likely debate what counts as 'profiting' from government funds, creating potential ambiguity in enforcement.

bills.who_affected: U.S. taxpayers, current and future presidents and vice presidents, federal contractors, ethics watchdog organizations, Congress members
bills.ticker_alert: 🚫 Senate bill would ban presidents from profiting off taxpayer dollars

bills.title: Directing the President, pursuant to section 5(c) of the War Powers Resolution, to remove United States Armed Forces from hostilities with Iran.
bills.plain_title: Order U.S. Troops Removed from Iran Hostilities
bills.deck: This bill directs the President to withdraw U.S. military forces from any armed conflict with Iran.
bills.plain_summary: This concurrent resolution uses the War Powers Resolution to formally order the President to pull U.S. Armed Forces out of hostilities involving Iran. It was referred to the House Committee on Foreign Affairs, which is the first step in the legislative process. The bill reflects congressional concern that military action against Iran may be occurring without a formal declaration of war or proper congressional authorization.

impacts.text: Critics argue it could weaken U.S. deterrence against Iran, potentially emboldening Iranian aggression toward American allies and troops in the region.
Could prevent an unauthorized escalation into a full-scale war with Iran, reducing risk to U.S. military personnel and avoiding massive economic costs.
The bill reignites a longstanding constitutional debate over whether Congress or the President holds primary authority to commit U.S. forces to hostilities.

bills.who_affected: U.S. military service members and their families, American civilians in the Middle East, U.S. allies in the region, defense contractors, Members of Congress
bills.ticker_alert: ⚠️ Congress seeks to order President to halt U.S. military hostilities with Iran

bills.title: TALENTS Act
bills.plain_title: Hire Veterans for Federal Government Jobs Act
bills.deck: This bill requires federal agencies to prioritize hiring unemployed veterans for certain government positions.
bills.plain_summary: The TALENTS Act directs federal agencies to give hiring preference to unemployed veterans when filling certain government jobs, aiming to reduce veteran unemployment by connecting military experience with public service roles. The bill was referred to the House Committee on Oversight and Government Reform, meaning it is in early review stages and has not yet been voted on. It reflects ongoing congressional efforts to translate military skills into civilian federal employment opportunities.

impacts.text: Non-veteran job seekers may face a stronger disadvantage competing for federal positions, reducing their hiring opportunities in the public sector.
Unemployed veterans gain a stronger pathway into stable federal jobs, potentially reducing veteran unemployment and honoring military service.
Federal agencies may need to adjust hiring processes and timelines, which could affect how quickly vacant government positions are filled.

bills.who_affected: Unemployed military veterans, federal job applicants without military service, federal agency HR departments, Department of Veterans Affairs beneficiaries
bills.ticker_alert: 🎖️ Bill would prioritize unemployed veterans for federal government hiring

bills.title: Tech to Save Moms Act
bills.plain_title: Using Technology to Reduce Maternal Deaths
bills.deck: This bill promotes digital health tools to help prevent pregnancy-related deaths and complications in the U.S.
bills.plain_summary: The Tech to Save Moms Act directs federal agencies to study and expand the use of digital health technologies — like telehealth, wearable devices, and health apps — to reduce maternal mortality and improve care for pregnant and postpartum women. It aims to address the U.S.'s high maternal death rate, which disproportionately affects Black women. The bill has been referred to the House Committee on Energy and Commerce and has not yet been voted on.

impacts.text: Critics warn that focusing on tech solutions may distract from addressing systemic racism and provider bias that drive racial disparities in maternal deaths.
Expanding telehealth and remote monitoring could give pregnant women in rural or underserved areas better access to prenatal and postpartum care.
The bill's effectiveness depends heavily on which technologies are prioritized, how funding is allocated, and whether low-income women have reliable internet access.

bills.who_affected: Pregnant women, postpartum mothers, Black and minority women, rural residents, obstetricians and midwives, telehealth providers, Medicaid recipients
bills.ticker_alert: 📱 Bill seeks to use digital health tech to cut maternal deaths

bills.title: Public Company Advisory Committee Act of 2026
bills.plain_title: Create Advisory Committee for Public Companies
bills.deck: This bill establishes a new advisory committee to advise the SEC on issues affecting publicly traded companies.
bills.plain_summary: This bill would create a formal advisory committee within the Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) focused specifically on the concerns of publicly traded companies. The committee would provide recommendations on regulations, reporting requirements, and other rules that affect businesses listed on U.S. stock exchanges. The bill has been placed on the Union Calendar, meaning it has passed committee review and is awaiting a full House vote.

impacts.text: Critics argue it could give large corporations more influence over SEC rulemaking, potentially weakening investor and consumer protections.
Supporters say it gives public companies a structured voice in shaping regulations, potentially reducing costly or burdensome compliance rules.
The committee would be advisory only, meaning the SEC is not required to follow its recommendations, limiting its direct authority.

bills.who_affected: Shareholders and retail investors, publicly traded corporations, SEC staff and regulators, corporate executives and boards, financial industry professionals
bills.ticker_alert: 📋 Bill would create new SEC advisory panel for publicly traded companies

bills.title: Workforce Recovery and Resilience Act
bills.plain_title: Job Training and Worker Recovery Support Act
bills.deck: This bill creates programs to help unemployed workers retrain and find new jobs faster.
bills.plain_summary: The Workforce Recovery and Resilience Act aims to strengthen job training programs and expand support for Americans who have lost their jobs, helping them gain new skills and re-enter the workforce. The bill has been referred to the House Committee on Education and Workforce, meaning it is still in the early stages of the legislative process and has not yet been voted on. It is part of a broader effort to address workforce disruptions caused by economic changes and industry shifts.

impacts.text: New federal spending on workforce programs could increase the deficit or divert resources from other priorities.
Displaced workers could gain access to more retraining opportunities, potentially speeding up their return to stable employment.
Effectiveness of federally funded job training programs is debated, with mixed evidence on whether they produce lasting employment gains.

bills.who_affected: Unemployed workers, displaced manufacturing and service workers, job training providers, community colleges, small business owners, low-income job seekers
bills.ticker_alert: 💼 New bill proposes expanded job training for displaced American workers

bills.title: Smithsonian American Women’s History Museum Act
bills.plain_title: Create a Women's History Museum on the National Mall
bills.deck: This bill establishes a new Smithsonian museum dedicated to American women's history in Washington, D.C.
bills.plain_summary: This bill would create the Smithsonian American Women's History Museum as an official Smithsonian Institution museum, likely located on or near the National Mall in Washington, D.C. It establishes a board of trustees to oversee the museum's development, collections, and programming celebrating women's contributions to American history. The bill was ordered to advance out of committee by a narrow 7-4 vote, meaning it has cleared one hurdle but still needs full House and Senate approval to become law.

impacts.text: Taxpayers will fund construction and ongoing operations, potentially costing hundreds of millions of dollars over time.
Millions of visitors annually would gain access to a dedicated institution preserving and celebrating women's often-overlooked contributions to U.S. history.
Finding a suitable location on the already crowded National Mall could require years of planning and may displace other proposed uses for limited federal land.

bills.who_affected: American taxpayers, historians and educators, women's advocacy groups, museum professionals, tourists and students visiting Washington D.C.
bills.ticker_alert: 🏛️ New Smithsonian women's history museum advances out of committee 7-4

bills.title: Denouncing statements by President Donald J. Trump that he may "nationalize," commandeer, or otherwise assume direct control over elections.
bills.plain_title: Congress Opposes Trump's Threats to Control Elections
bills.deck: This resolution formally condemns President Trump's statements suggesting he could take federal control of U.S. elections.
bills.plain_summary: This is a House resolution that officially denounces statements made by President Trump suggesting he could 'nationalize' or assume direct control over how elections are run in the United States. Elections are currently managed by individual states and local governments, not the federal executive branch. The resolution has been referred to the House Judiciary Committee and has no binding legal force — it is a symbolic statement of Congressional disapproval.

impacts.text: Critics say the resolution is purely symbolic and does nothing to legally prevent any executive overreach into election administration.
Supporters say it puts Congress on record defending state and local control of elections, reinforcing constitutional separation of powers.
The resolution has no enforcement mechanism and reflects a political disagreement about presidential authority over elections, not a change in law.

bills.who_affected: Voters in all 50 states, state election officials, local election administrators, members of Congress, constitutional law experts
bills.ticker_alert: 🗳️ House resolution condemns Trump's talk of federal election takeover

bills.title: Safeguarding Taxpayer Dollars in Child Care Act of 2026
bills.plain_title: Child Care Subsidy Fraud Prevention and Accountability Act
bills.deck: Requires stricter oversight and audits of federal child care subsidy programs to reduce fraud and waste.
bills.plain_summary: This bill would add new accountability and anti-fraud requirements to the Child Care and Development Fund, the federal program that helps low-income families pay for child care. It would require states to conduct more rigorous background checks, verification of provider eligibility, and audits to ensure taxpayer money is spent correctly. The bill has been placed on the Union Calendar, meaning it is ready for a full House vote.

impacts.text: Stricter verification requirements could delay or disrupt child care subsidies for eligible low-income families, leaving some parents temporarily without assistance
Stronger oversight could reduce fraudulent billing by unqualified providers, ensuring more federal dollars reach legitimate child care services for families in need
States would face new administrative and reporting burdens, which could mean redirecting existing staff resources or requesting additional federal administrative funding

bills.who_affected: Low-income parents receiving child care subsidies, licensed child care providers, state child care agencies, federal taxpayers
bills.ticker_alert: 🔍 House bill targets fraud in federal child care subsidy program

bills.title: Combatting Money Laundering in Cyber Crime Act of 2025
bills.plain_title: Cracking Down on Cryptocurrency Use in Cybercrime
bills.deck: This bill requires financial institutions to better detect and report cryptocurrency used to fund or profit from cyberattacks.
bills.plain_summary: This bill updates anti-money laundering laws to specifically address how criminals use digital currencies and online payment systems to hide profits from cybercrime. It would require banks and crypto exchanges to strengthen monitoring and reporting of suspicious transactions linked to hacking, ransomware, and other cyber offenses. The bill has been placed on the Union Calendar, meaning it is eligible for a full House floor vote.

impacts.text: Crypto exchanges and fintech companies face higher compliance costs, which could be passed on to everyday users as fees.
Stronger tracking of cybercrime profits could help law enforcement recover funds stolen from ransomware victims, including hospitals and schools.
The bill expands government visibility into cryptocurrency transactions, raising ongoing debate between security needs and financial privacy rights.

bills.who_affected: Cryptocurrency users, banks and financial institutions, crypto exchanges, cybercrime victims, law enforcement agencies, fintech companies
bills.ticker_alert: 💻 New bill targets cryptocurrency used to bankroll cyberattacks and ransomware

bills.title: SCAM Act
bills.plain_title: Bill to Combat Online Scams Targeting Americans
bills.deck: This bill would create new federal penalties for scammers who use technology to defraud Americans online.
bills.plain_summary: The SCAM Act (Stop Cyber and Mail-based Abuse and Manipulation Act) would strengthen federal laws against online fraud and scams, adding new criminal penalties for those who use digital tools or mail to deceive and steal from Americans. The bill was referred to the House Judiciary Committee, where it must be reviewed before moving forward. It targets a wide range of fraudulent schemes that have grown significantly with the rise of the internet and digital communications.

impacts.text: Broadly worded provisions could potentially expose small businesses or marketers to criminal liability for aggressive but legal sales practices.
Stronger federal penalties could deter scammers, offering greater protection to the millions of Americans who lose billions of dollars annually to fraud.
Enforcement would depend heavily on federal agency resources and prosecutorial priorities, meaning real-world impact could vary widely by region.

bills.who_affected: Online fraud victims, elderly Americans, small business owners, digital marketers, federal law enforcement agencies, telecommunications companies
bills.ticker_alert: 🚨 SCAM Act would add new federal penalties for online fraudsters

bills.title: Child Care Integrity Monitoring Act of 2026
bills.plain_title: Child Care Program Fraud Prevention and Oversight Bill
bills.deck: Requires stricter monitoring and verification of child care subsidy programs to reduce fraud and waste.
bills.plain_summary: This bill would strengthen oversight of federally funded child care assistance programs by requiring better identity verification, provider checks, and fraud detection systems. It aims to ensure that child care subsidies go to legitimate providers and eligible families. The bill has been placed on the Union Calendar, meaning it has passed committee review and is awaiting a full House vote.

impacts.text: Increased paperwork and verification burdens could make it harder for low-income families to quickly access child care assistance they need.
Reduces taxpayer money lost to fraudulent child care providers, potentially freeing up more funds for legitimate families in need.
States would face new federal reporting and compliance requirements, which some see as needed accountability and others view as bureaucratic overreach.

bills.who_affected: Low-income working parents, child care providers, state child care administrators, children enrolled in subsidized care programs
bills.ticker_alert: 🔍 House bill targets child care subsidy fraud with tougher oversight rules

bills.title: No Waivers for Fraud Act of 2026
bills.plain_title: Ban on Fraud Waivers in Government Programs
bills.deck: This bill would prohibit federal agencies from waiving fraud penalties for individuals or organizations caught cheating government programs.
bills.plain_summary: The No Waivers for Fraud Act of 2026 would prevent federal agencies from granting waivers or exceptions to fraud penalties in government benefit and contract programs. This means that if someone is found guilty of defrauding a federal program, agencies could not let them off the hook through administrative waivers. The bill has been placed on the Union Calendar, meaning it is eligible for a full House vote but has not yet passed.

impacts.text: Removes agency flexibility to consider mitigating circumstances, potentially treating minor or technical fraud violations the same as deliberate large-scale schemes
Strengthens accountability and could deter fraud in federal programs like Medicare, Medicaid, and government contracting, saving taxpayer money
The practical effect depends heavily on how 'fraud' is defined in the bill — a contested legal question that determines how broadly the waiver ban applies

bills.who_affected: Federal contractors, Medicare and Medicaid providers, recipients of federal benefits, small businesses contracting with the government, federal agency administrators
bills.ticker_alert: 🚫 House bill would ban agencies from waiving fraud penalties in federal programs

bills.title: No Funds for Repeat Child Care Violations Act of 2026
bills.plain_title: Ban Federal Funds for Repeat Child Care Violators
bills.deck: Bars federal child care funding from providers who repeatedly violate child safety rules.
bills.plain_summary: This bill would cut off federal child care assistance payments to any child care provider that has been cited multiple times for serious health or safety violations. It is designed to protect young children from neglect or harm in subsidized care settings. The bill has been placed on the Union Calendar, meaning it has cleared committee and is eligible for a full House vote.

impacts.text: Families using federal subsidies could lose access to their current provider if it is disqualified, forcing a disruptive search for new care in areas where options are scarce.
Children in federally funded care would be better protected from providers with repeated safety violations, potentially reducing incidents of harm or neglect.
The definition of what counts as a 'repeat violation' and how serious it must be is a contested policy detail that will determine how broadly or narrowly the law is applied.

bills.who_affected: Low-income families receiving child care subsidies, child care center operators, home-based child care providers, state child care licensing agencies, young children in subsidized care
bills.ticker_alert: 🚨 House bill would cut federal funds to repeat child care safety violators

bills.title: Combatting Money Laundering in Cyber Crime Act of 2025
bills.plain_title: Cracking Down on Cybercrime Money Laundering
bills.deck: This bill strengthens laws to stop criminals from hiding money earned through hacking and online fraud.
bills.plain_summary: This bill would update federal anti-money laundering laws to specifically target proceeds from cybercrimes like ransomware attacks, online fraud, and hacking. It would require financial institutions and potentially cryptocurrency exchanges to report suspicious transactions linked to cybercriminal activity. The bill was introduced in the Senate and has been referred to the Committee on Banking, Housing, and Urban Affairs, where it awaits further review.

impacts.text: Banks and crypto platforms may face new compliance burdens, potentially raising costs passed on to customers.
Ordinary Americans could see fewer ransomware attacks on hospitals, schools, and businesses as financial penalties for cybercriminals increase.
Expanded financial surveillance to catch cybercriminals could raise privacy concerns about monitoring of everyday digital transactions.

bills.who_affected: Banks and credit unions, cryptocurrency exchanges, cybercrime victims, small businesses, healthcare organizations, law enforcement agencies
bills.ticker_alert: 🔒 Senate bill targets money laundering from ransomware and cybercrime attacks

bills.title: Child Care Payment Integrity and Fraud Accountability Act of 2026
bills.plain_title: Child Care Subsidy Fraud Prevention and Accountability Act
bills.deck: This bill adds new safeguards to reduce fraud and improper payments in the federal child care assistance program.
bills.plain_summary: This bill strengthens oversight of the Child Care and Development Fund, the federal program that helps low-income families pay for child care. It introduces new verification requirements, data-matching tools, and penalties aimed at catching fraudulent providers and improper payments. The bill has been placed on the Union Calendar, meaning it is eligible for a full House vote.

impacts.text: Increased paperwork and verification requirements could delay or disrupt child care assistance for eligible low-income families already facing administrative burdens.
Taxpayer dollars are better protected by cracking down on fraudulent child care providers who have falsely billed the government for services not rendered.
The bill shifts more responsibility to states to implement new fraud-detection systems, which could vary widely in effectiveness and cost depending on state capacity.

bills.who_affected: Low-income working parents receiving child care subsidies, licensed child care providers, state child care agency administrators, federal auditors and inspectors general
bills.ticker_alert: 🔍 House bill targets fraud in child care subsidies for low-income families

bills.title: Stop Child Care Fraud Act of 2026
bills.plain_title: Child Care Subsidy Fraud Prevention and Enforcement Bill
bills.deck: Adds new rules and penalties to stop fraud in the federal child care subsidy program.
bills.plain_summary: This bill strengthens oversight of the Child Care and Development Fund (CCDF), the federal program that helps low-income families pay for child care. It introduces stricter verification requirements, new penalties for fraud by providers and families, and enhanced auditing tools. The bill has been placed on the Union Calendar, meaning it has passed committee review and is awaiting a full House vote.

impacts.text: Stricter verification rules could create more paperwork burdens for low-income families and small child care providers, potentially delaying or disrupting subsidy access.
Cracking down on fraudulent providers and billing practices could redirect more subsidy dollars to legitimate child care for working families who need it.
How aggressively federal agencies choose to enforce the new rules will determine whether the law primarily punishes bad actors or also burdens compliant families and providers.

bills.who_affected: Low-income working parents receiving child care subsidies, licensed child care providers, home-based day care operators, state child care agencies, federal oversight agencies
bills.ticker_alert: 🚨 House bill targets fraud in child care subsidies for low-income families

bills.title: Closing the Provider Fraud Gap Act
bills.plain_title: Stopping Medicaid and Medicare Billing Fraud by Providers
bills.deck: This bill requires better screening of healthcare providers to stop fraudulent billing in federal health programs.
bills.plain_summary: The Closing the Provider Fraud Gap Act aims to strengthen oversight of doctors, hospitals, and other healthcare providers who bill Medicare and Medicaid by closing loopholes that allow fraudulent or ineligible providers to receive government payments. It would require more rigorous enrollment checks and data sharing between federal and state agencies to catch bad actors. The bill was placed on the Union Calendar, meaning it has cleared committee review and is awaiting a full House vote.

impacts.text: Stricter enrollment requirements could delay or burden legitimate small healthcare providers trying to serve Medicare and Medicaid patients.
Taxpayers could save billions of dollars currently lost to fraudulent healthcare billing in federal programs each year.
States would need to update their Medicaid data systems to comply, raising questions about implementation costs and timelines.

bills.who_affected: Medicare beneficiaries, Medicaid recipients, healthcare providers, small medical practices, state Medicaid agencies, federal taxpayers
bills.ticker_alert: 🚨 House bill targets Medicare and Medicaid fraud by tightening provider screening rules

bills.title: Biodefense Diplomacy Enhancement Act
bills.plain_title: Strengthening Global Cooperation Against Biological Threats
bills.deck: Directs the U.S. government to improve international diplomacy efforts to prevent and respond to biological threats and pandemics.
bills.plain_summary: This bill requires the U.S. State Department and other federal agencies to strengthen diplomatic partnerships with foreign countries to better prepare for and respond to biological threats, including pandemics and bioterrorism. It aims to coordinate international efforts on disease surveillance, biosecurity, and outbreak response. The bill passed its committee unanimously 46-0, suggesting broad bipartisan support, and now moves forward in the legislative process.

impacts.text: May require new federal spending on international programs, potentially diverting resources from domestic public health needs.
Stronger global disease surveillance networks could provide Americans earlier warning of emerging pandemics, potentially saving lives and reducing economic disruption.
The bill's effectiveness depends heavily on how partner countries choose to cooperate, which the U.S. cannot fully control.

bills.who_affected: Public health workers, federal diplomats and State Department staff, military and defense biodefense personnel, pharmaceutical and biotech researchers, American travelers abroad
bills.ticker_alert: 🦠 Congress moves to boost global biological threat diplomacy with unanimous committee vote

bills.title: Multilateral Alignment of Technology Controls on Hardware (MATCH) Act
bills.plain_title: Coordinating Allied Nations on Tech Export Controls
bills.deck: This bill pushes U.S. allies to adopt matching restrictions on exporting advanced computer hardware to rivals.
bills.plain_summary: The MATCH Act directs the U.S. government to work with allied countries to align their export control rules on advanced semiconductors and hardware, preventing adversaries like China from obtaining restricted technology through third-party nations. It was introduced in the U.S. Senate and has been referred to the Committee on Banking, Housing, and Urban Affairs, meaning it has not yet been debated or voted on. The bill aims to close loopholes where countries without matching controls become workarounds for U.S. restrictions.

impacts.text: U.S. tech and semiconductor companies could face additional compliance burdens and reduced sales in allied markets if stricter coordinated controls are adopted.
Stronger multilateral controls could reduce the risk of advanced U.S. technology being used by adversaries to develop military capabilities that threaten American security.
Success depends entirely on whether allied nations agree to align their own export rules, which is a diplomatic challenge outside U.S. control.

bills.who_affected: Semiconductor manufacturers, technology exporters, defense contractors, U.S. diplomats and trade officials, allied nation trading partners
bills.ticker_alert: 🔒 Senate bill pushes allies to match U.S. chip export restrictions on rivals

bills.title: Taiwan Relations Reinforcement Act
bills.plain_title: Strengthening U.S. Commitment to Taiwan's Defense and Relations
bills.deck: This bill reinforces U.S. support for Taiwan by updating arms sales, diplomatic ties, and security commitments.
bills.plain_summary: The Taiwan Relations Reinforcement Act updates and strengthens the legal framework governing how the United States engages with Taiwan, including arms sales, diplomatic interactions, and security cooperation. It aims to bolster Taiwan's ability to defend itself and signal a stronger U.S. commitment amid rising tensions with China. The bill was introduced in the U.S. Senate and has been referred to the Senate Foreign Relations Committee, where it awaits further review.

impacts.text: Stronger U.S.-Taiwan ties could escalate tensions with China, risking trade disruptions or military confrontation that affects American businesses and workers.
Reinforcing Taiwan's security helps protect a key global semiconductor hub, reducing risks to American supply chains and technology industries.
The bill updates a decades-old foreign policy framework, reopening debate over how explicitly the U.S. should commit to Taiwan's defense without a formal treaty.

bills.who_affected: Defense contractors, semiconductor and tech industry workers, U.S. military personnel, American exporters trading with China, foreign policy officials
bills.ticker_alert: 🌏 Senate bill seeks stronger U.S. support for Taiwan amid China tensions

bills.title: Make Billionaires Pay Their Fair Share Act
bills.plain_title: Minimum Tax on Billionaires' Wealth and Income
bills.deck: Requires billionaires to pay at least 25% in taxes on income and unrealized investment gains.
bills.plain_summary: This bill would require Americans worth over $1 billion to pay a minimum 25% tax rate on all income, including gains on investments they haven't yet sold — a concept called 'unrealized gains.' It was introduced by Democratic lawmakers who argue the wealthiest Americans often pay lower effective tax rates than middle-class workers due to legal loopholes. The bill has been referred to several House committees and has not yet received a vote.

impacts.text: Wealthy individuals could be forced to sell assets to pay taxes on paper gains they haven't actually received as cash, potentially disrupting businesses or investments.
Revenue raised could fund public programs like healthcare, education, or deficit reduction, potentially benefiting millions of lower- and middle-income Americans.
Taxing unrealized gains is legally and administratively untested at the federal level, and its constitutionality may be challenged in courts.

bills.who_affected: Billionaires and centimillionaires, small business owners with high valuations, startup founders, institutional investors, taxpayers who may benefit from new revenue
bills.ticker_alert: 💰 New bill would impose 25% minimum tax on billionaires' wealth gains

bills.title: Plastic Pellet Free Waters Act
bills.plain_title: Ban Plastic Pellet Spills Into U.S. Waterways
bills.deck: This bill requires industries to prevent tiny plastic pellets from spilling into America's waters.
bills.plain_summary: The Plastic Pellet Free Waters Act would require companies that manufacture, transport, or use plastic pellets — tiny pre-production plastic beads called 'nurdles' — to obtain permits and take steps to prevent them from escaping into waterways. The bill was introduced in the U.S. Senate and has been referred to the Committee on Environment and Public Works, which means it has not yet been debated or voted on. It aims to address a largely invisible source of plastic pollution that harms marine life and drinking water.

impacts.text: Plastics manufacturers, shippers, and processors would face new permit requirements and compliance costs, potentially raising production expenses passed on to consumers.
Coastal and freshwater communities would see reduced plastic pellet pollution, protecting drinking water sources, fisheries, and recreational beaches.
Enforcement would fall under the Clean Water Act framework, raising questions about how EPA would monitor millions of industrial sites and shipping routes for pellet spills.

bills.who_affected: Plastic manufacturers, chemical and petrochemical workers, shipping and logistics companies, coastal and fishing communities, municipal water utilities, environmentalists, beachgoers
bills.ticker_alert: 🌊 Senate bill targets invisible plastic pellet pollution threatening U.S. waterways

bills.title: To require each local educational agency and institution of higher education that receives Federal financial assistance to treat discrimination motivated by antisemitism as vigorously as such agency or institution treats other forms of discrimination prohibited by title VI of the Civil Rights Act of 1964, and for other purposes.
bills.plain_title: Anti-Semitism Discrimination Protection in Schools Act
bills.deck: Requires schools receiving federal funds to treat antisemitism the same as other forms of illegal discrimination.
bills.plain_summary: This bill would require public schools and colleges that receive federal funding to investigate and respond to antisemitic discrimination with the same seriousness as other forms of discrimination already protected under the Civil Rights Act of 1964. It was referred to the House Committee on Education and Workforce and the Committee on the Judiciary, where it awaits further consideration. The bill aims to close a perceived gap in how Jewish students are protected from discrimination on campuses.

impacts.text: Critics warn the bill could restrict free speech and political debate about Israel on college campuses by broadly defining antisemitism.
Jewish students experiencing harassment or discrimination at school would gain clearer, stronger federal protections and enforcement mechanisms.
The bill's effectiveness depends heavily on which definition of antisemitism is adopted, a contested question with significant policy implications.

bills.who_affected: Jewish students, university administrators, K-12 school officials, civil rights officers, pro-Palestinian student groups, free speech advocates
bills.ticker_alert: 🛡️ Bill would require schools to treat antisemitism like other civil rights violations

bills.title: SEED Act
bills.plain_title: Small Business Export and Economic Development Act
bills.deck: This bill creates programs to help small businesses grow by selling products and services in foreign markets.
bills.plain_summary: The SEED Act (Small Business Export and Economic Development Act) aims to expand support for small businesses looking to enter or grow in international markets by strengthening federal export assistance programs. It would direct resources toward helping small firms navigate foreign trade opportunities, reduce barriers to exporting, and connect businesses with counseling and financing tools. The bill was placed on the Union Calendar, meaning it has cleared committee and is eligible for a full House vote.

impacts.text: May primarily benefit businesses with existing capacity to export, leaving the smallest or most rural firms behind despite federal spending
Small business owners could gain easier access to export counseling, financing, and market research, potentially creating jobs and increasing revenues
The bill expands federal agency roles in trade promotion, raising questions about government efficiency versus private-sector solutions

bills.who_affected: Small business owners, manufacturing workers, exporters, rural entrepreneurs, federal trade agency employees, trade counselors
bills.ticker_alert: 📦 SEED Act would boost federal export help for small businesses

bills.title: VA Zero Suicide Demonstration Project Act of 2025
bills.plain_title: VA Zero Suicide Program for Veterans
bills.deck: Creates a VA pilot program aimed at eliminating veteran suicides through coordinated mental health care.
bills.plain_summary: This bill would direct the Department of Veterans Affairs to launch a demonstration project using the 'Zero Suicide' framework, a research-backed approach that trains healthcare staff and builds coordinated systems to prevent suicides among patients. The goal is to test whether this model can reduce or eliminate suicides among veterans receiving VA care. The bill was introduced in the Senate and has been referred to the Committee on Veterans' Affairs, where it awaits further action.

impacts.text: The program requires new federal spending and staff training resources, and critics may question whether a 'zero suicide' goal is realistically achievable or measurable.
Veterans receiving VA mental health care could benefit from more coordinated, evidence-based suicide prevention that has shown results in civilian hospital systems.
The bill is a limited demonstration project, meaning results may take years to evaluate and there is no guarantee the program would be expanded VA-wide.

bills.who_affected: Veterans receiving VA mental health care, VA healthcare staff and clinicians, military families, suicide prevention advocates
bills.ticker_alert: 🛡️ Senate bill would launch VA pilot program to eliminate veteran suicides

bills.title: Main Street Act
bills.plain_title: Tax Relief for Small Businesses on Main Street
bills.deck: This bill would reduce the corporate tax rate for small and mid-sized American businesses.
bills.plain_summary: The Main Street Act (HR 6955) proposes lowering the corporate income tax rate specifically for smaller and mid-sized businesses, aiming to give them a competitive advantage over large corporations. The bill has been placed on the Union Calendar, meaning it has passed committee review and is eligible for a full House vote. It reflects ongoing congressional debate about how tax policy can support locally-owned businesses that form the backbone of many American communities.

impacts.text: Reduced corporate tax revenue could shrink federal funds available for public services like infrastructure, education, and healthcare programs.
Small and mid-sized business owners could keep more profits, potentially allowing them to hire more workers or raise employee wages.
The definition of which businesses qualify as 'small' or 'mid-sized' is a key contested detail that will determine how broadly the tax cut applies.

bills.who_affected: Small business owners, mid-sized company employees, local community members, federal taxpayers, large corporations competing with smaller rivals
bills.ticker_alert: 💼 Main Street Act would cut taxes for small and mid-sized businesses

bills.title: Systemic Risk Authority Transparency Act
bills.plain_title: Make Financial Stability Oversight More Transparent
bills.deck: Requires the Financial Stability Oversight Council to publicly explain its decisions about risky financial firms.
bills.plain_summary: This bill would force the Financial Stability Oversight Council (FSOC) — a federal group that monitors risks to the U.S. economy — to be more open about how and why it labels certain financial companies as potential threats to the broader economy. It requires more detailed public reporting and explanation of its decision-making process. The bill passed the House and is now awaiting action in the Senate Banking Committee.

impacts.text: Greater disclosure requirements could slow FSOC's ability to respond quickly to emerging financial threats
Americans and investors gain better insight into which financial firms are considered risky and why, improving public accountability
Financial firms labeled as systemically risky face stricter federal oversight, and this bill changes how that labeling process is explained — but not necessarily who gets labeled

bills.who_affected: Large bank customers, investors, insurance company policyholders, pension fund beneficiaries, financial industry workers, taxpayers
bills.ticker_alert: 📋 Bill would force financial watchdog to publicly explain risk decisions

bills.title: Lung Cancer Screening Expansion Act of 2025
bills.plain_title: Expanding Medicare Coverage for Lung Cancer Screenings
bills.deck: This bill would expand Medicare coverage for lung cancer CT screenings to more Americans at risk.
bills.plain_summary: The Lung Cancer Screening Expansion Act of 2025 (HR 6314) would broaden Medicare eligibility for low-dose CT lung cancer screenings, potentially lowering the age or smoking history requirements to qualify for covered screenings. The bill aims to catch lung cancer earlier in more patients, when it is most treatable. It has been referred to the House Subcommittee on Health, meaning it is in early stages and has not yet received a full committee vote.

impacts.text: Expanding coverage could increase Medicare costs, potentially raising premiums or adding to federal spending over time.
More Americans at risk for lung cancer would receive free early detection screenings, which can significantly improve survival rates.
Debate exists over the optimal age and risk criteria for screening, with some experts cautioning that broader screening may lead to false positives and unnecessary procedures.

bills.who_affected: Current and former smokers, Medicare beneficiaries, older Americans ages 50–80, lung cancer patients and survivors, oncologists and radiologists
bills.ticker_alert: 🫁 Bill would expand free Medicare lung cancer screenings to more Americans

bills.title: Governing for the People Act
bills.plain_title: Government Transparency and Ethics Reform Act
bills.deck: Requires federal officials to disclose financial conflicts and strengthens ethics rules for government employees.
bills.plain_summary: The Governing for the People Act aims to increase transparency and reduce corruption in the federal government by strengthening financial disclosure requirements and ethics rules for government officials and employees. The bill has been referred to the Subcommittee on Health, suggesting it may include provisions related to healthcare industry conflicts of interest. It is currently in an early stage of the legislative process and has not yet been voted on.

impacts.text: Some critics argue stricter disclosure rules could deter qualified private-sector professionals from accepting government positions.
Taxpayers could benefit from reduced government corruption and more accountable use of public funds by officials with disclosed conflicts.
Enforcement and oversight mechanisms would need to be established, raising questions about which agency would have authority and adequate resources.

bills.who_affected: Federal government employees, political appointees, healthcare industry lobbyists, taxpayers, patients relying on federal health programs
bills.ticker_alert: 🏛️ New ethics bill targets conflicts of interest among federal officials and staff

bills.title: Promoting a Safe Internet for Minors Act
bills.plain_title: Online Safety Rules for Children and Teens
bills.deck: This bill requires the FTC to study and report on online safety tools protecting minors from harmful internet content.
bills.plain_summary: The Promoting a Safe Internet for Minors Act directs the Federal Trade Commission (FTC) to examine existing tools, technologies, and methods used to protect children and teenagers from harmful or inappropriate content online. The bill calls for a comprehensive report on what is working and what gaps remain in keeping minors safe on the internet. It has been approved by a congressional subcommittee and is now awaiting a vote by the full committee before it can advance further.

impacts.text: Critics argue the bill only orders a study without mandating real protections, potentially delaying meaningful action on child online safety.
Families and parents could benefit from clearer, government-backed guidance on tools and practices that effectively protect children online.
The FTC report could inform future legislation but does not itself impose new rules on tech companies or social media platforms.

bills.who_affected: Children and teenagers, parents, schools, tech companies, social media platforms, internet service providers
bills.ticker_alert: 🔒 Congress moves to study online safety tools protecting children from harmful content

bills.title: FHA Small-Dollar Mortgages Act
bills.plain_title: Easier FHA Loans for Affordable Home Purchases
bills.deck: This bill would make it easier to get government-backed mortgages for lower-priced homes under $150,000.
bills.plain_summary: The FHA Small-Dollar Mortgages Act would require the Federal Housing Administration to create a special program making it easier and cheaper to obtain FHA-backed loans for homes priced under $150,000. Because lenders often avoid small loans since fees and profits are lower, this bill aims to incentivize banks to offer these mortgages. The bill has been referred to the House Committee on Financial Services and has not yet been voted on.

impacts.text: Lenders may still avoid small-dollar loans even with incentives, limiting real-world effectiveness of the program
First-time and low-income buyers in rural or lower-cost areas could more easily access affordable homeownership financing
The FHA program's financial reserves could face added risk depending on how borrower eligibility standards are set

bills.who_affected: First-time homebuyers, low-income buyers, rural residents, buyers in lower-cost housing markets, small community lenders, FHA-approved mortgage servicers
bills.ticker_alert: 🏡 Bill would expand FHA loans for homes under $150,000

bills.title: 21st Century ROAD to Housing Act
bills.plain_title: Rural and Remote Housing Modernization Act
bills.deck: Updates federal housing loan programs to expand affordable home financing in rural and underserved communities.
bills.plain_summary: The 21st Century ROAD to Housing Act modernizes USDA rural housing loan programs by updating income limits, expanding eligibility, and improving access to affordable mortgages for people living in rural and remote areas. The bill has passed the House and received Senate action, putting it close to potential enactment. It aims to address housing shortages and affordability challenges in communities that are often overlooked by conventional mortgage lenders.

impacts.text: Expanded loan guarantees could increase federal financial exposure if rural borrowers default at higher rates
Low- and moderate-income rural families gain easier access to affordable home loans that private lenders often deny them
Updated income eligibility thresholds may bring in more borrowers but also reshape who qualifies for existing assistance

bills.who_affected: Rural homebuyers, low- and moderate-income families in rural areas, USDA loan applicants, rural communities with housing shortages, remote and tribal area residents
bills.ticker_alert: 🏠 Rural housing bill advances — more Americans may qualify for USDA home loans

bills.title: SCOPE Act of 2026
bills.plain_title: Expanding Outdoor Recreation Access on Public Lands
bills.deck: This bill aims to increase Americans' access to public lands for outdoor recreation and related activities.
bills.plain_summary: The SCOPE Act of 2026 (S 3928) would expand opportunities for outdoor recreation on federally managed public lands, likely by streamlining permits, increasing access points, or directing agencies to prioritize recreational use. The bill was introduced in the Senate and referred to the Committee on Environment and Public Works, where it awaits further review before any vote can occur. Because the full bill text is not publicly detailed here, specifics on sponsor and exact provisions are based on the bill's title and committee assignment.

impacts.text: Expanded recreational access could increase wear on sensitive ecosystems, potentially harming wildlife habitats and natural landscapes on public lands.
Millions of hikers, campers, hunters, and outdoor enthusiasts could gain easier, more affordable access to federal public lands for recreation.
The bill may shift how federal agencies balance conservation priorities against recreational use, a debate with passionate advocates on both sides.

bills.who_affected: Outdoor recreationists, hunters, anglers, hikers, conservation groups, rural tourism businesses, federal land management agencies, indigenous communities near public lands
bills.ticker_alert: 🏕️ Senate bill seeks broader public land access for millions of outdoor Americans

bills.title: Taxpayer Due Process Enhancement Act
bills.plain_title: IRS Audit Rights and Taxpayer Protections Bill
bills.deck: This bill strengthens legal protections for Americans during IRS audits and tax disputes.
bills.plain_summary: The Taxpayer Due Process Enhancement Act would add new procedural safeguards for Americans being audited or challenged by the IRS, giving taxpayers more rights to contest tax agency actions before facing penalties or collection. It has been placed on the Union Calendar, meaning the House has scheduled it for potential floor consideration. The bill aims to rebalance the power dynamic between everyday taxpayers and the IRS.

impacts.text: Critics warn stronger procedural hurdles for the IRS could slow tax enforcement and allow some wealthy tax cheats to delay accountability.
Ordinary Americans facing IRS audits would gain clearer rights to challenge agency decisions before penalties are imposed or wages garnished.
The bill could increase administrative costs for the IRS and extend the timeline for resolving tax disputes, which may benefit or harm taxpayers depending on their situation.

bills.who_affected: Individual taxpayers under IRS audit, small business owners, self-employed workers, low-income Americans using tax credits, tax attorneys and accountants
bills.ticker_alert: ⚖️ House bill would expand taxpayer rights during IRS audits and disputes

bills.title: E-Access Act
bills.plain_title: Expanded Internet Access on Federal Public Lands
bills.deck: This bill would require improved broadband and internet access infrastructure across national parks and federal lands.
bills.plain_summary: The E-Access Act directs the federal government to expand electronic and internet access on public lands managed by agencies like the National Park Service and Bureau of Land Management. It aims to improve connectivity for visitors, workers, and surrounding rural communities. The bill was referred to the Senate Committee on Energy and Natural Resources, where it awaits further review before any potential vote.

impacts.text: New infrastructure on federal lands could disturb wildlife habitats, scenic viewsheds, and wilderness character that many visitors seek to escape from connectivity.
Visitors and rural residents near federal lands gain better emergency communication access, improving safety during accidents or natural disasters in remote areas.
Federal agencies would need to balance expanding broadband access with existing land-use rules, potentially requiring new permits and interagency coordination.

bills.who_affected: National park visitors, rural residents near federal lands, outdoor recreation businesses, federal land management workers, wilderness and conservation advocates
bills.ticker_alert: 📶 Senate bill seeks expanded internet access across U.S. public lands

bills.title: Clean Slate through Repayment Act of 2026
bills.plain_title: Student Loan Repayment Clears Criminal Records
bills.deck: Borrowers who fully repay federal student loans could have related criminal records automatically expunged.
bills.plain_summary: This bill would allow people who have fully repaid their federal student loans to have certain criminal convictions related to those loans wiped from their records. It was introduced in the House of Representatives and has been sent to the House Committee on Education and Workforce for review, where most bills are first examined before any floor vote. The bill aims to give a 'clean slate' to borrowers who have satisfied their financial obligations to the government.

impacts.text: Critics argue wiping criminal records for loan repayment could undermine accountability for fraud or misrepresentation in the loan process.
Former borrowers with repaid loans could have criminal records cleared, improving job prospects, housing access, and civil rights restoration.
The scope of which criminal offenses qualify for expungement is contested — broader definitions help more people but raise more legal concerns.

bills.who_affected: Federal student loan borrowers with criminal records, formerly incarcerated individuals with student debt, employers doing background checks, higher education institutions
bills.ticker_alert: 🧹 Bill would erase criminal records for Americans who fully repay student loans

bills.title: DPA Emerging Technology Act of 2026
bills.plain_title: Defense Production Act Update for Emerging Technologies
bills.deck: Expands Cold War-era emergency manufacturing law to cover modern technologies like AI and semiconductors.
