bills.plain_summary: This bill updates the Defense Production Act — a law originally passed in 1950 to mobilize U.S. industry during national emergencies — to explicitly include emerging technologies such as artificial intelligence, semiconductors, and quantum computing. It would give the federal government stronger authority to direct private companies to prioritize production of these technologies for national security purposes. The bill has been referred to the House Committee on Financial Services and has not yet been voted on.

impacts.text: Private tech and manufacturing companies could be compelled by the government to shift production priorities, potentially disrupting normal business operations and contracts.
Strengthens U.S. ability to rapidly scale up critical technology production during a national security crisis, reducing dependence on foreign suppliers like China.
Grants the executive branch expanded authority over the private technology sector, raising ongoing debates about the proper scope of government intervention in markets.

bills.who_affected: Semiconductor manufacturers, AI companies, tech investors, defense contractors, quantum computing researchers, U.S. military and national security agencies
bills.ticker_alert: ⚙️ Bill would give feds new power over AI and chip production for national security

bills.title: CLEAR (Committee Leadership and Enhanced Accountability for Resilience) Defense Production Act of 2025
bills.plain_title: Congressional Oversight Reform for Defense Production Act Powers
bills.deck: This bill would require Congress to approve presidential use of emergency industrial production powers.
bills.plain_summary: This bill would increase congressional oversight over the Defense Production Act, a law that gives the president broad powers to direct American industry during national emergencies. It would require committee approval or congressional authorization before certain presidential actions take effect. The bill has been referred to the House Committee on Financial Services and has not yet been voted on.

impacts.text: Could slow emergency government responses to supply chain crises or national security threats by adding congressional approval steps
Prevents presidents from unilaterally directing private industry without legislative accountability, protecting businesses from unchecked executive mandates
Shifts the balance of power between Congress and the White House on industrial policy, a change whose effects depend heavily on the political situation at any given time

bills.who_affected: U.S. manufacturers and defense contractors, businesses subject to federal production orders, members of Congress, federal agency officials, supply chain workers in critical industries
bills.ticker_alert: ⚖️ New bill would require Congress to approve presidential emergency industrial powers

bills.title: Strengthening and Improving Mobilization Act of 2026
bills.plain_title: Military Mobilization and Readiness Improvement Act
bills.deck: This bill aims to strengthen the U.S. military's ability to quickly mobilize personnel and resources during national emergencies.
bills.plain_summary: The Strengthening and Improving Mobilization Act of 2026 (HR 8071) seeks to improve how the U.S. military and government agencies prepare for and respond to large-scale emergencies requiring rapid troop or resource deployment. The bill was referred to the House Committee on Financial Services, suggesting it may also address funding mechanisms or financial infrastructure tied to mobilization efforts. It is currently in the early committee review stage and has not yet been voted on.

impacts.text: Could expand executive authority to mobilize civilians or redirect private-sector resources during declared emergencies with limited congressional oversight.
Faster and better-funded military mobilization could improve national security and reduce response time during major crises or conflicts.
The bill's referral to the Financial Services Committee suggests financial or banking system changes tied to mobilization, the scope of which remains unclear.

bills.who_affected: Active duty military members, military reservists, National Guard members, defense contractors, taxpayers funding defense spending, financial institutions tied to defense logistics
bills.ticker_alert: 🪖 Bill proposes faster U.S. military mobilization with potential new funding mechanisms

bills.title: USDA Loan Modernization Act
bills.plain_title: Update USDA Farm Loan Rules and Processes
bills.deck: This bill modernizes how the USDA manages and delivers loans to farmers and rural borrowers.
bills.plain_summary: The USDA Loan Modernization Act proposes updates to how the U.S. Department of Agriculture handles its farm loan programs, likely streamlining processes, updating eligibility rules, or improving digital access for applicants. The bill has been referred to the Senate Committee on Agriculture, Nutrition, and Forestry, meaning it is in early stages and has not yet been voted on. It aims to make USDA lending more efficient and accessible for farmers and rural communities.

impacts.text: Modernized loan criteria or processes could unintentionally disadvantage small or beginning farmers unfamiliar with new digital or administrative requirements.
Streamlined loan processes could reduce wait times and paperwork burdens for farmers seeking USDA financing for land, equipment, or operations.
Changes to loan program rules may shift which borrowers qualify, potentially benefiting some applicants while altering terms for others.

bills.who_affected: Farmers, ranchers, beginning farmers, rural small business owners, agricultural lenders, rural communities
bills.ticker_alert: 🌾 Senate bill proposes modernizing USDA farm loans for rural borrowers

bills.title: DPA Transparency Act of 2026
bills.plain_title: Data Privacy Agreement Disclosure Requirements Act
bills.deck: Requires companies to publicly disclose how they use data-sharing agreements with third parties.
bills.plain_summary: The DPA Transparency Act of 2026 (HR 8030) would require financial companies to publicly report details about their data processing agreements, including how consumer data is shared with outside parties. The bill aims to give Americans more visibility into how their personal financial information is being used and sold. It has been referred to the House Committee on Financial Services, meaning it is in early stages and has not yet been voted on.

impacts.text: Financial companies may face increased compliance costs, potentially passed on to consumers as higher fees
Consumers could gain clearer insight into how their personal financial data is shared or sold to third parties
The scope and enforcement mechanisms of required disclosures remain subject to debate and committee review

bills.who_affected: Bank customers, credit union members, fintech app users, financial services companies, data brokers, consumer privacy advocates
bills.ticker_alert: 📋 New bill would force financial firms to disclose data-sharing deals publicly

bills.title: Sanctuary Penalty and Public Protection Act of 2025
bills.plain_title: Penalize Cities That Limit Immigration Enforcement Cooperation
bills.deck: This bill would cut federal funding to cities and states that limit cooperation with federal immigration authorities.
bills.plain_summary: This bill would penalize so-called 'sanctuary' jurisdictions — cities, counties, or states that restrict their local police from fully cooperating with federal immigration enforcement — by withholding federal grant money from them. It was introduced in the House of Representatives and has been referred to two committees for review. The bill has not yet been voted on and is in early legislative stages.

impacts.text: Cities that limit immigration cooperation could lose federal funding for local services like public safety, housing, and infrastructure, potentially harming residents.
Supporters say it encourages local law enforcement to help remove undocumented immigrants with criminal records, potentially improving public safety.
The definition of a 'sanctuary' jurisdiction is contested and not uniformly established in law, making enforcement of penalties legally complex.

bills.who_affected: Undocumented immigrants, residents of sanctuary cities, local law enforcement agencies, municipal governments, taxpayers in affected jurisdictions
bills.ticker_alert: 🚨 Bill would cut federal funds to cities limiting immigration enforcement cooperation

bills.title: CHOICE for Veterans Act of 2025
bills.plain_title: Veterans Can Use Private Health Care Options Act
bills.deck: Lets eligible veterans use federal funds to buy private health insurance instead of VA care.
bills.plain_summary: The CHOICE for Veterans Act of 2025 would allow certain eligible veterans to receive a voucher or credit to purchase private health insurance as an alternative to using the Department of Veterans Affairs healthcare system. It gives veterans more flexibility in choosing their own doctors and health plans outside the VA network. The bill passed out of committee by a narrow 12-11 vote, indicating significant debate, and now moves to the full House for consideration.

impacts.text: Critics warn diverting funds to private insurance could reduce money and patients available to sustain VA hospitals, potentially weakening care for veterans who rely on specialized VA services like PTSD and prosthetics treatment.
Veterans in rural areas or those unhappy with VA wait times could access local private doctors faster, giving them more control over their own healthcare choices.
The shift toward private insurance could reduce VA patient volume, forcing long-term decisions about the size and mission of the VA healthcare system.

bills.who_affected: Military veterans, VA healthcare employees, rural veterans lacking nearby VA facilities, veterans with service-connected disabilities, private health insurers
bills.ticker_alert: 🏥 Veterans bill would allow private insurance alternative to VA healthcare

bills.title: Safe Routes Act of 2025
bills.plain_title: Funding Bill for Safer Walking and Biking Routes
bills.deck: This bill would expand federal funding for safe walking and biking infrastructure near schools and communities.
bills.plain_summary: The Safe Routes Act of 2025 (HR 2166) would increase federal support for building and improving sidewalks, bike lanes, and pedestrian crossings, particularly near schools, to make it safer for children and adults to walk or bike. The bill has been referred to the House Subcommittee on Highways and Transit, meaning it is in early review stages and has not yet been voted on. The sponsor's full details are available on Congress.gov, but the bill builds on existing Safe Routes to School program frameworks.

impacts.text: Federal spending on this program could increase the national deficit or redirect highway funds from road repair and vehicle infrastructure projects.
Children and pedestrians near schools could benefit from safer crossings and paths, potentially reducing traffic injuries and fatalities.
Local governments would gain more federal dollars but may need to match funds and manage new infrastructure long-term, shifting some cost burden locally.

bills.who_affected: K-12 students, parents, cyclists, pedestrians, local transportation planners, school districts, low-income communities with limited safe walking infrastructure
bills.ticker_alert: 🚶 Bill proposes more federal funding for safe school walking and biking routes

bills.title: Mental Health Research Accelerator Act of 2025
bills.plain_title: Boost Funding for Mental Health Research
bills.deck: This bill would create new tax incentives to accelerate private investment in mental health research.
bills.plain_summary: The Mental Health Research Accelerator Act of 2025 (HR 2085) would establish tax credits or incentives to encourage companies and investors to fund mental health research and development. The goal is to speed up the discovery of new treatments for conditions like depression, anxiety, and schizophrenia. The bill has been referred to the House Committee on Ways and Means, meaning it is in the early stages and has not yet been voted on.

impacts.text: Tax incentives for private companies could reduce federal revenue, potentially shifting research priorities toward profitable treatments rather than rare or underserved mental health conditions.
Increased private investment in mental health research could lead to faster development of new treatments, benefiting the roughly 1 in 5 Americans living with a mental illness.

The bill shifts some mental health research funding responsibility from the government to the private sector, which supporters see as efficient and critics view as prioritizing profit over public need.

bills.who_affected: People living with mental illness, mental health researchers, pharmaceutical and biotech companies, taxpayers, psychiatrists and therapists
bills.ticker_alert: 🧠 Bill proposes tax incentives to fast-track new mental health treatments

bills.title: Roadless Area Conservation Act of 2025
bills.plain_title: Permanent Protection for Undeveloped National Forest Lands
bills.deck: This bill permanently bans road-building and logging in 58 million acres of national forest wilderness.
bills.plain_summary: The Roadless Area Conservation Act of 2025 would make permanent the 2001 Roadless Area Conservation Rule, which prohibits road construction and most logging in about 58 million acres of undeveloped national forest land. The bill was referred to the House Agriculture and Natural Resources Committees, where it awaits further action. It aims to lock in protections that have faced legal and administrative challenges over the past two decades by giving them the force of federal law.

impacts.text: Timber, mining, and energy industries would be permanently blocked from accessing resources in 58 million acres, potentially reducing jobs and economic activity in rural forest communities.

Hikers, hunters, anglers, and outdoor recreation enthusiasts would gain permanent protection for pristine wilderness areas, clean water sources, and wildlife habitat currently threatened by development.
States and tribal governments would lose flexibility to negotiate local exceptions to roadless rules, which some see as federal overreach and others view as necessary consistency.

bills.who_affected: Timber and logging workers, mining and energy companies, rural Western communities, hunters and anglers, hikers and backpackers, conservation groups, Indigenous tribes near national forests, state governments in the West
bills.ticker_alert: 🌲 Bill would permanently ban roads and logging on 58M forest acres

bills.title: Cosmetic Hazardous Ingredient Right to Know Act of 2025
bills.plain_title: Hazardous Chemicals Warning Labels for Cosmetics
bills.deck: This bill would require cosmetic products to display warnings when they contain ingredients linked to cancer or other serious health risks.
bills.plain_summary: This bill would require cosmetic companies to place warning labels on products containing ingredients identified as hazardous, carcinogenic, or toxic. Consumers would be notified directly on product packaging if a cosmetic contains potentially dangerous chemicals. The bill was introduced in the House and has been referred to the House Committee on Energy and Commerce, where it awaits further review.

impacts.text: Cosmetic companies would face increased compliance costs for reformulating products or redesigning packaging, potentially raising prices for consumers.
Consumers would have clearer information about potentially harmful chemicals in everyday products like makeup, shampoo, and lotion before purchasing.
The definition of what counts as a 'hazardous ingredient' is contested — industry groups and health advocates often disagree on which chemicals meet that threshold.

bills.who_affected: Cosmetic consumers, women who use makeup daily, people with sensitive health conditions, cosmetic manufacturers, small beauty brands, retail pharmacies
bills.ticker_alert: ⚠️ New bill would put hazard warnings on cosmetics with risky chemicals

bills.title: To provide for a limitation on the transfer of defense articles and defense services to Israel.
bills.plain_title: Bill to Restrict U.S. Weapons Sales to Israel
bills.deck: This bill would block the U.S. government from sending weapons or military services to Israel.
bills.plain_summary: HR 3565 would prohibit the United States from transferring defense equipment or military services to Israel. The bill has been referred to the House Committee on Foreign Affairs, which is a very early stage — most bills never advance beyond this point. It reflects a growing debate in Congress over U.S. military aid to Israel amid ongoing conflict in the region.

impacts.text: Could weaken a long-standing U.S. military alliance with Israel, potentially destabilizing a key partner in the Middle East and affecting American strategic interests in the region.
Could reduce U.S. involvement in overseas conflicts and potentially limit American taxpayer dollars spent on foreign military assistance to Israel.
Reignites a contested national debate about the limits of U.S. foreign military aid and Congress's role in overseeing executive branch arms transfers.

bills.who_affected: U.S. defense contractors, American taxpayers, Jewish-American communities, Arab-American communities, U.S. foreign policy professionals, military veterans, human rights advocates
bills.ticker_alert: ⚠️ House bill introduced to ban all U.S. weapons transfers to Israel

bills.title: Supreme Court Ethics, Recusal, and Transparency Act of 2025
bills.plain_title: Supreme Court Ethics and Transparency Rules Bill
bills.deck: This bill would require Supreme Court justices to follow stricter ethics rules and disclose conflicts of interest.
bills.plain_summary: This bill would force Supreme Court justices to follow a binding code of ethics, disclose gifts and travel paid by outside parties, and recuse themselves from cases where they have conflicts of interest. It was introduced in the House and has been referred to the House Judiciary Committee, meaning it is in the early stages of the legislative process. The bill responds to controversies over undisclosed gifts and trips received by sitting justices.

impacts.text: Critics argue the bill could allow Congress to unconstitutionally interfere with the independence of a co-equal branch of government.
Americans would gain the ability to see whether justices have financial ties to parties in cases that could shape national law.
The bill raises an unresolved constitutional debate over whether Congress has the authority to impose ethics rules on the Supreme Court.

bills.who_affected: Supreme Court justices, litigants before the Supreme Court, legal advocacy groups, taxpayers, all Americans affected by Supreme Court rulings
bills.ticker_alert: ⚖️ Bill would impose binding ethics rules on Supreme Court justices

bills.title: No Medicaid for Illegal Immigrants Act of 2025
bills.plain_title: Block Medicaid Coverage for Undocumented Immigrants
bills.deck: This bill would prohibit undocumented immigrants from receiving Medicaid benefits, even in emergency situations.
bills.plain_summary: The No Medicaid for Illegal Immigrants Act of 2025 would ban undocumented immigrants from enrolling in or receiving any Medicaid-funded health coverage, including emergency Medicaid that currently covers life-threatening situations. The bill was introduced in the House of Representatives and has been sent to the House Committee on Energy and Commerce for review. Under current law, undocumented immigrants are already barred from most Medicaid benefits but can receive emergency Medicaid; this bill would eliminate that emergency exception.

impacts.text: Undocumented immigrants could be denied emergency medical care coverage, potentially leaving hospitals to absorb unpaid costs or patients to go untreated in life-threatening situations.
Supporters argue the bill could reduce federal and state Medicaid spending by ending taxpayer-funded health coverage for people who are not legal residents.
The actual federal savings are disputed — emergency Medicaid is a relatively small share of total Medicaid spending, and hospitals may shift uncompensated care costs elsewhere.

bills.who_affected: Undocumented immigrants, hospital emergency departments, low-income legal residents competing for Medicaid resources, state Medicaid administrators, taxpayers
bills.ticker_alert: 🚨 Bill would end all Medicaid coverage, including emergency care, for undocumented immigrants

bills.title: DOC Access Act of 2025
bills.plain_title: Expanding Public Access to Commerce Department Data
bills.deck: This bill would require the Department of Commerce to make more of its data and research freely available to the public.
bills.plain_summary: The DOC Access Act of 2025 (HR 1521) would increase public access to data, research, and information held by the Department of Commerce, which oversees agencies like NOAA, the Census Bureau, and the Patent Office. The bill aims to make government-funded data more transparent and usable by everyday Americans, businesses, and researchers. It has been referred to the House Committee on Energy and Commerce, meaning it is in the early stages of the legislative process.

impacts.text: Some agencies may face increased costs and workload to prepare, format, and publish additional data sets for public release.
Researchers, small businesses, and citizens could gain free access to valuable government-funded data currently behind paywalls or hard to obtain.
The bill's actual effect depends heavily on implementation rules, which agencies would have significant discretion in writing.

bills.who_affected: Academic researchers, small business owners, journalists, data scientists, technology companies, weather forecasters using NOAA data, entrepreneurs using Census data
bills.ticker_alert: 📂 New bill would open Commerce Department data to the public

bills.title: To amend the Public Health Service Act to update quality standards for mammography facilities for the use of AI systems, and for other purposes.
bills.plain_title: AI Standards for Mammogram Screening Facilities
bills.deck: This bill sets federal quality rules for how AI tools are used in mammography breast cancer screenings.
bills.plain_summary: HR 8526 would update the federal law governing mammography facilities to include new quality and safety standards specifically for artificial intelligence systems used in reading mammograms. These standards would ensure AI tools used in breast cancer screening meet consistent, reliable benchmarks before being used on patients. The bill has been referred to the House Committee on Energy and Commerce, meaning it is in an early stage and has not yet been voted on.

impacts.text: Smaller or rural mammography clinics may face higher compliance costs to meet new AI certification requirements, potentially reducing access in underserved areas.
Women undergoing mammograms could benefit from safer, more accurate AI-assisted screenings with federally verified quality standards reducing misdiagnosis risk.
It remains debated whether federal regulation or industry self-regulation is the better approach to setting AI standards in medical imaging.

bills.who_affected: Women seeking breast cancer screenings, mammography facility operators, radiologists, AI medical software developers, rural and underserved community patients
bills.ticker_alert: 🩺 Bill would set federal AI quality rules for breast cancer mammogram screenings

bills.title: Stopping Border Surges Act
bills.plain_title: Bill to Restrict Asylum and Limit Border Crossings
bills.deck: This bill would tighten asylum rules and reduce the number of migrants allowed into the U.S. during surges.
bills.plain_summary: The Stopping Border Surges Act would make it harder for migrants to qualify for asylum by raising the legal standard required to pass initial screenings, and would limit the number of people allowed to request asylum during high-volume periods at the border. It would also restrict certain humanitarian protections and expand expedited removal of migrants. The bill has been referred to the House Judiciary and Foreign Affairs Committees, where it awaits further consideration.

impacts.text: Asylum seekers fleeing genuine persecution could be turned away more quickly under stricter screening standards, potentially increasing danger for vulnerable individuals.
Border communities and immigration enforcement agencies could see reduced strain from large migrant surges, potentially improving processing efficiency and public safety.
The bill reshapes longstanding asylum procedures established under U.S. and international law, a change whose real-world effects on migration flows remain debated by experts.

bills.who_affected: Asylum seekers and migrants, border communities, immigration attorneys, U.S. Customs and Border Protection agents, immigration judges, Central American and other refugee populations
bills.ticker_alert: 🚧 House bill proposes stricter asylum rules and caps during border surges

bills.title: the Military PFAS Transparency Act of 2025
bills.plain_title: Military Base PFAS Chemical Disclosure Requirement
bills.deck: Requires the military to publicly report PFAS chemical contamination levels at U.S. military installations.
bills.plain_summary: This bill would require the Department of Defense to publicly disclose data about PFAS 'forever chemical' contamination at military bases across the country. PFAS chemicals, linked to serious health problems, have been widely used in military firefighting foam and are found in soil and water near many bases. The bill has been referred to the House Committee on Armed Services and has not yet been voted on.

impacts.text: Defense officials worry public disclosure requirements could create legal liability and divert resources from cleanup efforts themselves
Veterans, military families, and nearby residents would gain access to contamination data to make informed health and safety decisions
Disclosure mandates could pressure faster cleanup timelines but do not themselves fund or require remediation of contaminated sites

bills.who_affected: Military veterans, active-duty service members and their families, civilians living near military bases, local water utilities near bases, DoD contractors
bills.ticker_alert: 🔍 Bill would force Pentagon to disclose PFAS contamination at military bases

bills.title: Humane Cosmetics Act of 2025
bills.plain_title: Ban on Animal Testing for Cosmetics
bills.deck: This bill would prohibit cosmetic companies from testing their products on animals in the United States.
bills.plain_summary: The Humane Cosmetics Act of 2025 (HR 1657) would make it illegal for companies to sell cosmetics in the U.S. that were tested on animals, either domestically or abroad. It would also ban conducting animal tests for cosmetics within the country. The bill has been referred to the House Committee on Energy and Commerce, where it awaits further action before any floor vote.

impacts.text: Some cosmetic companies, especially smaller ones, may face higher costs transitioning to alternative safety-testing methods to remain compliant.
Millions of animals — including rabbits, mice, and guinea pigs — would no longer be subjected to painful safety tests for cosmetics sold in the U.S.
The bill's import ban on animal-tested cosmetics could restrict consumer access to certain foreign brands unless those companies adopt alternative testing methods.

bills.who_affected: Cosmetic manufacturers, animal testing laboratories, animal welfare advocates, cosmetic importers and retailers, American consumers
bills.ticker_alert: 🐇 Bill would ban animal-tested cosmetics from U.S. sale

bills.title: Expanding Whistleblower Protections for Contractors Act of 2025
bills.plain_title: Stronger Whistleblower Protections for Federal Contractors
bills.deck: This bill strengthens legal protections for federal contractors who report government waste, fraud, or abuse.
bills.plain_summary: This bill expands whistleblower protections for employees of federal contractors, subcontractors, and grantees who report suspected fraud, waste, or abuse of taxpayer money. It aims to make it easier for these workers to come forward without fear of losing their jobs or facing retaliation. The bill has been placed on the Senate Legislative Calendar, meaning it is eligible for a full Senate vote.

impacts.text: Some businesses argue expanded protections could expose contractors to more frivolous or disruptive employee complaints, increasing legal and compliance costs.
Federal contractor employees gain stronger legal footing to report government fraud or waste, reducing fear of retaliation when exposing taxpayer abuse.
The bill's effectiveness depends heavily on how 'retaliation' is defined and enforced, leaving room for legal disputes over its scope.

bills.who_affected: Federal contractor employees, federal subcontractor employees, grant recipients, taxpayers, federal agencies, private defense and government service companies
bills.ticker_alert: 🔔 Senate bill would strengthen job protections for federal contractor whistleblowers

bills.title: SBA Fraud Enforcement Extension Act
bills.plain_title: Extending Time to Prosecute SBA Pandemic Loan Fraud
bills.deck: This bill gives federal prosecutors more time to pursue fraud cases involving Small Business Administration pandemic-era loans.
bills.plain_summary: This bill extends the statute of limitations — the legal deadline for filing criminal charges — for fraud related to SBA loan programs, particularly those created during the COVID-19 pandemic like PPP and EIDL loans. It gives federal investigators and prosecutors additional years to build and file cases against individuals who may have fraudulently obtained government-backed small business loans. The bill has been introduced in the Senate and is currently in the Committee on Small Business and Entrepreneurship, where a hearing has been held.

impacts.text: Businesses and individuals accused of loan fraud could face prosecution years longer than current law allows, extending legal uncertainty.
Taxpayers could recover billions in fraudulently obtained pandemic relief funds that would otherwise go unprosecuted due to expiring deadlines.
Expanding prosecution windows is a contested practice — supporters say fraud is complex and takes time to detect; critics worry it enables overreach.

bills.who_affected: Small business owners who received SBA pandemic loans, federal fraud investigators, taxpayers, defense attorneys, PPP and EIDL loan recipients
bills.ticker_alert: ⚖️ Senate bill would extend deadline to prosecute pandemic small business loan fraud

bills.title: PIPELINE Safety Act of 2025
bills.plain_title: Pipeline Safety Inspection and Oversight Improvement Act
bills.deck: Strengthens federal oversight and safety standards for the nation's oil and gas pipeline network.
bills.plain_summary: The PIPELINE Safety Act of 2025 updates federal rules governing how oil and natural gas pipelines are inspected, maintained, and regulated to reduce the risk of leaks, explosions, and environmental damage. It has been placed on the Senate Legislative Calendar, meaning it is awaiting a floor vote. The bill aims to modernize aging pipeline infrastructure rules and expand the authority of federal safety regulators.

impacts.text: Increased compliance costs for pipeline operators could be passed on to consumers through higher energy bills.
Stronger inspection requirements could reduce the risk of pipeline explosions, leaks, and environmental disasters near homes and communities.
Expands federal regulatory authority over pipelines, which supporters call necessary modernization and critics call government overreach into the energy sector.

bills.who_affected: Residents near oil and gas pipelines, pipeline companies and workers, energy consumers, environmental advocates, emergency first responders, rural and suburban landowners
bills.ticker_alert: 🔧 Senate bill targets stronger oil & gas pipeline safety inspections nationwide

bills.title: Designate CAIR as a Terrorist Organization Act
bills.plain_title: Bill to Label Muslim Civil Rights Group a Terrorist Organization
bills.deck: Would officially designate the Council on American-Islamic Relations (CAIR) as a foreign terrorist organization.
bills.plain_summary: This bill would require the U.S. government to formally designate CAIR, a Muslim civil rights and advocacy organization founded in 1994, as a terrorist organization. Such a designation would make it illegal to provide material support to CAIR and could freeze its assets. The bill was referred to the House Judiciary Committee, which is the standard first step before any debate or vote.

impacts.text: Critics warn it could suppress First Amendment rights and target a legal civil rights organization, chilling Muslim Americans' political participation and advocacy.
Supporters argue it would cut off resources to an organization they allege has undisclosed ties to foreign extremist groups, potentially improving national security.
CAIR has been investigated by the FBI in past decades but has never been formally charged with terrorist activity; courts have not upheld terrorist designations against it.

bills.who_affected: Muslim Americans, civil rights organizations, First Amendment advocates, national security agencies, nonprofit advocacy groups
bills.ticker_alert: ⚠️ Bill seeks to label major Muslim civil rights group a terrorist organization

bills.title: To amend title 18, United States Code, to include rioting in the definition of racketeering activity.
bills.plain_title: Adding Rioting to Federal Organized Crime Law
bills.deck: This bill would make rioting a federal racketeering crime, allowing prosecutors to treat rioters like organized criminals.
bills.plain_summary: This bill proposes to add rioting to the list of crimes covered under federal racketeering law (RICO), a powerful legal tool originally designed to prosecute organized crime like the mafia. This would allow federal prosecutors to charge people involved in riots with racketeering offenses, which carry heavier penalties and allow prosecution of entire groups. The bill has been referred to the House Judiciary Committee, meaning it has not yet been debated or voted on.

impacts.text: Civil liberties advocates warn it could criminalize protest organizers or bystanders near riots, chilling free speech and assembly rights guaranteed by the First Amendment.
Supporters argue it gives law enforcement stronger tools to prosecute and deter organized groups that coordinate property destruction or violence during riots.
The bill's reach depends entirely on how 'rioting' is legally defined, which varies by state and raises questions about federal vs. state jurisdiction over public disorder.

bills.who_affected: Protest organizers, political activists, civil rights groups, law enforcement agencies, federal prosecutors, journalists covering protests, community organizers
bills.ticker_alert: ⚖️ Bill would classify rioting as federal racketeering, risking protest rights

bills.title: Homeless Children and Youth Act of 2025
bills.plain_title: Expanded Housing Aid for Homeless Children and Youth
bills.deck: This bill expands the federal definition of homelessness so more children and youth can access housing assistance.
bills.plain_summary: The Homeless Children and Youth Act of 2025 would align the definition of 'homeless' used by HUD (housing programs) with the broader definition already used by the Department of Education, allowing more children and youth who are doubled-up in housing or in unstable living situations to qualify for federal housing help. This change could open access to housing vouchers and support services for families currently excluded due to narrow definitions. The bill has been referred to the House Committees on Financial Services and Education and Workforce, where it awaits further consideration.

impacts.text: Expanding eligibility could strain limited HUD housing voucher budgets, potentially lengthening waitlists for currently eligible homeless families.
Thousands of children living in overcrowded or unstable housing situations would gain access to federal housing assistance and support services they currently cannot receive.
The bill creates a unified federal definition of youth homelessness, which could change how federal agencies count and report homeless populations, affecting funding formulas.

bills.who_affected: Homeless children and youth, families doubled-up in housing, school McKinney-Vento liaisons, HUD housing program administrators, nonprofit housing service providers
bills.ticker_alert: 🏠 Bill would expand federal housing aid eligibility for homeless kids and youth

bills.title: Nutrition CARE Act of 2025
bills.plain_title: Improving Nutrition Support for Seriously Ill Patients
bills.deck: This bill would expand Medicare and Medicaid coverage for medical nutrition therapy for seriously ill Americans.
bills.plain_summary: The Nutrition CARE Act of 2025 (HR 2495) aims to improve access to medical nutrition therapy and specialized nutrition support, such as enteral and parenteral nutrition, for patients with serious illnesses under Medicare and Medicaid. It would require better coverage and reimbursement for nutrition-related care provided by registered dietitians and other qualified professionals. The bill has been referred to the House Committees on Energy and Commerce and Ways and Means, where it awaits further consideration.

impacts.text: Expanded coverage mandates could increase federal spending on Medicare and Medicaid, contributing to program costs or requiring budget offsets.
Seriously ill patients — including those with cancer, kidney disease, or malnutrition — could gain better access to medically necessary nutrition care currently poorly covered.
The bill's effectiveness depends heavily on how reimbursement rates are set and whether enough registered dietitians are available in underserved areas.

bills.who_affected: Medicare beneficiaries, Medicaid recipients, seriously ill patients, cancer patients, kidney disease patients, registered dietitians, hospitals, home health agencies
bills.ticker_alert: 🍽️ Bill proposes expanded Medicare nutrition therapy coverage for seriously ill patients

bills.title: To amend title 11 of the United States Code to address misuse of bankruptcy proceedings in cases of child sex abuse, and for other purposes.
bills.plain_title: Stopping Bankruptcy Abuse in Child Sex Abuse Cases
bills.deck: This bill prevents organizations from using bankruptcy to avoid paying victims of child sexual abuse.
bills.plain_summary: This bill changes federal bankruptcy law to stop institutions like churches, schools, or youth organizations from misusing the bankruptcy process to reduce or delay payments to survivors of child sexual abuse. It aims to close loopholes that allow large organizations to shield assets while abuse victims receive little compensation. The bill was referred to the House Judiciary Committee, where it awaits further review before any vote.

impacts.text: Organizations with legitimate financial hardship may face stricter bankruptcy restrictions that limit their ability to restructure debts fairly.
Child sexual abuse survivors may receive greater compensation and faster justice instead of waiting years through drawn-out bankruptcy proceedings.
Courts would gain new authority to scrutinize how organizations handle abuse claims in bankruptcy, changing how these complex cases are managed.

bills.who_affected: Child sexual abuse survivors, religious institutions, youth-serving nonprofits, bankruptcy attorneys, federal bankruptcy courts, abuse victim advocacy groups
bills.ticker_alert: ⚖️ New bill aims to block institutions from using bankruptcy to dodge abuse payouts

bills.title: Fertilizer Transparency Act of 2026
bills.plain_title: Fertilizer Price and Ingredient Disclosure Requirement
bills.deck: Requires fertilizer manufacturers to publicly disclose pricing and ingredient information to farmers and consumers.
bills.plain_summary: The Fertilizer Transparency Act of 2026 (HR 8104) would require fertilizer companies to disclose their pricing structures and product ingredients more openly. This aims to help farmers make better-informed purchasing decisions and understand what they are buying. The bill has been referred to the House Committee on Agriculture, meaning it is in early stages and has not yet been voted on.

impacts.text: Fertilizer companies may face compliance costs that could be passed on to farmers as higher prices.
Farmers gain clearer price and ingredient data, potentially helping them find cheaper or more effective alternatives.
Disclosure requirements could reshape competition in the fertilizer market in ways that are difficult to predict.

bills.who_affected: Farmers, agricultural businesses, fertilizer manufacturers, rural communities, food producers
bills.ticker_alert: 🌱 New bill would require fertilizer companies to disclose prices and ingredients

bills.title: To prohibit the enforcement of certain contractual clauses that restrict disclosure of sexual abuse of minors, and for other purposes.
bills.plain_title: Ban on Secret Clauses Hiding Child Sexual Abuse
bills.deck: This bill would make it illegal to enforce contract terms that prevent people from reporting child sexual abuse.
bills.plain_summary: This bill would ban the enforcement of non-disclosure agreements (NDAs) and similar contract clauses that silence victims, witnesses, or others from reporting sexual abuse of minors. Essentially, no contract could legally stop someone from speaking out about child sexual abuse. The bill was referred to the House Judiciary Committee, which is the first step in the legislative process.

impacts.text: Organizations like churches, schools, and sports clubs may face increased litigation exposure if past confidential settlements can be revisited or disclosed.
Child abuse survivors and witnesses would be free to report abuse or speak publicly without fear of breaching a legal contract or facing financial penalties.
Legal experts debate whether this bill could conflict with existing settlement agreements and whether it should be a federal versus state-level policy decision.

bills.who_affected: Child sexual abuse survivors, minors, parents of minors, attorneys handling abuse cases, schools, religious institutions, youth sports organizations, employers, insurance companies
bills.ticker_alert: 🚨 Bill would void secret contracts silencing child sexual abuse disclosures

bills.title: After Hours Child Care Act
bills.plain_title: Federal Grants for After-Hours Child Care Programs
bills.deck: Creates federal grants to fund child care programs operating during evenings, nights, and weekends.
bills.plain_summary: The After Hours Child Care Act would establish a federal grant program to help communities create or expand child care options outside of standard business hours, such as evenings, overnight, and weekends. This bill addresses a significant gap for parents who work non-traditional shifts, including healthcare workers, first responders, and retail employees. The bill was referred to the House Committee on Education and Workforce, meaning it is in an early stage and has not yet been voted on.

impacts.text: Requires new federal spending, potentially adding to the deficit or diverting funds from other programs
Could allow shift workers and single parents to access safe, affordable child care during nights and weekends, enabling workforce participation
Grant eligibility criteria and funding amounts are not yet fully defined, making the program's reach and effectiveness uncertain

bills.who_affected: Parents working night or weekend shifts, child care providers, low-income families, single parents, healthcare workers, first responders, retail and service industry employees
bills.ticker_alert: 🌙 Bill would fund after-hours child care for night-shift working parents

bills.title: Restore Protections for Dialysis Patients Act
bills.plain_title: Restore Dialysis Patient Insurance Protections Act
bills.deck: This bill would restore federal rules protecting dialysis patients from being pushed out of employer health insurance into Medicare.
bills.plain_summary: This bill would reinstate protections that prevent insurers and employers from steering kidney dialysis patients away from private health insurance and onto Medicare or Medicaid. A 2021 Supreme Court ruling weakened these protections, and this legislation aims to close that loophole. The bill has been referred to two House committees and has not yet been voted on.

impacts.text: Health insurers and employer plans may face higher costs covering expensive dialysis treatments they had shifted to Medicare
Dialysis patients could keep their private insurance with better coverage options instead of being forced onto government programs
This shifts dialysis costs between private insurers and Medicare, affecting how the federal government and employers share the financial burden

bills.who_affected: Kidney dialysis patients, people with end-stage renal disease, employer-sponsored health plan members, private health insurers, Medicare beneficiaries
bills.ticker_alert: 🩺 Bill would restore insurance protections for Americans on kidney dialysis

bills.title: VA Employee Fairness Act of 2025
bills.plain_title: VA Employee Workplace Rights and Fairness Bill
bills.deck: This bill would strengthen workplace protections and appeal rights for Department of Veterans Affairs employees.
bills.plain_summary: The VA Employee Fairness Act of 2025 (HR 3261) aims to improve employment protections for workers at the Department of Veterans Affairs, potentially restoring or expanding rights that were reduced under previous laws. The bill has been referred to the Subcommittee on Oversight and Investigations, meaning it is in early stages and has not yet received a full committee vote. It reflects ongoing congressional debate over how to balance accountability for VA employees with fair workplace treatment.

impacts.text: Critics argue stronger employee protections could make it harder to fire underperforming VA staff, potentially harming veterans who rely on quality care.
VA workers would gain stronger due process rights, potentially improving employee morale and retention at a chronically understaffed agency.
The bill reignites a long-running debate over whether VA accountability measures or employee protections better serve veterans in the long run.

bills.who_affected: VA employees and federal workers, veterans receiving VA healthcare and benefits, VA administrators and managers, federal labor unions
bills.ticker_alert: ⚖️ Bill would expand workplace rights for VA employees nationwide

bills.title: Virginia Plan to Reduce Gun Violence Act of 2026
bills.plain_title: Virginia State Plan to Cut Gun Violence
bills.deck: This bill would fund and promote Virginia's comprehensive strategy to reduce gun violence statewide.
bills.plain_summary: The Virginia Plan to Reduce Gun Violence Act of 2026 is a Senate bill that would support Virginia's multi-pronged approach to addressing gun violence, likely through federal funding, data sharing, or policy frameworks modeled on Virginia's existing state-level initiatives. The bill was introduced in the U.S. Senate and has been referred to the Senate Judiciary Committee, meaning it is in its earliest stage and has not yet received a vote. Because the full bill text is not publicly detailed here, the specific funding amounts and mechanisms are not yet confirmed.

impacts.text: Critics argue it could impose new restrictions on lawful gun owners or set precedents for federal involvement in state firearms policy.
Supporters say targeted gun violence reduction programs could lower shooting rates and save lives in communities across Virginia and potentially nationwide.
The bill's effectiveness depends heavily on implementation details and funding levels, which remain subject to committee revision.

bills.who_affected: Virginia residents, gun owners, law enforcement agencies, public health officials, gun violence survivors and advocates, Second Amendment advocacy groups
bills.ticker_alert: 🔫 Senate bill targets gun violence using Virginia's statewide reduction strategy

bills.title: WATCH Personnel Act of 2026
bills.plain_title: Aviation Safety Inspector Hiring Reform Act
bills.deck: Speeds up hiring of FAA safety inspectors to reduce staffing shortages in aviation oversight.
bills.plain_summary: The WATCH Personnel Act of 2026 aims to streamline and accelerate the hiring process for Federal Aviation Administration (FAA) safety inspectors and related personnel who oversee aircraft and airline safety. The bill addresses persistent staffing shortages at the FAA that critics say have hampered the agency's ability to adequately monitor aviation safety. It was referred to the Senate Committee on Commerce, Science, and Transportation, meaning it has not yet been voted on.

impacts.text: Fast-tracking hiring could reduce vetting rigor, potentially placing underqualified inspectors in critical safety roles.
More FAA inspectors on the job could mean more thorough oversight of airlines and aircraft, improving passenger safety.
The bill changes federal hiring rules for a specific agency, raising broader debates about civil service procedures and workforce standards.

bills.who_affected: Airline passengers, FAA employees and job applicants, commercial airlines, aircraft manufacturers, aviation safety advocates
bills.ticker_alert: ✈️ Senate bill aims to fast-track hiring of FAA aviation safety inspectors

bills.title: A joint resolution providing for congressional disapproval under chapter 8 of title 5, United States Code, of the rule submitted by the Bureau of Consumer Financial Protection relating to the withdrawal of the rule relating to "Debt Collection Practices (Regulation F); Pay-to-Pay Fees".
bills.plain_title: Overturning CFPB Rule on Debt Collector Junk Fees
bills.deck: This bill would block the CFPB from withdrawing its rule limiting extra fees charged by debt collectors.
bills.plain_summary: This bill uses a special congressional process to disapprove of the CFPB's decision to withdraw a rule that restricted 'pay-to-pay' fees — extra charges debt collectors impose when people pay by phone or online. By passing this resolution, Congress would essentially try to reinstate or preserve that consumer protection rule. The resolution has been reported out of committee and is awaiting further Senate action.

impacts.text: Without the rule, debt collectors can continue charging extra convenience fees when consumers pay by phone or online, adding costs to people already in financial distress
Blocking the CFPB's withdrawal could restore protections that shield consumers from surprise fees on top of existing debt payments
The bill's outcome depends on whether Congress agrees the CFPB overstepped by withdrawing the rule, a procedural and legal question with no settled consensus

bills.who_affected: Consumers with debt in collections, low-income Americans, debt collection agencies, financial technology payment processors
bills.ticker_alert: ⚖️ Senate bill seeks to restore limits on debt collector payment fees

bills.title: A joint resolution providing for congressional disapproval under chapter 8 of title 5, United States Code, of the rule submitted by the Bureau of Consumer Financial Protection relating to the withdrawal of the rule relating to "Equal Credit Opportunity (Regulation B); Revocations or Unfavorable Changes to the Terms of Existing Credit Arrangements".
bills.plain_title: Congress Overturning CFPB Fair Lending Protection Rollback
bills.deck: This bill would block a federal agency from withdrawing a rule protecting borrowers from discriminatory credit changes.
bills.plain_summary: This joint resolution uses a congressional tool called the Congressional Review Act to disapprove of a CFPB decision that withdrew a rule protecting borrowers from unfair or discriminatory changes to their existing credit terms. In plain terms, it tries to reinstate protections that required lenders to explain and justify negative changes — like rate hikes or credit limit cuts — made to existing loan accounts. The bill has been placed on the Senate calendar, meaning it is eligible for a full Senate vote.

impacts.text: If this resolution fails, lenders could more freely reduce credit limits or raise rates on existing accounts without detailed fair-lending scrutiny, potentially harming minority and low-income borrowers most.
If passed, it would restore rules requiring lenders to document and justify adverse changes to credit accounts, helping protect borrowers from discriminatory treatment.
Critics of the original CFPB rule argue it created regulatory burdens on lenders and that existing laws already provide sufficient discrimination protections without additional mandates.

bills.who_affected: Credit card holders, mortgage borrowers, minority borrowers, low-income borrowers, small business credit applicants, banks and credit lenders
bills.ticker_alert: ⚖️ Senate weighs restoring fair lending protections for existing borrowers

bills.title: Violet’s Law
bills.plain_title: Pet Food Safety and Ingredient Transparency Act
bills.deck: Requires pet food manufacturers to disclose ingredients and safety standards more clearly to consumers.
bills.plain_summary: Violet's Law would strengthen federal oversight of pet food safety and labeling, requiring manufacturers to provide clearer ingredient information and meet stricter safety standards. The bill is named after a pet who died allegedly due to unsafe pet food, and was referred to the House Committee on Agriculture, meaning it is in early stages. It has not yet been voted on by the full House.

impacts.text: Pet food manufacturers may face higher compliance costs, potentially raising prices for pet owners.
Pet owners would gain clearer information about what is in their pet's food, helping prevent illness or death from unsafe products.
Regulatory authority over pet food could shift or expand between USDA and FDA, raising questions about which agency leads enforcement.

bills.who_affected: Pet owners, pet food manufacturers, veterinarians, retail pet stores, agricultural producers supplying pet food ingredients
bills.ticker_alert: 🐾 Violet's Law targets pet food safety rules and labeling transparency nationwide

bills.title: FOOD for Health Act
bills.plain_title: Produce Prescriptions and Food-as-Medicine Federal Program
bills.deck: Creates federal programs allowing doctors to prescribe fruits and vegetables to patients with diet-related chronic diseases.
bills.plain_summary: The FOOD for Health Act would establish federal programs enabling healthcare providers to 'prescribe' fresh fruits, vegetables, and other healthy foods to patients suffering from diet-related illnesses like diabetes, heart disease, and obesity. Patients would receive vouchers or subsidies to purchase nutritious food as part of their medical treatment. The bill was referred to the House Committees on Agriculture and Energy and Commerce, where it awaits further review before any floor vote.

impacts.text: Federal spending increases could be significant, and critics question whether food prescriptions are cost-effective compared to other medical interventions.
Low-income Americans with chronic diseases like diabetes could receive subsidized healthy food, potentially reducing long-term medical costs and hospitalizations.
Implementation would require coordination between the agriculture and healthcare systems, raising unresolved questions about administration, eligibility, and program oversight.

bills.who_affected: Low-income patients with diet-related chronic diseases, farmers and grocery retailers, healthcare providers, Medicaid and Medicare enrollees, food banks and nutrition programs
bills.ticker_alert: 🥦 Bill would let doctors prescribe fruits and vegetables as medical treatment

bills.title: Precision Agriculture Satellite Connectivity Act
bills.plain_title: Farm Satellite Internet Access Expansion Act
bills.deck: This bill directs the FCC to study and improve satellite broadband connectivity for precision farming technology.
bills.plain_summary: This bill requires the Federal Communications Commission (FCC) to examine barriers to satellite internet access for farmers who use GPS-guided and data-driven farming tools, known as precision agriculture. The goal is to help rural farmers get reliable connectivity so they can use modern technology to improve crop yields and reduce waste. The bill has passed the House and is now waiting for action in the Senate Commerce Committee.

impacts.text: The bill focuses on study and recommendations rather than direct funding, meaning farmers may wait years before seeing real broadband improvements.
Farmers using GPS-guided tractors, soil sensors, and drone technology could get better satellite coverage, reducing costs and boosting productivity.
Satellite internet providers like SpaceX Starlink and others could face new regulatory scrutiny or benefit from expanded rural market demand, depending on outcomes.

bills.who_affected: Rural farmers, agricultural technology companies, satellite internet providers, rural small businesses, farm equipment manufacturers
bills.ticker_alert: 📡 Senate considers bill to boost satellite internet for America's farmers

bills.title: Tribal Access to Clean Water Act of 2025
bills.plain_title: Clean Water Access for Native American Tribes
bills.deck: This bill would improve access to clean drinking water and sanitation infrastructure for tribal communities across the United States.
bills.plain_summary: The Tribal Access to Clean Water Act of 2025 aims to address longstanding water quality and infrastructure gaps in Native American tribal communities, many of which lack reliable access to safe drinking water. The bill would direct federal agencies to fund and support water system improvements on tribal lands. It has been referred to three House committees — Natural Resources, Energy and Commerce, and Agriculture — where it must be reviewed before any floor vote can occur.

impacts.text: Federal spending on tribal water infrastructure could cost taxpayers hundreds of millions of dollars, adding to the national deficit.
Hundreds of thousands of Native Americans who currently lack safe drinking water could gain access to clean, reliable water systems.
Jurisdiction over tribal water projects involves multiple federal agencies, raising questions about coordination efficiency and which communities qualify for aid.

bills.who_affected: Native American tribal members, tribal governments, rural communities near tribal lands, federal agency employees, taxpayers
bills.ticker_alert: 💧 Bill proposes clean water infrastructure funding for Native American tribes nationwide

bills.title: Satellite-Based Agricultural Data Act
bills.plain_title: Farm Satellite Data Collection and Use Act
bills.deck: This bill directs the USDA to use satellite imagery to collect and analyze agricultural data for farmers.
bills.plain_summary: This bill would require or authorize the U.S. Department of Agriculture to use satellite-based technology to gather data on crops, land use, and farming conditions across the country. The goal is to improve the accuracy of agricultural statistics and help farmers and policymakers make better decisions. The bill has been referred to the House Committee on Agriculture, meaning it is in the early stages of the legislative process.

impacts.text: Farmers and landowners may have privacy concerns about government satellites monitoring their private land and crops without explicit consent.
Improved crop and weather data from satellites could help farmers better predict yields and access more timely disaster relief or crop insurance support.
The program's usefulness depends heavily on how the collected data is shared — whether it benefits individual farmers or primarily serves government statistical agencies.

bills.who_affected: Farmers, ranchers, agricultural landowners, crop insurance companies, USDA officials, rural communities, food industry analysts
bills.ticker_alert: 🛰️ New bill would use satellites to track and improve U.S. farm data

bills.title: Save Our Sequoias Act
bills.plain_title: Emergency Protection Plan for Giant Sequoia Trees
bills.deck: This bill funds emergency efforts to protect giant sequoia trees from wildfires, beetles, and disease.
bills.plain_summary: The Save Our Sequoias Act directs federal agencies to develop and fund an emergency conservation program to protect giant sequoia trees, which have been devastated by recent wildfires and other threats. The bill authorizes funding for habitat restoration, forest thinning, and research to prevent further loss of these ancient trees found primarily in California's Sierra Nevada. It passed the House and has been referred to the Senate Committee on Energy and Natural Resources, where it awaits further action.

impacts.text: Expedited environmental review processes could reduce public input and oversight on federal forest management actions.
Protects irreplaceable ancient trees up to 3,000 years old that support ecosystems, tourism, and cultural heritage for millions of Americans.
The bill authorizes new federal spending on forest management, with debate over whether logging and thinning methods are the right approach.

bills.who_affected: California residents, national park visitors, timber and forestry workers, environmental conservationists, Indigenous tribes with cultural ties to sequoias, outdoor recreation and tourism businesses
bills.ticker_alert: 🌲 Congress moves to rescue ancient giant sequoias threatened by wildfires and disease

bills.title: SAWMILL Act
bills.plain_title: Expanding Sawmill and Timber Processing Near National Forests
bills.deck: This bill would make it easier to build and operate sawmills on or near federal forest land.
bills.plain_summary: The SAWMILL Act would streamline the permitting and approval process for sawmill construction and timber processing facilities on or adjacent to National Forest System lands. It aims to boost domestic lumber production by reducing regulatory hurdles for wood processing operations. The bill was referred to the House Committees on Agriculture and Natural Resources, where it awaits further consideration.

impacts.text: Faster sawmill approvals near federal forests could increase logging pressure, potentially harming wildlife habitats and local ecosystems.
Expanded domestic sawmill capacity could lower lumber prices for American homebuilders and consumers while creating rural timber industry jobs.
The bill shifts some decision-making authority over federal forest land use toward timber interests, a balance debated by conservationists and industry alike.

bills.who_affected: Timber industry workers, rural communities near national forests, homebuilders, conservationists, wildlife advocates, federal forest managers
bills.ticker_alert: 🪵 SAWMILL Act would fast-track lumber mills near federal forests

bills.title: Joint Chiefs Reauthorization Act of 2025
bills.plain_title: Wildfire Prevention and Forest Management Reauthorization
bills.deck: Reauthorizes federal programs for wildfire prevention, forest health, and land management on public lands.
bills.plain_summary: Despite its name referencing 'Joint Chiefs,' this bill reauthorizes the Joint Chiefs Landscape Restoration Partnership, a USDA and Interior Department program that funds collaborative wildfire prevention and forest restoration projects on both federal and private lands. It was referred to the Agriculture and Natural Resources committees, meaning it is in early stages and has not yet received a vote. The bill aims to continue funding cooperative efforts between landowners, states, and federal agencies to reduce wildfire risk.

impacts.text: Critics worry reauthorization may extend bureaucratic land management programs that some argue slow timber harvesting and local economic activity.
Continued federal funding for wildfire prevention could reduce catastrophic wildfires that destroy homes, communities, and air quality across the western U.S.
The program coordinates federal and private landowners, raising questions about federal influence over state and private land decisions.

bills.who_affected: Western U.S. residents in wildfire-prone areas, private landowners near federal forests, timber industry workers, ranchers, state forestry agencies, rural communities
bills.ticker_alert: 🔥 Bill seeks to renew federal wildfire prevention and forest restoration partnerships

bills.title: Healthy Poultry Assistance and Indemnification Act of 2025
bills.plain_title: Poultry Farm Compensation for Bird Flu Losses
bills.deck: This bill would provide financial help to poultry farmers who lose birds to disease outbreaks like bird flu.
bills.plain_summary: The Healthy Poultry Assistance and Indemnification Act of 2025 (HR 1376) would set up or strengthen a federal program to compensate poultry farmers when their flocks must be destroyed due to disease outbreaks, such as avian influenza (bird flu). This type of indemnification helps farmers recover some of their financial losses so they can rebuild their operations. The bill has been referred to the House Subcommittee on Livestock, Dairy, and Poultry, meaning it is in early review and has not yet been voted on.

impacts.text: Taxpayers would fund compensation payments to poultry businesses, including large commercial operations, even if those businesses could afford private insurance.
Faster, guaranteed compensation could help poultry farmers recover quickly from disease outbreaks, stabilizing egg and chicken prices for consumers.
The bill's effectiveness depends heavily on how compensation amounts are calculated and whether small family farms receive the same benefits as large agribusinesses.

bills.who_affected: Poultry farmers, egg producers, chicken and turkey processors, rural agricultural communities, grocery shoppers, federal taxpayers
bills.ticker_alert: 🐔 Bill proposes federal payouts to poultry farmers hit by disease outbreaks

bills.title: Grasslands Grazing Act of 2025
bills.plain_title: Expanding Cattle Grazing on Federal Grasslands
bills.deck: This bill would increase livestock grazing access on federally managed grasslands and prairie lands.
bills.plain_summary: The Grasslands Grazing Act of 2025 would expand grazing permits and rights for ranchers and livestock operators on federal grasslands, likely managed by agencies like the Forest Service or BLM. It has been ordered to advance out of committee with amendments, passing by a 25-14 vote, suggesting some bipartisan division. The bill now moves toward a full chamber vote.

impacts.text: Increased grazing on federal lands could damage native prairie ecosystems, reduce biodiversity, and harm water quality for communities near affected lands.
Ranchers and rural livestock operators would gain expanded, more secure access to federal grazing land, supporting livelihoods in agricultural communities.

The bill shifts how federal land managers balance conservation goals against livestock industry needs, a long-contested land-use policy debate with no clear scientific consensus on optimal grazing levels.

bills.who_affected: Ranchers and livestock operators, rural agricultural communities, conservation and wildlife advocates, hikers and outdoor recreationists, Native American tribes with land interests, taxpayers who fund federal land management
bills.ticker_alert: 🌾 Congress advances bill to expand cattle grazing on federal grasslands

bills.title: MATCH Act of 2025
bills.plain_title: Matching Funds for Agriculture Conservation Programs
bills.deck: Requires federal agriculture conservation funding to be matched by state or local contributions before release.
bills.plain_summary: The MATCH Act of 2025 (HR 5781) would require state or local governments to provide matching funds before receiving certain federal agricultural conservation program dollars. This bill has been referred to the House Committee on Agriculture, meaning it is in the early stages of the legislative process and has not yet been voted on. The sponsor's full details are available on Congress.gov.

impacts.text: Farmers in lower-income or rural states may lose access to conservation funding if their state cannot afford the required match.
Taxpayers benefit from increased accountability and shared investment, ensuring states have skin in the game before federal dollars are spent.
Wealthier states with larger budgets could receive disproportionately more federal conservation money, shifting program distribution patterns significantly.

bills.who_affected: Farmers, ranchers, state agriculture departments, rural landowners, conservation program administrators, federal taxpayers
bills.ticker_alert: 🌾 MATCH Act would require states to share costs of farm conservation grants

bills.title: Combating Organized Retail Crime Act of 2025
bills.plain_title: Federal Crackdown on Organized Retail Theft Rings
bills.deck: This bill creates federal tools to prosecute and disrupt large-scale organized retail theft operations across the country.
bills.plain_summary: The Combating Organized Retail Crime Act of 2025 would make organized retail theft a federal crime when it crosses state lines or involves criminal networks, and would create a dedicated federal task force to investigate and prosecute these cases. It also targets online marketplaces that are used to resell stolen goods. The bill has passed committee review and is awaiting a full House vote, having been placed on the Union Calendar.

impacts.text: Civil liberties advocates warn that broad federal definitions of 'organized' crime could ensnare small-scale offenders or individuals with minimal coordination in serious federal charges.
Shoppers and retailers could benefit from lower prices and better-stocked stores if large theft rings are disrupted, as retailers report losses of billions annually passed on to consumers.
The bill shifts jurisdiction of certain theft cases from state and local courts to federal prosecutors, changing how and where defendants are tried.

bills.who_affected: Retail workers, retail business owners, online marketplace sellers, consumers, law enforcement agencies, criminal defense attorneys, people accused of retail theft
bills.ticker_alert: 🚨 Congress targets organized retail theft rings with new federal crime law

bills.title: Combating Organized Retail Crime Act
bills.plain_title: Federal Crackdown on Organized Retail Theft Rings
bills.deck: Creates new federal crimes and enforcement tools to combat large-scale, coordinated retail theft operations.
bills.plain_summary: This bill makes organized retail theft a federal crime when it involves criminal networks stealing merchandise to resell it. It strengthens penalties, creates a new federal task force, and requires online marketplaces to better screen high-volume sellers to stop stolen goods from being resold online. The bill was referred to the Senate Judiciary Committee, where it awaits further review before any vote.

impacts.text: Critics warn expanded federal criminal definitions could ensnare small-time offenders or be applied too broadly beyond true organized crime rings.
Retailers and consumers could see fewer store closures, lower prices, and reduced product shortages caused by large theft rings draining inventory.
Online marketplaces like Amazon and Facebook would face new compliance burdens to verify sellers, which could raise operating costs or restrict legitimate small sellers.

bills.who_affected: Retail workers, retail store owners, small businesses, online marketplace sellers, consumers, law enforcement agencies, e-commerce platforms
bills.ticker_alert: 🚨 Senate bill targets organized retail theft rings with new federal crimes

bills.title: Workforce Investments Accountability Act
bills.plain_title: Job Training Program Transparency and Accountability Act
bills.deck: Requires federal job training programs to publicly report how well they actually help workers find employment.
bills.plain_summary: This bill would require federally funded workforce development and job training programs to publicly disclose detailed performance data, including how many participants found jobs and their wages afterward. It aims to hold these programs accountable for the taxpayer dollars they receive. The bill was referred to the House Committee on Education and Workforce, meaning it is in early stages and has not yet been voted on.

impacts.text: Smaller workforce programs may face costly administrative burdens to collect and report detailed outcome data, potentially diverting funds from actual training services.
Job seekers and policymakers would gain clearer information about which training programs actually lead to good-paying jobs, helping workers make better choices.
Defining what counts as a successful job placement outcome is contested — critics debate whether wage thresholds or employment duration best measures program success.

bills.who_affected: Unemployed workers, displaced workers seeking retraining, community colleges, vocational training providers, workforce development nonprofits, state workforce agencies, low-income job seekers
bills.ticker_alert: 📋 New bill would require job training programs to prove they actually work

bills.title: Kimberly Vaughan Firearm Safe Storage Act
bills.plain_title: Firearm Safe Storage Tax Credit and Safety Requirements
bills.deck: Requires gun owners to safely store firearms and offers tax credits for buying gun safes.
bills.plain_summary: This bill would require firearm owners to store guns in a locked container or with a trigger lock when not in use, especially when minors are present. It also creates a tax credit to help Americans offset the cost of purchasing safe storage devices. The bill was referred to the House Judiciary and Ways and Means Committees, where it awaits further action.

impacts.text: Gun owners could face legal penalties if firearms are not stored per requirements, potentially limiting quick access for self-defense in emergencies.
Safe storage mandates could reduce accidental child shootings, teen suicides, and gun thefts, potentially saving thousands of lives annually.
The tax credit makes compliance more affordable, but debates continue over whether storage mandates infringe on Second Amendment rights.

bills.who_affected: Gun owners, parents with children at home, low-income firearm owners, children and teenagers, law enforcement agencies
bills.ticker_alert: 🔒 New bill would require locked gun storage, offer tax credits for safes

bills.title: Protect Moms From Domestic Violence Act
bills.plain_title: Protecting Mothers Who Are Domestic Violence Survivors
bills.deck: Requires federal maternal health programs to screen for and address domestic violence affecting pregnant and postpartum women.
bills.plain_summary: This bill would require federally funded maternal health programs to incorporate domestic violence screening, referrals, and support services for pregnant women and new mothers. It aims to connect women experiencing abuse with resources during a vulnerable period when domestic violence rates often spike. The bill was referred to the Senate Health, Education, Labor, and Pensions Committee, where it awaits further consideration.

impacts.text: Could place additional administrative and cost burdens on already underfunded maternal health clinics and providers.
Pregnant and postpartum women experiencing domestic violence could be identified earlier and connected to potentially life-saving resources.
Effectiveness depends on funding levels, provider training, and whether screened women can actually access domestic violence services in their area.

bills.who_affected: Pregnant women, new mothers, domestic violence survivors, maternal health providers, federally funded health clinics, social workers
bills.ticker_alert: 🤱 Senate bill would require domestic violence screening at maternal health programs

bills.title: Federal Law Enforcement and Public Protection Act
bills.plain_title: Federal Officers Protected from State Gun Laws
bills.deck: This bill would exempt federal law enforcement officers and military from state and local firearms laws.
bills.plain_summary: The Federal Law Enforcement and Public Protection Act would allow federal law enforcement officers and military personnel to carry firearms anywhere in the country, overriding state and local gun restrictions. The bill was referred to both the House Judiciary Committee and the Armed Services Committee, meaning it must clear both panels before advancing. It reflects an ongoing tension between federal authority and states' rights to regulate firearms within their borders.

impacts.text: State and local gun safety laws could be bypassed by tens of thousands of federal employees, undermining local voter-approved regulations
Federal officers could respond to emergencies off-duty without fear of violating local gun laws, potentially improving public safety responses
The bill raises unresolved constitutional questions about federal supremacy versus states' rights to govern firearms within their own jurisdictions

bills.who_affected: Federal law enforcement officers, military personnel, state and local governments, residents of states with strict gun laws
bills.ticker_alert: ⚖️ Bill would let federal officers bypass state gun laws nationwide

bills.title: FAIR Labels Act of 2026
bills.plain_title: Honest Food Label Requirements Act
bills.deck: Requires clearer, standardized labeling on food products sold in the United States.
bills.plain_summary: The FAIR Labels Act of 2026 would establish new federal requirements for how food products must be labeled, aiming to give consumers more accurate and transparent information about what they are buying. The bill has been referred to both the House Agriculture Committee and the Energy and Commerce Committee, meaning it must pass through two separate panels before a full House vote. It is in early stages and has not yet received a committee hearing or vote.

impacts.text: Food manufacturers and retailers could face significant compliance costs to redesign packaging, potentially raising grocery prices for consumers.
Shoppers would gain clearer, standardized information on food labels, making it easier to compare products and make informed dietary choices.
Federal oversight of food labeling would expand, shifting some authority from states and industry to federal regulators, which supporters see as consistency and critics see as overreach.

bills.who_affected: Grocery shoppers, food manufacturers, farmers, food retailers, nutritionists, food allergy sufferers, parents of young children
bills.ticker_alert: 🏷️ New bill would overhaul food labels to boost consumer transparency nationwide

bills.title: Equitable Transit Oriented Development Support Act
bills.plain_title: Federal Support for Affordable Housing Near Public Transit
bills.deck: This bill would create federal grants to build affordable housing and community services near public transit stops.
bills.plain_summary: The Equitable Transit Oriented Development Support Act would establish a federal grant program to fund affordable housing, small businesses, and community facilities built near public transit stations.
