The goal is to ensure lower-income residents can live close to transit and avoid displacement as neighborhoods around transit hubs become more expensive. The bill has been referred to the House Committee on Transportation and Infrastructure, meaning it is in early stages and has not yet been voted on.

impacts.text: Federal spending increases could add to the deficit, and critics argue local housing decisions should not be directed by Washington bureaucrats.
Low-income families and renters near transit hubs could gain access to affordable housing, reducing commute costs and preventing displacement from gentrifying neighborhoods.
Grant eligibility rules and how 'equitable' development is defined could be contested, with debates over which communities or projects qualify for funding.

bills.who_affected: Low-income renters, public transit riders, residents near transit stations, local governments, affordable housing developers, small business owners in transit corridors
bills.ticker_alert: 🏘️ New bill proposes federal grants for affordable housing near transit stops

bills.title: College Affordability and Accessibility Act
bills.plain_title: Making College More Affordable and Accessible
bills.deck: This bill aims to reduce college costs and improve access to higher education for American students.
bills.plain_summary: The College Affordability and Accessibility Act (HR 8618) proposes measures to lower the cost of attending college and make higher education more accessible to students across the country. The bill has been referred to the House Committee on Education and Workforce, meaning it is in the early stages of the legislative process and has not yet been voted on. Until the committee reviews it, no further action will be taken on the bill.

impacts.text: Proposed funding measures or mandates could increase costs for colleges, potentially leading to higher tuition or reduced program offerings at some institutions.
Students from low- and middle-income families could gain greater access to financial aid or lower tuition costs, reducing student debt burdens.
The bill's actual effectiveness in lowering college costs is debated, as critics argue structural issues in higher education pricing may limit its real-world impact.

bills.who_affected: College students, prospective college students, low-income families, middle-income families, college administrators, financial aid officers, taxpayers
bills.ticker_alert: 🎓 HR 8618 targets college affordability — in committee review now

bills.title: Community Connect Grant Program Act of 2026
bills.plain_title: Rural Broadband Internet Grant Program Expansion
bills.deck: This bill would expand federal grants to bring high-speed internet to unserved rural communities.
bills.plain_summary: This bill would reauthorize and strengthen the Community Connect Grant Program, which provides federal funding to help bring broadband internet access to rural areas that currently have little or no service. The grants help communities build shared internet infrastructure like community centers with public computer access. The bill has been referred to the Senate Agriculture Committee and has not yet been voted on.

impacts.text: Increases federal spending, adding to the deficit without guaranteed private-sector efficiency in broadband deployment.
Rural residents gain access to high-speed internet, enabling remote work, telehealth, and better educational opportunities.
Effectiveness depends on how grants are administered and whether funded infrastructure remains viable long-term without ongoing subsidies.

bills.who_affected: Rural residents without broadband access, small-town businesses, farmers, rural students, telehealth patients, rural local governments
bills.ticker_alert: 📡 Senate bill would expand broadband grants for underserved rural communities

bills.title: Providing for congressional disapproval under chapter 8 of title 5, United States Code, of the rule submitted by the Bureau of Consumer Financial Protection relating to the withdrawal of the rule relating to "Application of Regulation Z's Ability-To-Repay Rule to Certain Situations Involving Successors-In-Interest".
bills.plain_title: Blocking Mortgage Protections for Surviving Home Heirs
bills.deck: This bill would cancel a consumer protection rule that helps family members take over a deceased relative's mortgage.
bills.plain_summary: This resolution would overturn a federal rule that protected people who inherit a home and need to take over the mortgage — such as a surviving spouse or child. The original rule ensured lenders had to work with these 'successors-in-interest' rather than immediately demanding full repayment. The bill has been referred to the House Committee on Financial Services and has not yet been voted on.

impacts.text: Surviving family members who inherit a home could face immediate foreclosure demands instead of being allowed to assume the mortgage under existing consumer protections.
Lenders would have more flexibility in how they handle successor mortgage situations, potentially reducing regulatory compliance costs that can affect loan pricing.
The underlying legal debate centers on whether the CFPB had authority to withdraw its own prior rule, making this a procedural dispute as much as a policy one.

bills.who_affected: Surviving spouses, adult children who inherit homes, estate executors, mortgage servicers, housing counselors
bills.ticker_alert: ⚠️ Congress moves to strip mortgage protections for Americans who inherit homes

bills.title: Providing for congressional disapproval under chapter 8 of title 5, United States Code, of the rule submitted by the Bureau of Consumer Financial Protection relating to the withdrawal of the rule relating to "Consumer Financial Protection Circular 2024-04: Whistleblower Protections Under CFPA Section 1057".
bills.plain_title: Restore Whistleblower Protections for Financial Watchdog Reporters
bills.deck: This bill would reinstate federal protections for people who report wrongdoing at consumer financial companies.
bills.plain_summary: This bill uses a congressional tool to overturn a rule by the Consumer Financial Protection Bureau (CFPB) that withdrew whistleblower protections for employees who report violations of consumer financial laws. Essentially, Congress is trying to restore those protections after the CFPB eliminated them. The bill has been referred to the House Committee on Financial Services, which is the first step in the legislative process.

impacts.text: Without restored protections, financial industry employees who report corporate misconduct may face retaliation with less legal recourse, chilling future reporting.
Restoring these protections could encourage more workers to report financial fraud, helping consumers avoid predatory practices and scams.
The debate reflects an ongoing disagreement about the CFPB's proper scope and whether its whistleblower rules are legally sound or necessary.

bills.who_affected: Bank and financial company employees, consumer advocates, financial industry compliance officers, everyday banking and loan customers
bills.ticker_alert: 📢 Congress moves to restore CFPB whistleblower protections for financial workers

bills.title: Adopting the Rules of the House of Representatives for the One Hundred Nineteenth Congress, and for other purposes.
bills.plain_title: House of Representatives Rules for 119th Congress
bills.deck: Sets the official rules and procedures governing how the House of Representatives will operate for two years.
bills.plain_summary: This resolution adopts the formal rulebook that governs how the House of Representatives conducts its business for the 119th Congress (2025-2026). It covers everything from how bills are debated and voted on, to committee structures and member conduct. The resolution has been passed and finalized, meaning the House is now operating under these rules.

impacts.text: Critics argue procedural rules can be used by the majority party to limit debate, restrict amendments, and reduce minority party input on legislation affecting all Americans
Establishes clear, consistent procedures that bring order and transparency to the lawmaking process, helping the public understand how legislation moves through Congress
Rules determine which party has procedural advantages in scheduling votes and advancing legislation, directly shaping what laws Congress can pass over the next two years

bills.who_affected: All Americans (indirectly through legislation shaped by these rules), Members of Congress, Congressional staff, lobbyists, advocacy groups, journalists covering Congress
bills.ticker_alert: 📋 House adopts official rules governing all legislation for 119th Congress

bills.title: Protecting American Energy Production Act
bills.plain_title: Ban on Presidential Moratoriums on Energy Drilling
bills.deck: This bill would prohibit presidents from unilaterally banning oil, gas, or coal production on federal lands.
bills.plain_summary: The Protecting American Energy Production Act would make it illegal for a president to declare a moratorium on oil, natural gas, or coal production on federal lands without approval from Congress. This means no single executive order could halt energy drilling or mining on public lands. The bill has been referred to the House Committees on Natural Resources and Energy and Commerce for review.

impacts.text: Limits the president's ability to pause fossil fuel extraction in response to environmental emergencies or climate concerns without a Congressional vote.
Could provide greater stability for energy companies and workers by reducing the risk of sudden production shutdowns from executive orders.
Shifts the balance of power over federal energy policy from the executive branch to Congress, a separation-of-powers debate with no clear partisan winner long-term.

bills.who_affected: Oil and gas workers, coal miners, energy companies, environmental advocates, residents near federal drilling sites, taxpayers who benefit from federal energy royalties
bills.ticker_alert: ⚡ Bill would require Congress to approve any federal energy drilling ban

bills.title: Clean Air and Building Infrastructure Improvement Act
bills.plain_title: School and Federal Building Air Quality Upgrade Act
bills.deck: This bill funds HVAC and air filtration upgrades in schools and federal buildings to improve indoor air quality.
bills.plain_summary: The Clean Air and Building Infrastructure Improvement Act would provide funding to upgrade heating, ventilation, and air conditioning (HVAC) systems in public schools and federal buildings, with a focus on improving indoor air quality. The bill has been placed on the Union Calendar, meaning it has cleared committee and is eligible for a full House vote. It aims to reduce exposure to airborne pollutants and pathogens in buildings where Americans work, learn, and receive government services.

impacts.text: Federal spending on building upgrades adds to the national deficit without guaranteed long-term maintenance funding.
Students and federal workers would breathe cleaner air, potentially reducing illness, absenteeism, and healthcare costs.
Prioritization of which schools or buildings receive upgrades first remains unclear and could be contentious.

bills.who_affected: Public school students and teachers, federal government employees, building maintenance contractors, taxpayers, low-income communities near older school buildings
bills.ticker_alert: 🏫 Bill would fund air quality upgrades in schools and federal buildings

bills.title: Chip Security Act
bills.plain_title: Semiconductor Chip Security Standards and Tracking Bill
bills.deck: Requires security standards and tracking measures for semiconductors used in critical U.S. systems.
bills.plain_summary: The Chip Security Act would establish security requirements and tracking or authentication measures for semiconductor chips used in critical infrastructure and government systems, helping to detect counterfeit or compromised chips. The bill passed its committee vote unanimously 42-0, signaling broad bipartisan support. It is now moving forward in the legislative process toward a full House vote.

impacts.text: Compliance costs for chip manufacturers and buyers could raise prices for electronics and government contracts.
Stronger chip authentication could reduce counterfeit and malicious chips in military and critical infrastructure systems, improving national security.
Implementation details, including which chips are covered and who enforces standards, remain subject to rulemaking and could affect industry broadly or narrowly.

bills.who_affected: Semiconductor manufacturers, defense contractors, federal agencies, critical infrastructure operators, electronics consumers
bills.ticker_alert: 🔒 Chip Security Act advances unanimously to protect U.S. semiconductors from tampering

bills.title: Consumer Protection and Corporate Accountability in Bankruptcy Act of 2026
bills.plain_title: Protecting Consumers When Big Companies Go Bankrupt
bills.deck: This bill limits how corporations can use bankruptcy to escape consumer lawsuits and debt obligations.
bills.plain_summary: This bill would change bankruptcy rules to make it harder for large corporations to use Chapter 11 bankruptcy to dismiss lawsuits from injured consumers, cancel warranties, or avoid paying settlements. It aims to ensure that ordinary people harmed by a company still have a path to compensation even if that company declares bankruptcy. The bill was introduced in the U.S. Senate and has been referred to the Senate Judiciary Committee, where it awaits further review.

impacts.text: Businesses facing mass litigation may find it harder to reorganize financially, potentially leading to liquidation and more job losses instead of restructuring.
Consumers injured by defective products or corporate misconduct would have stronger legal rights to seek compensation even if the company files for bankruptcy.
Courts would need to give greater priority to consumer claims in bankruptcy proceedings, shifting how creditor payments are ranked and distributed.

bills.who_affected: Bankruptcy claimants, personal injury victims, product liability plaintiffs, corporate creditors, bankruptcy attorneys, large corporations facing mass tort litigation
bills.ticker_alert: ⚖️ Senate bill would block corporations from using bankruptcy to dodge consumer lawsuits

bills.title: Ensuring Federal Purchasing Efficiency Act
bills.plain_title: Federal Government Buying Process Efficiency Reform
bills.deck: This bill reforms how the federal government purchases goods and services to reduce waste and save taxpayer money.
bills.plain_summary: The Ensuring Federal Purchasing Efficiency Act aims to streamline and modernize how the federal government buys goods and services, reducing bureaucratic waste and improving cost-effectiveness. The bill was ordered to move forward by a unanimous 44-0 committee vote, suggesting broad bipartisan support. It is currently in the legislative process after clearing committee and awaiting further congressional action.

impacts.text: Streamlining procurement rules could reduce competition, potentially favoring larger contractors over small businesses that rely on current federal purchasing processes.
More efficient federal purchasing could reduce government waste and save taxpayer dollars, potentially freeing funds for other public priorities.
Changes to procurement rules will shift how billions in federal contracts are awarded, reshaping relationships between agencies and private vendors in ways that are still unclear.

bills.who_affected: Federal government contractors, small business owners, taxpayers, federal agency procurement officers, defense and civilian agency vendors
bills.ticker_alert: 🏛️ Congress moves to overhaul federal buying rules in unanimous committee vote

bills.title: CLEAR LABELS Act
bills.plain_title: Mandatory Food Labeling for Lab-Grown and Gene-Edited Products
bills.deck: Requires clear labels on food made from lab-grown cells or gene-edited organisms so consumers know what they're buying.
bills.plain_summary: The CLEAR LABELS Act would require food products made using cell-cultured technology or gene-editing techniques to be clearly labeled as such on packaging. This gives shoppers transparent information about how their food was produced before they buy it. The bill has been introduced in the Senate and is currently under review by the Health, Education, Labor, and Pensions Committee.

impacts.text: Labeling requirements could raise costs for food companies, potentially increasing prices for consumers at the grocery store.
Shoppers gain clear, upfront information about whether food came from lab-grown cells or gene-edited sources, empowering informed choices.
Scientists and industry groups debate whether lab-grown or gene-edited foods are meaningfully different from conventional foods in safety or nutrition.

bills.who_affected: Grocery shoppers, food manufacturers, lab-grown meat startups, gene-edited crop producers, farmers, religious and dietary-restriction communities
bills.ticker_alert: 🏷️ Senate bill would require labels on lab-grown and gene-edited foods

bills.title: Merger Process Review Act
bills.plain_title: Review How Government Approves or Blocks Corporate Mergers
bills.deck: This bill would study and potentially reform the federal process for reviewing corporate mergers and acquisitions.
bills.plain_summary: The Merger Process Review Act directs a review of how federal agencies currently evaluate proposed corporate mergers to determine if the process is fair, efficient, and transparent. It examines whether companies seeking to merge face unclear rules or inconsistent enforcement from agencies like the FTC and DOJ. The bill has been placed on the Union Calendar, meaning it is eligible for a full House vote but has not yet passed.

impacts.text: Critics worry the review could be used to weaken merger oversight, allowing monopolies that raise prices for consumers.
Supporters say clearer merger rules could reduce costly legal uncertainty for businesses and potentially encourage investment and job creation.
The bill's outcome depends entirely on what the review recommends — it could lead to stronger or weaker antitrust enforcement.

bills.who_affected: Corporate executives, antitrust lawyers, small business competitors, consumers buying goods from merging industries, investors, federal regulators
bills.ticker_alert: 📋 Congress may reform how feds approve or block corporate mergers

bills.title: FAIR Exams Act
bills.plain_title: Allow Private Exam Centers to Compete with College Board
bills.deck: This bill lets independent testing centers administer AP exams, breaking College Board's monopoly on Advanced Placement testing.
bills.plain_summary: The FAIR Exams Act would require the College Board to allow third-party, non-school testing centers to administer Advanced Placement (AP) exams, not just schools. This would give students more options for where and when they take AP tests. The bill has been placed on the Union Calendar, meaning it has passed committee review and is awaiting a full House vote.

impacts.text: Critics worry that loosening College Board oversight of testing sites could increase cheating risks or inconsistent testing conditions, potentially undermining AP exam credibility.
Students without access to AP courses at their schools — including homeschooled or rural students — could more easily access affordable AP testing at nearby independent centers.
The bill shifts control over exam site approval away from the College Board, a private nonprofit, which could spark legal or contractual disputes over who sets quality standards.

bills.who_affected: High school students, homeschooled students, rural students, College Board, private tutoring and test prep centers, K-12 schools
bills.ticker_alert: 📋 FAIR Exams Act would open AP testing beyond school campuses nationwide

bills.title: SAFE Guidance Act
bills.plain_title: Require Congress Approval for Major Federal Agency Guidance
bills.deck: This bill would require Congress to approve major federal agency guidance documents before they take effect.
bills.plain_summary: The SAFE Guidance Act would require federal agencies to submit significant guidance documents to Congress for review and approval before those guidelines can be enforced. Guidance documents are instructions agencies issue to explain how they interpret laws and regulations, and critics argue agencies sometimes use them to create new rules without going through the normal public comment process. The bill has been placed on the Union Calendar, meaning it has passed committee and is awaiting a full House vote.

impacts.text: Could slow down important public health, safety, or environmental protections if Congress delays approving agency guidance
Businesses and individuals would have more democratic oversight and predictability over rules that affect them before enforcement begins
The definition of what counts as a 'significant' guidance document subject to review remains a contested and debated threshold

bills.who_affected: Small business owners, corporations subject to federal regulation, environmental advocates, public health officials, federal agency employees, lawmakers and their staff
bills.ticker_alert: 📋 Bill would give Congress veto power over major federal agency guidance rules

bills.title: Failing Bank Acquisition Fairness Act
bills.plain_title: Fair Rules for Buying Failing Banks
bills.deck: This bill sets new rules for how the government handles sales of failing banks to private buyers.
bills.plain_summary: The Failing Bank Acquisition Fairness Act would change the process the FDIC uses when selling a failing bank, requiring more transparency and competitive bidding to prevent large banks from getting unfair advantages when buying collapsed rivals. It was placed on the Union Calendar, meaning it has cleared committee and is awaiting a full House vote. The bill responds to concerns raised after high-profile bank failures in 2023, where critics argued the acquisition process favored big banks over smaller competitors.

impacts.text: Could slow down emergency bank rescues if stricter bidding rules delay deals during financial crises, potentially increasing risks to depositors.
More competitive bidding for failing banks could reduce costs to the FDIC's deposit insurance fund, which ultimately protects taxpayers and depositors.
The bill could reshape which types of financial institutions are able to acquire failing banks, shifting market power in ways that are debated by economists.

bills.who_affected: Bank depositors, small and regional banks, large national banks, FDIC regulators, taxpayers who back deposit insurance
bills.ticker_alert: 🏦 House bill seeks fairer rules when government sells failing banks

bills.title: Keeping Deposits Local Act
bills.plain_title: Keep Bank Deposits in Local Communities Act
bills.deck: Requires banks to reinvest a portion of deposits back into the local communities where those deposits were collected.
bills.plain_summary: The Keeping Deposits Local Act would require banks and financial institutions to reinvest a set percentage of deposits into the communities where customers made those deposits, rather than funneling money to distant markets. This bill aims to ensure that community savings stay accessible for local lending, small business loans, and neighborhood development. It has been placed on the Union Calendar, meaning it has passed committee and is eligible for a full House vote.

impacts.text: Banks may face higher compliance costs, potentially leading to reduced interest rates on savings accounts or higher loan fees for customers.
Residents in underserved or rural communities could gain better access to local loans and credit that might otherwise flow to wealthier markets.
How 'local' reinvestment is defined and enforced remains contested, with ongoing debate about whether geographic deposit-matching truly improves community economic outcomes.

bills.who_affected: Community bank customers, small business owners, rural residents, low-income neighborhoods, large national banks, mortgage borrowers
bills.ticker_alert: 🏦 New bill would force banks to reinvest deposits in local communities

bills.title: TIER Act of 2025
bills.plain_title: Tax Incentives for Employee Retirement Savings Act
bills.deck: This bill would expand tax benefits for employer-sponsored retirement plans to encourage more workplace savings.
bills.plain_summary: The TIER Act of 2025 (HR 6553) proposes changes to tax incentives related to employer-provided retirement plans, aiming to make it easier and more attractive for businesses to offer retirement benefits to workers. The bill has been placed on the Union Calendar, meaning it has cleared committee and is awaiting a full House vote. Details on the specific sponsor are available on Congress.gov, but the bill reflects ongoing congressional efforts to strengthen retirement security for American workers.

impacts.text: Tax incentives for employers could reduce federal revenue, potentially requiring spending cuts or tax increases elsewhere to offset the cost.
Workers at small and mid-sized businesses may gain access to better employer-sponsored retirement plans, boosting long-term savings security.
Critics debate whether tax incentives primarily benefit higher-income workers and larger businesses rather than lower-wage employees who need retirement support most.

bills.who_affected: Private-sector employees, small business owners, self-employed workers, retirement plan administrators, lower-income workers without current retirement benefits
bills.ticker_alert: 💼 TIER Act advances in House, targeting expanded workplace retirement savings incentives

bills.title: Promoting New Bank Formation Act
bills.plain_title: Make It Easier to Start New Banks
bills.deck: This bill reduces regulatory burdens to encourage the creation of new community and small banks across the U.S.
bills.plain_summary: The Promoting New Bank Formation Act (HR 478) aims to make it easier and less costly to open new banks in the United States by easing certain federal regulatory requirements that critics say have discouraged new bank formation since the 2008 financial crisis. The number of new bank applications has dropped dramatically in recent decades, and supporters argue this bill would help restore competition and expand access to credit, especially in underserved communities. The bill has been placed on the Union Calendar, meaning it has cleared committee and is awaiting a full House vote.

impacts.text: Loosening startup requirements for new banks could increase the risk of bank failures, potentially exposing depositors and taxpayers to financial losses.
More new banks could mean greater access to loans and financial services for small businesses and residents in rural or underserved communities.
Whether reduced regulatory barriers will actually lead to meaningful new bank formation — or simply lower safety standards — is actively debated among economists and banking experts.

bills.who_affected: Small business owners, rural and underserved community residents, prospective bank founders, existing community banks, bank regulators, investors
bills.ticker_alert: 🏦 House bill seeks to lower barriers for starting new community banks

bills.title: Preventing Environmental Hazards Act of 2025
bills.plain_title: Bill to Reduce Environmental Hazard Financial Risks
bills.deck: This bill would require financial institutions to account for environmental hazard risks in their lending and investment decisions.
bills.plain_summary: The Preventing Environmental Hazards Act of 2025 (HR 3161) would direct financial regulators and institutions to consider environmental hazard risks — such as pollution or toxic exposure — when making loans or investments. The bill has been referred to the House Committee on Financial Services, meaning it is in the early stages of the legislative process. Sponsor information is available on Congress.gov, and the bill has not yet moved to a full House vote.

impacts.text: Banks and lenders may face new compliance costs, potentially making loans harder or more expensive to obtain in affected areas
Communities near environmental hazards could gain stronger financial protections and clearer risk disclosures from lenders
How 'environmental hazard' is defined in practice will significantly shape which industries and regions are most affected

bills.who_affected: Homebuyers near industrial sites, small business owners, banks and lenders, real estate investors, residents of low-income or environmentally burdened communities
bills.ticker_alert: 🌿 New bill would require banks to factor environmental hazards into lending decisions

bills.title: Merger Agreement Approvals Clarity and Predictability Act
bills.plain_title: Clearer Rules for Corporate Merger Vote Approvals
bills.deck: This bill sets clear federal standards for how shareholders must approve major company mergers and acquisitions.
bills.plain_summary: This bill clarifies the legal rules governing how shareholder votes on corporate mergers and acquisitions must be conducted and approved. It aims to reduce legal uncertainty and costly litigation by standardizing when a merger agreement is considered properly approved. The bill has been placed on the Union Calendar, meaning it has passed committee review and awaits a full House vote.

impacts.text: Could make it harder for shareholders to challenge merger deals they believe undervalue their stock or harm their interests
Reduces expensive legal battles over merger approvals, potentially lowering costs that companies pass on to investors and consumers
Shifts some authority over merger approval standards from state courts, like Delaware's, to a federal framework, altering long-standing corporate law traditions

bills.who_affected: Corporate shareholders, public company executives and boards, mergers and acquisitions lawyers, institutional investors, pension fund managers
bills.ticker_alert: 📋 House bill would standardize corporate merger shareholder approval rules nationwide

bills.title: Enhancing Bank Resolution Participation Act
bills.plain_title: Bank Failure Resolution Process Transparency Act
bills.deck: Requires federal regulators to include more private-sector experts when planning how to handle large bank failures.
bills.plain_summary: This bill requires federal banking regulators to expand who participates in planning for the orderly wind-down of large, failing banks. It would bring in private-sector financial experts alongside government officials in the resolution planning process. The bill has passed committee review and is awaiting a full House vote, having been placed on the Union Calendar.

impacts.text: Critics warn private-sector participants in bank resolution planning could face conflicts of interest, potentially benefiting certain Wall Street firms over ordinary depositors.
Broader expert involvement could lead to better, faster plans for handling bank failures, potentially protecting taxpayers and account holders from costly bailouts.
The bill shifts some oversight influence from career government regulators to outside financial industry experts, changing who shapes bank failure policy.

bills.who_affected: Bank customers and depositors, large financial institutions, federal banking regulators, taxpayers, Wall Street firms
bills.ticker_alert: 🏦 House bill would add private experts to big-bank failure planning process

bills.title: Community Bank Deposit Access Act of 2025
bills.plain_title: Helping Community Banks Access Federal Deposit Insurance
bills.deck: This bill makes it easier for small community banks to offer federally insured deposits to customers.
bills.plain_summary: The Community Bank Deposit Access Act of 2025 (HR 5317) aims to improve how community banks can access and offer deposit programs backed by federal insurance, potentially allowing them to hold more insured deposits for their customers. The bill has been placed on the Union Calendar, meaning it has passed committee review and is awaiting a full House vote. It targets the competitive disadvantage smaller community banks face compared to larger financial institutions when it comes to deposit insurance programs.

impacts.text: Critics warn it could increase taxpayer exposure to risk if community banks expand insured deposits beyond their financial capacity to manage safely.
Customers at small community banks could gain access to higher levels of federally insured deposit protection, making their savings safer.
The bill shifts how deposit insurance access is structured for community banks, but the full competitive effects on large versus small banks remain debated.

bills.who_affected: Community bank customers, small and community bank owners, rural Americans who rely on local banks, small business depositors
bills.ticker_alert: 🏦 Community Bank Bill advances to House floor vote — depositor protections at stake

bills.title: Community Bank Regulatory Tailoring Act
bills.plain_title: Reduce Red Tape for Small Community Banks
bills.deck: This bill would ease federal regulations on small community banks to reduce their compliance costs.
bills.plain_summary: The Community Bank Regulatory Tailoring Act would adjust federal banking rules to be less burdensome on smaller community banks, recognizing that one-size-fits-all regulations designed for giant banks can be costly for local institutions. The bill aims to scale regulatory requirements based on a bank's size and risk level. It has passed committee review and is awaiting a full House vote, having been placed on the Union Calendar.

impacts.text: Looser oversight of community banks could increase risk of bank failures, potentially harming local depositors and small business borrowers.
Community banks may lower fees and offer more loans to local residents and small businesses if compliance costs are reduced.
Debate continues over whether current regulations are genuinely excessive for small banks or serve as necessary consumer protections.

bills.who_affected: Community bank customers, small business owners, rural residents, community bank employees, local homebuyers seeking mortgages
bills.ticker_alert: 🏦 House bill would cut red tape for local community banks nationwide

bills.title: Community Investment and Prosperity Act
bills.plain_title: Tax Credits for Community Development Financial Institutions
bills.deck: Creates tax incentives to encourage investment in underserved and low-income communities through community lenders.
bills.plain_summary: This bill would establish new tax credits to attract private investment into Community Development Financial Institutions (CDFIs), which are specialized lenders that serve low-income and underserved areas. The goal is to boost economic activity in communities that traditional banks often overlook. The bill has been referred to the House Committee on Financial Services, meaning it is in the early stages of the legislative process.

impacts.text: Tax credits reduce federal revenue, potentially adding to the deficit or offsetting funding from other programs
Low-income residents in underserved communities could gain greater access to affordable loans, small business funding, and homeownership opportunities
Effectiveness depends on whether private investors actually redirect capital to targeted communities or claim credits for investments they would have made anyway

bills.who_affected: Low-income residents, small business owners in underserved areas, community development lenders, private investors, rural communities, minority communities
bills.ticker_alert: 💰 Bill would create new tax credits to boost lending in underserved communities

bills.title: FDIC Board Accountability Act
bills.plain_title: Reform How FDIC Board Members Are Held Accountable
bills.deck: This bill changes rules for removing FDIC board members and requires more transparency from the agency.
bills.plain_summary: The FDIC Board Accountability Act would change the rules around how members of the Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation's board can be removed and would require more congressional oversight of the agency. The FDIC is the government body that insures American bank deposits up to $250,000. The bill has been placed on the Union Calendar, meaning it has passed committee review and is eligible for a House floor vote.

impacts.text: Could make the FDIC more politically vulnerable if board removal becomes easier, potentially weakening independent banking oversight
Increases accountability and transparency at a powerful financial regulator that protects everyday Americans' bank deposits
Shifts the balance of power between Congress and independent regulatory agencies, a long-debated constitutional question

bills.who_affected: Bank depositors, commercial banks, community banks, FDIC employees, financial regulators, Congress
bills.ticker_alert: 🏦 House bill would reform oversight rules for the agency protecting your bank deposits

bills.title: Bank-Fintech Partnership Enhancement Act
bills.plain_title: Rules for Banks Partnering with Financial Technology Companies
bills.deck: Sets federal standards for how traditional banks can work with fintech companies to offer financial products.
bills.plain_summary: This bill creates clearer rules for partnerships between traditional banks and financial technology (fintech) companies, such as when a bank teams up with an app to offer loans or savings accounts. It aims to define who is responsible for following banking laws when banks and fintechs collaborate. The bill has passed committee and is awaiting a vote by the full House of Representatives.

impacts.text: Critics warn looser fintech-bank partnerships could expose consumers to less oversight, increasing risks of predatory lending or data misuse.
More Americans, including those in underserved communities, could gain access to innovative financial products through expanded bank-fintech partnerships.
The bill shifts some regulatory responsibility between banks and their fintech partners, which may change how disputes over fees or errors are resolved.

bills.who_affected: Bank customers, fintech app users, small community banks, fintech startups, underbanked Americans, financial regulators
bills.ticker_alert: 🏦 Congress moves to set rules for bank-fintech company partnerships nationwide

bills.title: Keeping Gun Dealers Honest Act of 2025
bills.plain_title: Gun Dealer Compliance and Accountability Act
bills.deck: This bill would increase penalties and oversight for licensed gun dealers who break federal firearms laws.
bills.plain_summary: The Keeping Gun Dealers Honest Act of 2025 would strengthen enforcement against federally licensed firearms dealers who violate existing gun sale rules, such as failing to conduct background checks or falsifying records. It was introduced in the U.S. Senate and has been referred to the Senate Judiciary Committee, meaning it has not yet received a vote. The bill aims to close gaps in how the ATF monitors and penalizes gun dealers who repeatedly break the law.

impacts.text: Small, independent gun shop owners could face harsher penalties or license revocation for administrative errors that critics say are minor or unintentional.
Stricter dealer accountability could reduce illegal gun sales and straw purchases, potentially keeping more firearms out of criminal hands.
The bill's effectiveness depends heavily on ATF enforcement resources and how broadly 'violations' are defined and prosecuted in practice.

bills.who_affected: Licensed gun dealers, gun buyers, gun shop employees, law enforcement agencies, ATF inspectors, Second Amendment advocacy groups
bills.ticker_alert: 🔫 Senate bill targets gun dealers who break federal firearms sales laws

bills.title: Community Bank LIFT Act
bills.plain_title: Tax Relief for Small Community Banks
bills.deck: This bill lets small community banks deduct more loan losses from their taxes, freeing up money to lend locally.
bills.plain_summary: The Community Bank LIFT Act would allow small community banks to use a more favorable accounting method for deducting loan losses on their taxes, reducing their tax burden and potentially freeing up more capital for local lending. The bill targets banks with under $10 billion in assets, aiming to help them compete with larger financial institutions. It has been placed on the Union Calendar, meaning it is eligible for a full House floor vote but has not yet passed.

impacts.text: Reduces federal tax revenue from community banks, potentially shifting the tax burden to other taxpayers or cutting funding for public programs.
Community banks could lend more to small businesses and rural borrowers who are often underserved by large national banks.
Critics debate whether tax savings would actually flow to consumers as better loan rates or simply boost bank profits and shareholder returns.

bills.who_affected: Community bank customers, small business owners, rural residents, community bank shareholders and employees, federal taxpayers
bills.ticker_alert: 🏦 Community Bank LIFT Act would cut taxes on small local banks to boost lending

bills.title: Least Cost Exception Act
bills.plain_title: Allow Cheaper Power Grid Alternatives for Federal Projects
bills.deck: This bill lets federal agencies choose lower-cost alternatives when upgrading or expanding the electrical grid.
bills.plain_summary: The Least Cost Exception Act would allow federal agencies to select the most cost-effective option when making decisions about electricity transmission infrastructure, even if it differs from a standard default requirement. The bill has been placed on the Union Calendar, meaning it has passed committee review and is awaiting a full House vote. It aims to reduce costs associated with federal energy transmission projects by giving agencies more flexibility in their procurement choices.

impacts.text: Critics worry that prioritizing lowest cost could lead to selection of less reliable or less environmentally friendly grid infrastructure, potentially affecting long-term grid stability.
Taxpayers could save money if federal agencies are allowed to choose cheaper transmission options rather than being locked into more expensive default requirements.
The bill shifts decision-making authority to agency administrators, which could lead to inconsistent standards across different federal energy projects depending on interpretation.

bills.who_affected: Electric utility ratepayers, federal taxpayers, energy transmission companies, rural electric cooperatives, federal energy regulators
bills.ticker_alert: ⚡ House bill would let federal agencies pick cheaper power grid options

bills.title: Employee Paycheck and Small Business Protection Act
bills.plain_title: Paycheck and Small Business Protection from Regulation
bills.deck: This bill would limit certain federal regulatory actions affecting employee pay and small business operations.
bills.plain_summary: The Employee Paycheck and Small Business Protection Act appears designed to restrict or delay certain federal agency rules that could impact how workers are paid and how small businesses operate. The bill was referred to the House Committee on Financial Services and the Committee on Rules, meaning it is still in early stages and has not been debated or voted on. Without full bill text available, the exact mechanisms remain unclear, but the title suggests it targets regulations seen as burdensome to small employers and workers' take-home pay.

impacts.text: Critics argue limiting financial regulations could reduce worker protections against wage theft or predatory lending practices.
Supporters say reducing regulatory burdens on small businesses could lower compliance costs and help preserve jobs and paychecks.
The bill's actual effect depends heavily on which specific regulations it targets, a detail requiring full bill text to assess.

bills.who_affected: Small business owners, hourly and salaried employees, payroll administrators, federal regulatory agencies
bills.ticker_alert: 💼 Bill seeks to shield small businesses and paychecks from certain federal rules

bills.title: TAILOR Act of 2025
bills.plain_title: Financial Regulators Must Tailor Rules to Bank Size
bills.deck: Requires federal financial regulators to customize rules based on the size and risk level of each financial institution.
bills.plain_summary: The TAILOR Act of 2025 would require federal financial regulators — like the FDIC, Federal Reserve, and OCC — to consider the size, complexity, and risk profile of banks and credit unions before imposing regulations, rather than applying one-size-fits-all rules. The idea is that small community banks shouldn't face the same regulatory burden as giant Wall Street institutions. The bill has been placed on the Union Calendar, meaning it has cleared committee and is awaiting a full House vote.

impacts.text: Critics warn this could weaken consumer protections by allowing regulators to reduce oversight of mid-size banks that still pose significant risks to depositors.
Community banks and credit unions could face less regulatory paperwork, potentially freeing up resources to offer more loans to small businesses and local borrowers.
The bill shifts regulatory discretion to agency officials, meaning its real-world impact depends heavily on how those agencies choose to apply the new tailoring standard.

bills.who_affected: Community bank customers, small business borrowers, credit union members, bank shareholders, federal financial regulators, Wall Street banks
bills.ticker_alert: 🏦 House bill would require regulators to customize rules by bank size

bills.title: New BANK Act of 2025
bills.plain_title: New Rules for Bank Accounts at Federal Reserve
bills.deck: This bill would allow everyday Americans to open bank accounts directly at the Federal Reserve.
bills.plain_summary: The New BANK Act of 2025 would require the Federal Reserve to offer basic bank accounts directly to all U.S. residents, bypassing traditional commercial banks. This could give millions of unbanked Americans access to a free, federally backed account. The bill has been placed on the Union Calendar, meaning it is eligible for full House consideration.

impacts.text: Private banks could lose customers and fee revenue, potentially reducing lending and financial services in local communities.
Roughly 5-6 million unbanked American households could gain free, safe access to basic banking and direct deposit services.
The Federal Reserve would take on a new retail banking role it has never performed, raising debate about government's proper role in finance.

bills.who_affected: Unbanked and underbanked Americans, low-income households, commercial and community banks, credit unions, Federal Reserve staff
bills.ticker_alert: 🏦 Bill would let Americans bank directly with the Federal Reserve

bills.title: Bringing the Discount Window into the 21st Century Act
bills.plain_title: Modernizing the Federal Reserve's Emergency Bank Lending Program
bills.deck: This bill updates rules for how banks can borrow emergency funds directly from the Federal Reserve.
bills.plain_summary: This bill reforms the Federal Reserve's 'discount window,' which is the system banks use to borrow emergency funds from the Fed during financial stress. It aims to modernize the process, reduce the stigma banks feel about using it, and make the system work better in a crisis. The bill passed the House and is now awaiting action in the Senate Banking Committee.

impacts.text: Critics worry easier Fed lending could encourage banks to take excessive risks, knowing a safety net is readily available.
A more effective emergency lending system could prevent bank runs and financial crises that harm everyday Americans' savings and jobs.
The bill would increase transparency and reporting requirements around discount window use, which some see as helpful and others see as burdensome.

bills.who_affected: Bank customers, small and regional bank depositors, taxpayers, financial industry workers, investors
bills.ticker_alert: 🏦 House passes bill to modernize Fed's emergency bank lending system

bills.title: Providing for consideration of the bill (H.R. 4690) to amend the Energy Conservation and Production Act to repeal certain Federal building energy efficiency performance standards, and for other purposes; providing for consideration of the resolution (H. Res. 1182) expressing support for rural communities across the United States as stewards of the environment, major suppliers of United States energy resources, critical providers of food production and manufacturing capacity, and drivers of national economic stability, and recognizing the work of the House of Representatives in the 119th Congress in support of those vital communities; providing for consideration of the bill (H.R. 1897) to amend the Endangered Species Act of 1973 to optimize conservation through resource prioritization, incentivize wildlife conservation on private lands, provide for greater incentives to recover listed species, create greater transparency and accountability in recovering listed species, streamline the permitting process, eliminate barriers to conservation, and restore congressional intent; and providing for consideration of the bill (H.R. 5587) to amend the Geothermal Steam Act of 1970 to waive the requirement for a Federal drilling permit for certain activities, to exempt certain activities from the requirements of the National Environmental Policy Act of 1969, and for other purposes.
bills.plain_title: Providing for consideration of the bill (H.R. 4690) to amend the Energy Conservation and Production Act to repeal certain Federal building energy efficiency performance standards, and for other purposes; providing for consideration of the resolution (H. Res. 1182) expressing support for rural communities across the United States as stewards of the environment, major suppliers of United States energy resources, critical providers of food production and manufacturing capacity, and drivers of national economic stability, and recognizing the work of the House of Representatives in the 119th Congress in support of those vital communities; providing for consideration of the bill (H.R. 1897) to amend the Endangered Species Act of 1973 to optimize conservation through resource prioritization, incentivize wildlife conservation on private lands, provide for greater incentives to recover listed species, create greater transparency and accountability in recovering listed species, streamline the permitting process, eliminate barriers to conservation, and restore congressional intent; and providing for consideration of the bill (H.R. 5587) to amend the Geothermal Steam Act of 1970 to waive the requirement for a Federal drilling permit for certain activities, to exempt certain activities from the requirements of the National Environmental Policy Act of 1969, and for other purposes.
bills.deck: Placed on the House Calendar, Calendar No. 72.
bills.plain_summary: Providing for consideration of the bill (H.R. 4690) to amend the Energy Conservation and Production Act to repeal certain Federal building energy efficiency performance standards, and for other purposes; providing for consideration of the resolution (H. Res. 1182) expressing support for rural communities across the United States as stewards of the environment, major suppliers of United States energy resources, critical providers of food production and manufacturing capacity, and drivers of national economic stability, and recognizing the work of the House of Representatives in the 119th Congress in support of those vital communities; providing for consideration of the bill (H.R. 1897) to amend the Endangered Species Act of 1973 to optimize conservation through resource prioritization, incentivize wildlife conservation on private lands, provide for greater incentives to recover listed species, create greater transparency and accountability in recovering listed species, streamline the permitting process, eliminate barriers to conservation, and restore congressional intent; and providing for consideration of the bill (H.R. 5587) to amend the Geothermal Steam Act of 1970 to waive the requirement for a Federal drilling permit for certain activities, to exempt certain activities from the requirements of the National Environmental Policy Act of 1969, and for other purposes.. bills.title: End Polluter Welfare for Enhanced Oil Recovery Act of 2026
bills.plain_title: Remove Tax Breaks for Enhanced Oil Recovery
bills.deck: This bill eliminates federal tax credits that help oil companies extract hard-to-reach petroleum using special techniques.
bills.plain_summary: This bill would end a longstanding federal tax credit that oil companies use to offset costs of 'enhanced oil recovery' — techniques like injecting carbon dioxide or steam into old wells to extract more oil. Supporters say these subsidies waste taxpayer money on a profitable fossil fuel industry. The bill has been referred to the House Committee on Ways and Means, the first step in the legislative process.

impacts.text: Oil companies and workers in enhanced recovery operations could face higher costs and potential job losses in oil-producing states like Texas, Wyoming, and North Dakota.
Ending the tax credit could save the federal government hundreds of millions of dollars annually, potentially reducing the deficit or freeing funds for other priorities.
Whether eliminating this credit meaningfully reduces oil production or greenhouse gas emissions is disputed among energy economists and industry analysts.

bills.who_affected: Oil and gas companies, oil field workers, residents of oil-producing states, taxpayers, energy investors, rural communities near oil operations
bills.ticker_alert: ⚡ Bill would cut oil company tax breaks worth hundreds of millions annually

bills.title: America’s Living Library Act
bills.plain_title: National Living Library Network Creation Act
bills.deck: This bill would create a national network of living libraries preserving plant and animal genetic diversity across America.
bills.plain_summary: The America's Living Library Act proposes establishing a coordinated national network of 'living libraries' — physical collections of living plants, animals, and genetic material — to preserve biodiversity and support scientific research. The bill was referred to multiple House committees including Natural Resources, Agriculture, House Administration, and Energy and Commerce, meaning it faces review from several different panels before it could advance. It has not yet been voted on and is in early stages of the legislative process.

impacts.text: Establishing and maintaining multiple living library facilities could require significant federal spending and ongoing taxpayer-funded operational costs.
Americans would benefit from preserved biodiversity resources that support food security, medical research, and ecological resilience against climate change and disease.
The bill's referral to four separate committees reflects overlapping jurisdictions, making its path to passage complex and uncertain regardless of political support.

bills.who_affected: Farmers and agricultural researchers, conservationists and wildlife biologists, federal land managers, university researchers, rural communities near designated sites, taxpayers
bills.ticker_alert: 🌿 New bill proposes national network of living biodiversity libraries across America

bills.title: Providing for the expulsion of Representative Cory Mills from the United States House of Representatives.
bills.plain_title: Resolution to Expel Representative Cory Mills from Congress
bills.deck: This resolution would remove Florida Congressman Cory Mills from the House of Representatives.
bills.plain_summary: This resolution calls for the expulsion of Republican Representative Cory Mills of Florida from the U.S. House of Representatives. Expulsion is the most severe punishment Congress can impose on one of its own members, requiring a two-thirds supermajority vote to succeed. The resolution has been referred to the House Ethics Committee, which would need to investigate and make a recommendation before any full House vote.

impacts.text: Florida's 7th congressional district would temporarily lose its representation in Congress until a special election could be held.
If allegations against Mills are serious and substantiated, expulsion would uphold accountability standards and public trust in Congress.
Expulsion resolutions are extremely rare and politically charged; the two-thirds threshold makes success unlikely without broad bipartisan support.

bills.who_affected: Florida 7th district constituents, House Republican members, House Ethics Committee, voters in Florida
bills.ticker_alert: ⚖️ House resolution seeks to expel Florida Rep. Cory Mills from Congress

bills.title: Electing Members to certain standing committees of the House of Representatives.
bills.plain_title: House Committee Membership Assignments Resolution
bills.deck: This resolution formally assigns specific House members to serve on standing committees.
bills.plain_summary: This resolution assigns members of the House of Representatives to seats on specific standing committees, which are the groups that review and shape legislation before it reaches the full House floor. It is a routine procedural action typically introduced by party leadership at the start of a new Congress or when vacancies arise. The resolution has already passed, as the motion to reconsider was laid on the table without objection.

impacts.text: Partisan reshuffling of committees can concentrate power and limit minority party influence over legislation.
Properly staffed committees allow Congress to conduct oversight and move legislation efficiently, benefiting all Americans.
Committee assignments reflect party priorities and seniority norms, which critics and supporters debate in terms of fairness and effectiveness.

bills.who_affected: Members of Congress, congressional staff, lobbyists and advocacy groups, voters whose representatives gain or lose committee seats
bills.ticker_alert: 🏛️ House assigns members to standing committees in routine procedural vote

bills.title: Native American Housing Assistance and Self-Determination Modernization Act of 2026
bills.plain_title: Modernizing Housing Aid for Native American Tribes
bills.deck: Updates federal housing assistance rules to give Native American tribes more control over their housing programs.
bills.plain_summary: This bill updates a 1996 law that governs how federal housing funds are given to Native American tribes. It aims to reduce red tape, increase tribal control over housing decisions, and modernize outdated rules to better address housing shortages on reservations. The bill has been introduced in the Senate and referred to the Committee on Indian Affairs for review.

impacts.text: Critics worry relaxed federal oversight could reduce accountability for how housing funds are spent on reservations.
Tribes would gain greater flexibility to design housing programs tailored to their specific community needs and cultures.
Changes to federal funding formulas could shift how much individual tribes receive, benefiting some while reducing allocations for others.

bills.who_affected: Native American tribal members, tribal housing authorities, reservation residents, federal housing agencies, tribal governments
bills.ticker_alert: 🏠 Senate bill seeks to modernize housing aid for Native American tribes

bills.title: TALENTS Act
bills.plain_title: Use Government Workers to Boost US Tech Talent
bills.deck: This bill would let federal agencies place government employees in private tech companies to share AI and emerging tech skills.
bills.plain_summary: The TALENTS Act would create a program allowing federal employees to temporarily work at private technology companies focused on artificial intelligence and other advanced technologies, and vice versa, so both sectors can share expertise. The goal is to help the U.S. government stay competitive in cutting-edge technology by building a workforce skilled in AI, cybersecurity, and related fields. The bill was introduced in the Senate and has been referred to the Committee on Homeland Security and Governmental Affairs, meaning it has not yet been voted on.

impacts.text: Critics worry that rotating federal workers into private tech firms could create conflicts of interest or allow companies to gain unfair influence over government policy and contracts.
American taxpayers could benefit from a more technologically capable federal government better equipped to defend against cyberattacks and deploy modern digital services.
The program would primarily affect a limited number of federal employees and tech sector workers selected for exchanges, leaving most government workers unaffected.

bills.who_affected: Federal government employees, private technology company workers, AI researchers, federal contractors, taxpayers who rely on government digital services
bills.ticker_alert: 🔄 Senate bill proposes swapping federal workers with private tech firms to boost AI skills

bills.title: United States Legal Gold and Mining Partnership Act
bills.plain_title: U.S. Gold Mining International Partnership Act
bills.deck: This bill would establish partnerships between U.S. companies and foreign nations to develop gold mining operations abroad.
bills.plain_summary: The United States Legal Gold and Mining Partnership Act (S 799) would create a framework for the U.S. government to support and facilitate partnerships between American mining companies and foreign countries to develop gold and mineral resources overseas. The bill aims to strengthen U.S. access to gold supplies and expand American mining industry interests internationally. It has been referred to the Senate Committee on Foreign Relations, where it must be reviewed before any further action.

impacts.text: U.S. taxpayer resources or government backing could be used to subsidize private mining companies operating in foreign countries, with profits going to corporations rather than the public.
American gold and mining companies could gain new business opportunities and jobs through government-supported international partnerships, potentially boosting domestic industry employment.
The bill would expand U.S. diplomatic and economic engagement in resource-rich nations, which supporters call strategic and critics call corporate favoritism.

bills.who_affected: American mining company employees, mining industry investors, U.S. diplomatic personnel, taxpayers, communities in foreign host nations
bills.ticker_alert: ⛏️ Senate bill seeks U.S.-backed gold mining deals with foreign nations

bills.title: United States Legal Gold and Mining Partnership Act
bills.plain_title: Federal Support for U.S. Gold Mining Industry Act
bills.deck: This bill would create a federal program to support and expand domestic gold mining operations in the United States.
bills.plain_summary: The United States Legal Gold and Mining Partnership Act would establish a federal partnership program to promote domestic gold and precious metal mining. The bill aims to boost American mining output, reduce reliance on foreign sources of gold, and support mining industry jobs. It has been placed on the Senate Legislative Calendar, meaning it is eligible for a full Senate vote.

impacts.text: Expanded mining operations could increase environmental risks including land disruption, water contamination, and habitat destruction near mining communities.
Could create or preserve well-paying mining and related jobs in rural states with significant gold deposits like Nevada, Alaska, and Colorado.
The bill would shift federal policy toward treating domestic gold mining as a national economic priority, changing how federal lands may be used.

bills.who_affected: Gold and precious metal miners, mining company shareholders, rural communities near mining sites, environmental advocates, federal land managers, American consumers of electronics and jewelry
bills.ticker_alert: ⛏️ Senate bill proposes federal partnership to boost U.S. gold mining industry

bills.title: RSF Terrorist Designation Act
bills.plain_title: Designate Sudan's RSF Militia as a Terrorist Organization
bills.deck: This bill would officially label Sudan's Rapid Support Forces militia as a foreign terrorist organization.
bills.plain_summary: This bill directs the U.S. government to designate the Rapid Support Forces (RSF), a Sudanese paramilitary group accused of mass atrocities in Sudan's ongoing civil war, as a Foreign Terrorist Organization. This designation would trigger legal consequences including sanctions, travel bans, and restrictions on anyone providing material support to the group. The bill was referred to the House Foreign Affairs and Judiciary Committees, where it awaits further consideration.

impacts.text: American aid organizations and NGOs operating in Sudan could face legal risks if any transactions inadvertently involve RSF-controlled areas or personnel.
The designation would strengthen U.S. tools to cut off the RSF's financial networks and hold its leaders accountable for alleged war crimes and ethnic cleansing.
Critics debate whether the designation would meaningfully pressure the RSF or complicate already difficult diplomatic and humanitarian efforts in Sudan.

bills.who_affected: Sudanese Americans, humanitarian aid workers in Sudan, U.S. foreign policy officials, human rights advocates, American businesses with Sudan ties
bills.ticker_alert: 🚨 Bill seeks to label Sudan's RSF militia a U.S.-designated terrorist organization

bills.title: Long-Term Good Neighbor Authority Act
bills.plain_title: Extending Forest Service Land Management Agreements
bills.deck: This bill allows the Forest Service to sign longer cooperative agreements with neighboring landowners for forest management.
bills.plain_summary: The Long-Term Good Neighbor Authority Act would extend the length of cooperative agreements between the U.S. Forest Service and state, tribal, and local governments or private landowners who border national forests. These 'Good Neighbor' agreements let neighboring parties help manage forest land, including thinning trees and reducing wildfire risk. The bill has passed through subcommittee hearings and is still working its way through Congress.

impacts.text: Critics worry longer agreements reduce federal oversight and could allow private interests to influence public land management decisions.
Longer agreements give states and local communities more stability to plan and fund multi-year forest health and wildfire prevention projects.
The bill shifts some forest management responsibility to non-federal partners, which supporters call efficient but critics see as a reduced federal role.

bills.who_affected: National forest neighbors, state forestry agencies, tribal governments, ranchers and farmers bordering federal land, wildfire-prone rural communities, timber industry workers
bills.ticker_alert: 🌲 Bill would let Forest Service sign longer land management deals with neighbors

bills.title: Survivor Justice Tax Prevention Act
bills.plain_title: Tax Relief for Sexual Abuse and Trafficking Survivors
bills.deck: Prevents the IRS from taxing lawsuit settlements received by survivors of sexual abuse or human trafficking.
bills.plain_summary: This bill would make financial settlements received by survivors of sexual abuse, assault, or human trafficking fully tax-free, even when the money is paid by a third party like an employer or institution. Under current law, some survivor compensation can be subject to federal income tax depending on how the settlement is structured. The bill has been placed on the Union Calendar, meaning it has cleared committee and is awaiting a full House vote.

impacts.text: Reduces federal tax revenue by exempting certain settlement payments, potentially a small but real budget cost
Survivors of abuse and trafficking keep their full settlement amount rather than losing a portion to federal income taxes
The bill's benefit is tied to who pays the settlement — critics may debate whether third-party payers like corporations deserve the same tax treatment

bills.who_affected: Sexual abuse survivors, human trafficking victims, personal injury attorneys, employers and institutions that settle abuse claims, IRS
bills.ticker_alert: 💰 Bill would make abuse and trafficking survivor settlements fully tax-free

bills.title: Impacts and Outcomes for Health Career Training Act
bills.plain_title: Health Career Training Program Transparency and Outcomes Act
bills.deck: Requires health career training programs to report student outcome data to improve accountability and transparency.
bills.plain_summary: This bill would require health career training programs to track and report data on student outcomes, such as job placement rates and earnings after graduation. The goal is to help prospective students make better-informed decisions about which programs are worth their time and money. The bill has been referred to the House Committee on Ways and Means, meaning it is in the early stages of the legislative process.

impacts.text: Smaller or community-based health training programs may face costly new reporting burdens that strain limited administrative resources.
Prospective students can compare real job placement and earnings data before enrolling, reducing risk of wasted tuition on low-value programs.
The quality and comparability of reported outcome data will depend heavily on how the federal government standardizes and enforces the reporting requirements.

bills.who_affected: Health career training students, vocational and trade school administrators, community college health programs, healthcare employers, federal education regulators
bills.ticker_alert: 📊 New bill would require health training schools to publish job placement and earnings data

bills.title: World War II Women's Memorial Location Act
bills.plain_title: WWII Women's Memorial New Location Bill
bills.deck: This bill would allow a permanent memorial honoring women who served in World War II to be built in a specific location in Washington, D.C.
bills.plain_summary: This bill would designate an approved location in Washington, D.C. for a memorial dedicated to the women who served the United States during World War II. It removes a previous restriction that limited where this memorial could be built, giving organizers more flexibility to find a fitting site. The bill has had a hearing in the Senate Energy and Natural Resources Subcommittee on National Parks but has not yet passed into law.

impacts.text: Could require additional federal review processes and use of limited National Mall space, potentially displacing other planned memorials or green areas.
Would honor the roughly 400,000 women who served in WWII, giving veterans and their families a permanent, visible place of recognition and remembrance.
The specific final location of the memorial has not been determined, meaning debates over placement, design, and funding may continue after passage.

bills.who_affected: WWII women veterans and their descendants, veterans organizations, Washington D.C. residents and tourists, National Park Service
bills.ticker_alert: 🏛️ Senate bill seeks new D.C. location for WWII Women's Memorial

bills.title: Advance Global Health Act
bills.plain_title: U.S. Global Health Program Reform and Authorization Act
bills.deck: Reorganizes and strengthens how the U.S. government funds and coordinates global health programs abroad.
bills.plain_summary: The Advance Global Health Act would update and authorize U.S. government efforts to fight diseases, improve health systems, and respond to health crises in other countries. It aims to make American global health spending more strategic and accountable. The bill passed its committee vote overwhelmingly, 41 to 2, and now awaits a full House vote.

impacts.text: Critics argue it continues directing billions in taxpayer dollars to foreign health programs rather than domestic needs.
Stronger global disease prevention could reduce the risk of future pandemics reaching American shores, protecting U.S. public health.
The bill restructures existing global health bureaucracy, which may shift priorities and funding allocations among current international programs.

bills.who_affected: U.S. foreign aid workers, global health NGOs receiving federal grants, State Department and USAID employees, American pharmaceutical and public health contractors, taxpayers
bills.ticker_alert: 🌍 House panel advances bill to overhaul U.S. global health foreign aid programs

bills.title: Rejecting the Erasure of Afghan Women and Girls Act
bills.plain_title: U.S. Policy Against Taliban Oppression of Afghan Women
bills.deck: This bill directs the U.S. government to formally oppose Taliban policies that erase Afghan women and girls from public life.
bills.plain_summary: This bill requires the U.S. government to officially reject Taliban policies that ban Afghan women and girls from education, work, and public spaces. It directs U.S. diplomats and agencies to treat these Taliban restrictions as a form of gender-based persecution and to make opposing them a priority in foreign policy. The bill passed committee with overwhelming bipartisan support (44-2) and is awaiting a full House vote.

impacts.text: Critics argue the bill adds diplomatic pressure without enforcement tools, potentially straining negotiations needed for other U.S. interests in the region.
Human rights advocates say the bill strengthens U.S. moral authority and gives diplomats a clearer mandate to push back against Taliban gender oppression.
The bill is largely symbolic in its direct reach, directing policy statements and diplomatic posture rather than imposing new sanctions or funding measures.

bills.who_affected: U.S. foreign policy officials and diplomats, Afghan-American communities, human rights organizations, women's advocacy groups, NGOs operating in Afghanistan
bills.ticker_alert: 🌐 Congress moves to formally oppose Taliban's ban on Afghan women's rights

bills.title: Venezuela Democratic Transition Strategy Act
bills.plain_title: US Strategy Plan for Venezuela's Democratic Transition
bills.deck: Requires the U.S. government to develop a formal strategy supporting a democratic transition in Venezuela.
bills.plain_summary: This bill directs the State Department and other agencies to create a comprehensive strategy for supporting democracy in Venezuela, including coordinating international pressure on the Maduro government and assisting Venezuelan civil society. It was introduced in the House and passed committee with strong bipartisan support, 41-5. The bill reflects ongoing U.S. concerns about authoritarian rule and humanitarian crisis in Venezuela.

impacts.text: Could draw the U.S. into deeper foreign entanglements, potentially costing taxpayer dollars on foreign policy initiatives with uncertain outcomes.
A stable, democratic Venezuela could reduce migration pressure to the U.S. and restore a regional trade and energy partner.
The strategy is primarily a planning requirement and does not by itself authorize military action or major new spending, leaving real impact uncertain.

bills.who_affected: Venezuelan-American communities, U.S. foreign policy and diplomatic staff, American energy companies with Venezuela interests, U.S. border and immigration officials
bills.ticker_alert: 🌎 Congress pushes formal U.S. strategy to restore democracy in Venezuela

bills.title: No Funding to Honor Crime Scenes Act
bills.plain_title: Ban Federal Funds for Memorials at Mass Shooting Sites
bills.deck: This bill would prohibit federal money from being used to create memorials or monuments at locations where mass shootings occurred.
bills.plain_summary: The 'No Funding to Honor Crime Scenes Act' would ban the use of federal funds to build, maintain, or support any memorial or monument at a site where a mass shooting took place. The bill was referred to the Senate Committee on Energy and Natural Resources, suggesting it may apply particularly to federally managed lands or national parks. It has not yet moved beyond the committee stage and would need to pass both chambers of Congress before becoming law.

impacts.text: Families and communities affected by mass shootings may be denied federal support to memorialize victims at the locations where tragedies occurred.
Supporters argue this prevents taxpayer dollars from being used to permanently mark crime scenes, which some believe could glorify or institutionalize sites of violence.
The bill raises unresolved questions about how 'mass shooting' is defined and whether existing memorials on federal land would be affected or removed.

bills.who_affected: Mass shooting survivors, victims' families, local communities near shooting sites, federal land managers, taxpayers, memorial advocates
bills.ticker_alert: 🚫 Senate bill would ban federal funds for mass shooting site memorials

bills.title: Special Operations Forces Concealed Carry Act
bills.plain_title: Concealed Carry Rights for Special Operations Veterans
bills.deck: This bill would allow retired special operations forces members to carry concealed firearms nationwide.
bills.plain_summary: HR 8332 would grant current and former U.S. Special Operations Forces members — such as Navy SEALs and Army Rangers — the right to carry concealed firearms in all 50 states, similar to rights already extended to law enforcement officers under existing federal law. The bill was referred to the House Committees on Judiciary, Armed Services, and Veterans' Affairs for review. It has not yet been voted on and remains in the early committee stage.

impacts.text: Critics argue it creates a privileged class of gun carriers, bypassing state concealed carry laws and local public safety decisions.
Supporters say it recognizes the elite training of special operations veterans and helps them protect themselves and others after service.
The bill raises unresolved questions about how 'special operations forces' would be defined and verified, which could affect implementation.

bills.who_affected: Active and retired special operations military personnel, state law enforcement agencies, gun rights advocates, gun control advocates, veterans organizations
bills.ticker_alert: 🔫 Bill proposes nationwide concealed carry rights for special operations veterans
