bills.title: A resolution designating the week beginning February 2, 2026, as "National Tribal Colleges and Universities Week".
bills.plain_title: National Tribal Colleges and Universities Awareness Week
bills.deck: This resolution officially designates one week in February 2026 to honor tribal colleges and universities across America.
bills.plain_summary: This Senate resolution designates the week of February 2, 2026, as 'National Tribal Colleges and Universities Week' to recognize the importance of tribal colleges and universities (TCUs) in serving Native American communities. It is a symbolic measure with no funding or legal mandates attached. The resolution passed the Senate unanimously by consent, meaning no formal vote was needed.

impacts.text: The resolution is purely symbolic and allocates no funding or resources to address the chronic underfunding many tribal colleges face.
Raises national awareness of tribal colleges and universities, potentially boosting public support, enrollment interest, and future legislative attention for these institutions.
Symbolic resolutions like this have no binding legal effect but are sometimes used as stepping stones toward more substantive legislation.

bills.who_affected: Native American and Alaska Native students, tribal college faculty and staff, tribal nations, Indigenous communities, prospective students considering tribal colleges
bills.ticker_alert: 🎓 Senate unanimously honors tribal colleges with official awareness week designation

bills.title: Golden Thirteen Congressional Gold Medal Act
bills.plain_title: Gold Medal Honor for First Black Navy Officers
bills.deck: Awards the Congressional Gold Medal to the Golden Thirteen, the first Black officers commissioned in the U.S. Navy.
bills.plain_summary: This bill proposes awarding the Congressional Gold Medal — Congress's highest civilian honor — to the Golden Thirteen, the group of 13 African American men who became the first Black officers commissioned in the U.S. Navy in 1944. The bill recognizes their historic role in breaking racial barriers in the military during World War II. It has been referred to the House Financial Services and House Administration committees for review.

impacts.text: Minimal fiscal cost to produce the medal, but critics note symbolic honors don't address ongoing racial disparities in the military.
Formally recognizes a historically overlooked milestone in civil rights and military history, preserving it in the national record.
The medal is largely ceremonial with no direct policy changes; its broader cultural significance is debated.

bills.who_affected: African American veterans and their descendants, U.S. Navy personnel, historians, civil rights advocates, World War II commemorative communities
bills.ticker_alert: 🏅 Congress moves to honor first Black U.S. Navy officers with Gold Medal

bills.title: A resolution condemning the Government of Iran's state-sponsored persecution of the Baha'i minority and its continued violation of the International Covenants on Human Rights.
bills.plain_title: Senate Condemnation of Iran's Persecution of Baha'i People
bills.deck: This resolution formally condemns Iran's government for persecuting its Baha'i religious minority and violating international human rights law.
bills.plain_summary: This Senate resolution formally expresses the U.S. Senate's condemnation of Iran's ongoing persecution of the Baha'i faith community, including arrests, executions, and denial of education and employment. It calls attention to Iran's violations of international human rights agreements it has signed. The resolution has been referred to the Senate Foreign Relations Committee, where it awaits further consideration. Resolutions like this are symbolic statements and do not create new law or policy.

impacts.text: Could further strain U.S.-Iran diplomatic relations, potentially complicating nuclear talks or hostage negotiations.
Gives American Baha'i communities and human rights advocates official congressional backing for their advocacy efforts.
As a non-binding resolution, it carries moral weight but imposes no legal obligations on Iran or changes U.S. foreign policy.

bills.who_affected: American Baha'i community members, Iranian-Americans, human rights advocates, U.S. foreign policy professionals, diplomats
bills.ticker_alert: 🕊️ Senate condemns Iran's persecution of Baha'i religious minority in symbolic resolution

bills.title: National Cemetery Access Act
bills.plain_title: Improving Veterans' Access to National Cemetery Services
bills.deck: This bill aims to make it easier for veterans and families to access national cemetery burial benefits and services.
bills.plain_summary: The National Cemetery Access Act seeks to improve how veterans and their eligible family members can access burial services at national cemeteries, potentially addressing transportation, scheduling, or logistical barriers. The bill was referred to the House Subcommittee on Disability Assistance and Memorial Affairs, meaning it is in early stages of review. Without full bill text publicly available, the specifics of how access would be improved remain subject to the subcommittee's examination.

impacts.text: Implementation costs could draw from limited VA budget resources, potentially affecting other veteran services.
Veterans and families in rural or underserved areas may find it easier to access burial honors and cemetery services they are entitled to.
The bill's effectiveness depends heavily on how 'access' is defined and funded, which has not yet been publicly detailed.

bills.who_affected: Veterans, surviving spouses of veterans, eligible family members of deceased veterans, VA cemetery staff, rural communities near national cemeteries
bills.ticker_alert: 🪖 Bill introduced to improve veterans' access to national cemetery burial services

bills.title: A resolution designating September 2025 as "National Voting Rights Month".
bills.plain_title: Designate September 2025 as National Voting Rights Month
bills.deck: This resolution would officially recognize September 2025 as a month to raise awareness about Americans' voting rights.
bills.plain_summary: This is a Senate resolution that would designate September 2025 as 'National Voting Rights Month' to highlight the importance of voting rights in the United States. It is a symbolic measure, meaning it does not create new laws, spend money, or change any existing rules. The resolution has been referred to the Senate Judiciary Committee, where it awaits further review.

impacts.text: Critics may argue it uses congressional time on symbolic gestures rather than passing enforceable voting rights legislation.
Could raise public awareness about voting rights protections and encourage civic participation among eligible voters nationwide.
As a non-binding resolution, it carries no legal force and creates no new rights, obligations, or government programs.

bills.who_affected: Eligible voters, voting rights advocates, minority communities with historically restricted ballot access, civic education organizations
bills.ticker_alert: 🗳️ Senate resolution proposes September 2025 as National Voting Rights Month

bills.title: Gold Star and Surviving Spouse Career Services Act
bills.plain_title: Career Help for Military Widows and Gold Star Families
bills.deck: This bill would provide career counseling and job placement services to surviving spouses and families of fallen service members.
bills.plain_summary: The Gold Star and Surviving Spouse Career Services Act would direct the Department of Labor or a related agency to offer dedicated career services, including job training and employment assistance, to Gold Star family members and surviving spouses of deceased military personnel. The bill aims to help these individuals rebuild financial stability after losing a loved one in military service. It has been introduced in the House and has reached the subcommittee hearing stage, meaning it is still in early review.

impacts.text: Creating a dedicated program may require new federal funding or administrative overhead, adding to government expenditures with uncertain cost-effectiveness.
Surviving military spouses and Gold Star family members would gain access to targeted job training and employment support during a difficult financial transition.
The bill's reach is narrow, affecting a relatively small population, so broader debates focus on whether dedicated programs outperform existing veteran and workforce services.

bills.who_affected: Surviving spouses of fallen service members, Gold Star family members, Department of Labor workforce staff, federal budget planners
bills.ticker_alert: 🎖️ Bill proposes career services for Gold Star families and military surviving spouses

bills.title: Credit Union Board Modernization Act
bills.plain_title: Allow Credit Unions to Meet Less Often
bills.deck: This bill lets credit union boards hold fewer required meetings each year.
bills.plain_summary: The Credit Union Board Modernization Act would reduce how often federally chartered credit union boards of directors are required to meet, giving them more scheduling flexibility. The bill passed the House and was sent to the Senate Banking Committee for review. It aims to reduce administrative burdens on volunteer board members who run these nonprofit financial institutions.

impacts.text: Less frequent board meetings could reduce oversight of credit union finances, potentially increasing risk of mismanagement going undetected.
Volunteer board members face less scheduling pressure, making it easier to recruit qualified people to govern community credit unions.
Credit unions would still set their own meeting schedules; this bill simply removes a federal minimum frequency requirement.

bills.who_affected: Credit union members, credit union volunteer board directors, federally chartered credit union staff, community banking customers
bills.ticker_alert: 📋 Bill would let credit union boards skip some required annual meetings

bills.deck: Requires the government to consult with private businesses when using emergency industrial powers.
bills.plain_summary: This bill requires federal officials to conduct outreach to private sector companies before invoking the Defense Production Act, a law that gives the government broad powers to direct industries during national emergencies. It aims to ensure businesses have input before the government mandates production priorities or allocates resources. The bill has been referred to the House Committee on Financial Services and has not yet been voted on.

impacts.text: Adding a consultation requirement could slow the government's ability to respond quickly in fast-moving national emergencies or supply chain crises.
Businesses would get advance notice and a voice before the government issues mandates, reducing economic disruption and legal uncertainty for manufacturers.
The bill defines a new procedural step but does not limit what the government can ultimately do under the Defense Production Act once outreach occurs.

bills.who_affected: Defense contractors, manufacturers, pharmaceutical companies, critical materials producers, small businesses in defense supply chains, federal emergency planners
bills.ticker_alert: 📋 New bill would require gov't to consult businesses before invoking emergency production powers

bills.title: DPA Advanced Procurement Act of 2026
bills.plain_title: Defense Production Act Advanced Buying Authority Bill
bills.deck: This bill would allow the government to buy defense materials earlier using Defense Production Act powers.
bills.plain_summary: The DPA Advanced Procurement Act of 2026 (HR 8136) would expand the government's authority under the Defense Production Act to make advance purchases of critical materials, components, and supplies needed for national defense before they are immediately needed. This approach is intended to strengthen supply chains and reduce shortages during emergencies or conflicts. The bill has been referred to the House Committee on Financial Services, meaning it is in an early stage of the legislative process.

impacts.text: Taxpayers could bear financial risk if pre-purchased materials become obsolete, are overpriced, or go unused due to changing defense needs.
American manufacturers in the defense supply chain could receive more stable, earlier contracts, supporting domestic jobs and industrial readiness.
The scope of what qualifies as a 'critical' defense material eligible for advance procurement remains subject to interpretation and agency discretion.

bills.who_affected: Defense contractors, domestic manufacturers, U.S. taxpayers, Pentagon procurement officials, defense supply chain workers
bills.ticker_alert: 🛡️ Bill would let Pentagon pre-buy defense supplies before shortages hit

bills.title: DPA Specialized Staffing Act
bills.plain_title: Hire Defense Production Act Specialists in Federal Agencies
bills.deck: Requires federal agencies to hire dedicated staff experts to manage Defense Production Act authorities.
bills.plain_summary: This bill would require certain federal agencies to employ specialized staff who are trained and focused on using the Defense Production Act (DPA), a law that lets the government direct private companies to produce critical goods during national emergencies. The goal is to make sure agencies have experts ready to quickly mobilize industrial production when crises arise, such as during pandemics or supply chain disruptions. The bill was referred to two House committees and has not yet advanced further.

impacts.text: Adds new federal personnel costs and bureaucratic layers that taxpayers must fund, even outside of emergencies.
Could speed up government response during crises like pandemics or shortages, getting critical supplies to Americans faster.
The effectiveness of specialized staffing depends heavily on how agencies implement the requirement and whether emergencies actually occur.

bills.who_affected: Federal agency employees, defense contractors, manufacturers, emergency management officials, taxpayers, Americans reliant on critical goods during crises
bills.ticker_alert: 🏭 Bill would require federal agencies to hire Defense Production Act specialists

bills.title: DPA Workforce and Skilled Labor Needs Act of 2026
bills.plain_title: Defense Production Act Workforce Training and Labor Study
bills.deck: Requires a study on skilled labor shortages affecting U.S. defense manufacturing and critical industries.
bills.plain_summary: This bill directs a formal assessment of workforce and skilled labor gaps in industries covered by the Defense Production Act, which governs U.S. production of critical goods like weapons, semiconductors, and medical supplies. The goal is to identify shortages and inform future policy solutions for defense and national security manufacturing. The bill has been referred to the House Committee on Financial Services and has not yet been voted on.

impacts.text: A study alone does not fund job training programs, meaning workers may wait years before any real help arrives.
Identifying specific skilled labor gaps could lead to targeted investments that create well-paying manufacturing and defense jobs for Americans.
The bill's findings could be used to argue for expanding apprenticeship programs, union workforce pipelines, or corporate training subsidies — a contested policy debate.

bills.who_affected: Defense manufacturing workers, semiconductor and tech industry employees, military contractors, vocational and trade school students, rural and industrial communities
bills.ticker_alert: 🔍 Congress orders study on skilled worker shortages in U.S. defense industries

bills.title: Polisario Front Terrorist Designation Act
bills.plain_title: Label a North African Separatist Group as Terrorists
bills.deck: This bill would officially designate the Polisario Front as a foreign terrorist organization.
bills.plain_summary: This bill would require the U.S. government to formally label the Polisario Front — a group seeking independence for Western Sahara from Morocco — as a foreign terrorist organization and a specially designated global terrorist group. That designation would trigger legal consequences including asset freezes and bans on providing material support. The bill has been referred to the House Foreign Affairs and Judiciary Committees, where it awaits further consideration.

impacts.text: Could strain U.S. diplomatic relations with Algeria, which backs the Polisario Front, complicating North Africa policy and counterterrorism cooperation.
Supporters say it would protect Americans and allies by cutting off any financial or logistical support to a group they argue has terror ties.
The Polisario Front's status is disputed — Morocco views it as a terrorist group, while the UN recognizes it as a legitimate representative of the Sahrawi people.

bills.who_affected: U.S. diplomats and foreign policy officials, American businesses operating in North Africa, Moroccan-American and Sahrawi-American communities, U.S. aid and humanitarian workers in the Western Sahara region
bills.ticker_alert: 🚨 Bill would label Western Sahara separatist group Polisario Front as terrorists

bills.title: To amend the Internal Revenue Code of 1986 to extend the credit period for the production of refined coal, and for other purposes.
bills.plain_title: Extend Tax Credit for Refined Coal Production
bills.deck: This bill would give coal producers more time to claim a special tax credit for making cleaner-burning refined coal.
bills.plain_summary: HR 7070 proposes extending the tax credit period that coal producers can use when they manufacture refined coal, which is coal treated to reduce pollution when burned. The bill was referred to the House Committee on Ways and Means, meaning it is in early stages and has not yet been voted on. Its sponsor seeks to give the refined coal industry more financial incentive to continue producing a product that burns with fewer emissions than standard coal.

impacts.text: Extends government subsidies for coal, a fossil fuel, potentially slowing investment in renewable energy alternatives and adding to the federal deficit.
Refined coal burns with lower emissions of mercury and nitrogen oxides, so extending this credit could result in modest air quality improvements near coal-burning power plants.
Experts debate whether refined coal tax credits meaningfully reduce pollution or primarily benefit investors who structure deals around the credit rather than drive genuine environmental improvement.

bills.who_affected: Coal producers, coal-burning power plant operators, energy investors, communities near coal plants, federal taxpayers
bills.ticker_alert: ⚡ Congress considers extending tax breaks for refined coal producers

bills.title: To amend the National Agricultural Research, Extension, and Teaching Policy Act of 1977 to make improvements to the veterinary services grant program, and for other purposes.
bills.plain_title: Improving Grants for Rural Veterinary Services
bills.deck: This bill updates a federal grant program that helps place veterinarians in underserved rural communities.
bills.plain_summary: This bill modifies an existing federal grant program designed to address veterinarian shortages in rural and underserved areas by improving how grants are awarded and administered. The program helps fund veterinary education in exchange for service commitments in communities that struggle to attract animal doctors. The bill has been referred to the House Committee on Agriculture, meaning it has not yet been voted on.

impacts.text: Changes to grant criteria could shift funding away from some regions or veterinary schools currently benefiting from the existing program structure.
Rural farmers and pet owners in underserved areas could gain better access to veterinary care, protecting livestock and food supply chains.
The bill adjusts administrative rules for an existing program rather than creating a new one, so its real-world effect depends heavily on implementation details not yet public.

bills.who_affected: Rural farmers and ranchers, livestock owners, rural pet owners, veterinary students, rural veterinary practices, agricultural communities in underserved areas
bills.ticker_alert: 🐄 Bill aims to boost veterinarian access in rural and underserved communities

bills.title: Eastern Mediterranean Gateway Act
bills.plain_title: U.S. Partnership Strategy for Eastern Mediterranean Region
bills.deck: This bill directs the U.S. to strengthen diplomatic and security ties with Eastern Mediterranean allies like Greece, Cyprus, and Israel.
bills.plain_summary: The Eastern Mediterranean Gateway Act instructs the U.S. government to develop a formal strategy for deepening partnerships with countries in the Eastern Mediterranean, including Greece, Cyprus, and Israel, covering areas like energy, security, and trade. It was referred to the House Committee on Foreign Affairs, meaning it is in early review stages and has not yet been voted on. The bill reflects congressional interest in countering Turkish influence and Russian activity in the region while securing U.S. strategic interests.

impacts.text: Could strain U.S.-Turkey relations, a NATO ally, potentially complicating military cooperation and increasing regional tensions that American troops may navigate.
May open new trade and energy partnership opportunities for U.S. companies in the Eastern Mediterranean's growing natural gas sector.
Shifts U.S. diplomatic focus and resources toward a specific regional bloc, a strategy that analysts debate in terms of long-term effectiveness.

bills.who_affected: U.S. defense contractors, energy companies with Mediterranean interests, Greek-American and Jewish-American communities, U.S. military personnel in the region, foreign policy professionals
bills.ticker_alert: 🌐 Congress considers bill to boost U.S. ties with Greece, Cyprus, Israel

bills.title: Motorsports Fairness and Permanency Act of 2025
bills.plain_title: Permanent Tax Break for Motorsports Racing Facilities
bills.deck: Makes a tax depreciation benefit for motorsports racing tracks permanent instead of requiring periodic renewal.
bills.plain_summary: This bill would permanently extend a tax provision that allows motorsports entertainment complexes — like NASCAR and other racing facilities — to depreciate their assets over 7 years instead of the standard 15-39 years, reducing their tax burden. Without this bill, the tax break expires and must be repeatedly renewed by Congress. The bill has been referred to the House Ways and Means Committee, where it awaits further review.

impacts.text: Reduces federal tax revenue, potentially shifting the tax burden to other businesses or individuals not receiving similar breaks
Racing facility owners and operators get long-term tax certainty, potentially supporting jobs and investment at motorsports venues
Critics call this a special-interest carve-out, while supporters argue it levels the playing field with other entertainment industries

bills.who_affected: Motorsports facility owners, NASCAR track operators, racing venue employees, fans of motorsports, federal taxpayers
bills.ticker_alert: 🏁 Bill would permanently lock in tax breaks for NASCAR-style racing tracks

bills.title: To amend title 5, United States Code, to improve access to workers' compensation for civilian Federal employees exposed to toxic burn pits, and for other purposes.
bills.plain_title: Workers' Comp for Federal Employees Exposed to Burn Pits
bills.deck: Makes it easier for civilian federal workers sickened by toxic burn pit exposure to receive workers' compensation benefits.
bills.plain_summary: This bill would change federal law to help civilian government employees who were exposed to toxic burn pits — open-air fires used to dispose of waste at military bases — access workers' compensation more easily. It would likely streamline the claims process and potentially create presumptions that certain illnesses are linked to burn pit exposure. The bill was referred to the House Committee on Education and Workforce, meaning it is in an early stage and has not yet been voted on.

impacts.text: Expanded presumptive eligibility could increase federal workers' compensation costs for taxpayers over time.
Civilian federal workers with burn pit-related illnesses could receive faster, fairer compensation without lengthy proof-of-exposure battles.
The scope of which illnesses qualify and which worksites count as burn pit locations remains subject to regulatory interpretation.

bills.who_affected: Civilian federal employees deployed near military burn pits, Department of Defense contractors, federal workers' compensation program administrators, taxpayers
bills.ticker_alert: 🔥 Bill would ease workers' comp access for feds exposed to toxic burn pits

bills.title: Condemning attacks on civilians in Sudan and calling for an end to external support to the warring parties and for efforts to promote a negotiated settlement of the war.
bills.plain_title: Congress Condemns Sudan War, Calls for Peace Negotiations
bills.deck: This resolution condemns attacks on Sudanese civilians and urges foreign powers to stop fueling the ongoing civil war.
bills.plain_summary: This House resolution formally condemns attacks on civilians in Sudan's ongoing civil war and calls on outside countries to stop sending weapons or support to the warring factions. It also urges efforts to broker a negotiated peace settlement. The resolution was referred to the House Committee on Foreign Affairs and has not yet been voted on by the full House.

impacts.text: The resolution is non-binding and critics argue it does nothing concrete to stop the violence or protect aid workers in Sudan.
May signal U.S. political will to pressure foreign governments backing Sudan's warring parties, potentially supporting diplomatic efforts.
As a symbolic resolution rather than a law, it carries no legal force and creates no new policy, funding, or sanctions.

bills.who_affected: Sudanese-Americans, humanitarian aid organizations, U.S. foreign policy officials, American diplomats in the region
bills.ticker_alert: 🕊️ House resolution urges end to Sudan war, no binding action taken

bills.title: Stop Shackling and Detaining Pregnant Women Act
bills.plain_title: Ban on Shackling Pregnant Women in Federal Custody
bills.deck: This bill would prohibit using physical restraints on pregnant women held in federal detention facilities.
bills.plain_summary: This bill would ban federal detention facilities from using shackles, chains, or other physical restraints on pregnant women during pregnancy, labor, childbirth, and the postpartum period. It would also restrict the solitary confinement of pregnant detainees. The bill has been referred to the House Judiciary Committee, where it awaits further consideration before any vote.

impacts.text: Critics argue it could limit officers' ability to manage high-risk detainees who may pose a security or flight risk during transport or procedures.
Medical experts say it would reduce serious health risks to pregnant women and unborn children, including falls, complications during labor, and trauma-related harm.
The bill applies only to federal detention facilities, leaving state and local jails — where most detained pregnant women are held — unaffected without separate state-level action.

bills.who_affected: Pregnant women in federal immigration detention, pregnant federal pretrial detainees, federal Bureau of Prisons inmates, correctional officers, medical staff in federal facilities
bills.ticker_alert: 🤰 Bill would ban shackling pregnant women in federal detention facilities

bills.title: Fallen Servicemembers Religious Heritage Restoration Act
bills.plain_title: Restore Religious Symbols on Fallen Soldiers' Memorials
bills.deck: This bill would allow religious symbols to be placed or restored on federal memorials honoring fallen military servicemembers.
bills.plain_summary: The Fallen Servicemembers Religious Heritage Restoration Act would permit religious symbols, such as crosses or Stars of David, to be displayed on federal memorials dedicated to fallen military members. The bill aims to reverse or prevent removals of such symbols that have occurred due to court rulings citing separation of church and state. It has passed its final congressional action and is considered agreed to without objection.

impacts.text: Critics argue it could violate the First Amendment's Establishment Clause by placing government-endorsed religious symbols on public federal land.
Families of fallen servicemembers may have religious symbols honoring their loved ones preserved or restored on federal memorials.
The bill's scope and enforcement mechanisms remain debated — it is unclear how it would interact with existing court orders requiring symbol removals.

bills.who_affected: Military families, veterans organizations, religious communities, atheist and secular advocacy groups, federal memorial site visitors
bills.ticker_alert: ⚔️ Bill passed restoring religious symbols on fallen soldiers' federal memorials

bills.title: Expanding the Surety Bond Program Act of 2025
bills.plain_title: Expand Small Business Bonding Access Program
bills.deck: This bill would allow more small businesses to access federal surety bond guarantees, making it easier to compete for government contracts.
bills.plain_summary: The Expanding the Surety Bond Program Act of 2025 would broaden the Small Business Administration's surety bond guarantee program, which helps small businesses obtain the bonds required to bid on government construction and service contracts. Many small businesses — especially minority-owned and newer firms — struggle to qualify for bonds on their own, so this program acts as a federal backstop for bonding companies. The bill has been placed on the Senate Legislative Calendar, meaning it is awaiting a floor vote.

impacts.text: Expanding federal bond guarantees increases taxpayer exposure if small businesses default on bonded contracts.
More small and minority-owned businesses could compete for federal contracts, potentially growing their revenues and creating local jobs.
The program's expansion could shift more government contracting away from large established firms toward smaller competitors, reshaping the market.

bills.who_affected: Small business owners, minority-owned businesses, federal contractors, SBA-approved surety bond companies, taxpayers
bills.ticker_alert: 📋 Senate bill would expand federal bonding help for small businesses seeking contracts

bills.title: Access Technology Affordability Act of 2025
bills.plain_title: Tax Credit for Blind and Low-Vision Assistive Technology
bills.deck: Creates a tax credit to help blind and visually impaired Americans afford assistive technology devices.
bills.plain_summary: This bill would create a refundable tax credit of up to $2,000 per year to help people who are blind or have low vision pay for assistive technology such as screen readers, braille displays, and magnification software. It was introduced in the House and has been referred to the Ways and Means Committee, meaning it has not yet been voted on. The goal is to reduce the financial barrier that prevents many visually impaired Americans from accessing tools they need for independence and employment.

impacts.text: Costs the federal government estimated tax revenue, adding modestly to the deficit without a clear offset funding mechanism.
Blind and low-vision Americans could receive up to $2,000 annually to offset the high cost of screen readers and braille devices, which often cost thousands of dollars.
Eligibility and qualifying technology definitions will be set by the IRS, leaving uncertainty about which devices and software actually qualify.

bills.who_affected: Blind Americans, people with low vision, assistive technology manufacturers, disability advocates, taxpayers
bills.ticker_alert: ♿ New bill would offer $2,000 tax credit for blind Americans' assistive tech

bills.title: CREATE Act
bills.plain_title: Tax Incentives for American Creative Workers Act
bills.deck: Creates new tax deductions and credits for artists, musicians, writers, and other creative professionals.
bills.plain_summary: The CREATE Act would allow creative workers — like musicians, authors, visual artists, and performers — to deduct the fair market value of their own creative works when they donate them to nonprofits or cultural institutions, and may include other tax incentives for the creative industry. The bill was referred to the House Committee on Ways and Means, where it awaits further review. It aims to level the tax playing field for creators, who currently can only deduct the cost basis (often near zero) of self-created works they donate.

impacts.text: Could reduce federal tax revenue, potentially adding to the deficit without offsetting cuts elsewhere.
Artists and creative professionals could claim larger tax deductions when donating their work to museums, libraries, or nonprofits.
Determining the 'fair market value' of donated creative works is subjective and could lead to disputes with the IRS.

bills.who_affected: Visual artists, musicians, authors, photographers, filmmakers, nonprofit cultural institutions, museums, libraries, tax professionals
bills.ticker_alert: 🎨 CREATE Act would give artists bigger tax breaks for donating their work

bills.title: Veterans Earned Education Act
bills.plain_title: GI Bill Benefits for Veterans in Prison
bills.deck: This bill would restore education benefits for incarcerated veterans who earned them through military service.
bills.plain_summary: The Veterans Earned Education Act would allow veterans who are incarcerated to use their GI Bill education benefits while in prison or jail. Currently, federal law restricts these benefits for incarcerated individuals, even though veterans earned them through military service. The bill was referred to the House Subcommittee on Economic Opportunity, meaning it is in early stages and has not yet received a full vote.

impacts.text: Critics argue that allowing federal education benefits in prison could be seen as rewarding criminal behavior and may face political opposition from taxpayer-focused groups.
Incarcerated veterans could access education and job training they earned through service, potentially reducing recidivism and easing their transition back into civilian life.
The bill raises a policy debate about whether earned military benefits should follow veterans regardless of their circumstances, or be subject to the same restrictions as other federal aid.

bills.who_affected: Incarcerated veterans, military veterans with GI Bill benefits, prison education program administrators, families of incarcerated veterans
bills.ticker_alert: ⚖️ Bill would restore GI Bill education rights for incarcerated veterans

bills.title: To direct the Secretary of Defense to establish a pilot program for partnerships between covered basic needs banks and military installations to provide diapers and diapering supplies to military families, and for other purposes.
bills.plain_title: Free Diapers for Military Families Pilot Program
bills.deck: This bill would create a program providing free diapers and supplies to military families through partnerships with on-base diaper banks.
bills.plain_summary: This bill directs the Department of Defense to launch a pilot program connecting diaper banks with military installations, so eligible military families can receive free diapers and related supplies. The program addresses diaper need, which can be a financial burden for lower-income service members. The bill has been referred to the House Armed Services Committee, meaning it has not yet been debated or voted on.

impacts.text: Critics may argue the program creates new administrative overhead and ongoing costs for the Pentagon without a clear funding mechanism or proof of necessity.
Low-income military families, especially junior enlisted members, could receive meaningful financial relief from the high recurring cost of diapers.
As a pilot program, its reach is limited and temporary by design, leaving questions about whether it would ever scale into a permanent benefit.

bills.who_affected: Active-duty military families with infants, junior enlisted service members, Department of Defense logistics staff, nonprofit diaper bank organizations
bills.ticker_alert: 👶 Bill proposes free diapers for military families through base partnerships

bills.title: Better CARE for Animals Act of 2025
bills.plain_title: Improved Animal Care Standards in Research Labs
bills.deck: This bill would strengthen federal rules for how animals used in research and testing are housed and cared for.
bills.plain_summary: The Better CARE for Animals Act of 2025 (HR 3112) aims to update and improve the standards for the treatment, housing, and welfare of animals used in scientific research, testing, and exhibition under the Animal Welfare Act. It has been referred to the House Committee on Agriculture, meaning it is in early stages and has not yet received a full congressional vote. The bill was introduced in 2025 as part of ongoing efforts to modernize animal care requirements in federally regulated facilities.

impacts.text: Stricter animal care standards could increase costs for research institutions, potentially slowing some medical or scientific studies.
Animals in federally regulated labs and facilities would receive stronger protections, reducing suffering and improving living conditions.
The bill's effect depends heavily on how new regulations are written and enforced by the USDA, which remains an open question.

bills.who_affected: Research scientists, pharmaceutical companies, university research labs, animal advocacy groups, zoo and exhibition operators, USDA inspectors
bills.ticker_alert: 🐾 House bill proposes stronger federal protections for research and lab animals

bills.title: Puppy Protection Act of 2025
bills.plain_title: Federal Standards for Dog Breeding Facilities
bills.deck: This bill would set stricter federal welfare standards for commercial dog breeders selling puppies.
bills.plain_summary: The Puppy Protection Act of 2025 (HR 2253) would strengthen federal regulations for commercial dog breeders, requiring better living conditions, veterinary care, and handling standards for dogs at breeding facilities. It builds on existing Animal Welfare Act rules to address concerns about inhumane conditions at large-scale breeders, sometimes called 'puppy mills.' The bill has been referred to the House Subcommittee on Livestock, Dairy, and Poultry, meaning it is in early review stages and has not yet been voted on.

impacts.text: Small and mid-sized dog breeders could face higher compliance costs, potentially forcing some out of business or raising puppy prices for consumers.
Dogs in commercial breeding facilities would receive stronger federal protections, including better housing, exercise requirements, and regular veterinary care.
Enforcement would fall to the USDA, raising questions about whether the agency has sufficient resources and inspectors to implement new rules effectively.

bills.who_affected: Commercial dog breeders, pet store owners, prospective puppy buyers, animal welfare advocates, USDA inspectors, veterinarians serving breeding facilities
bills.ticker_alert: 🐾 Bill would impose new federal welfare rules on commercial dog breeders

bills.title: To require certain duties and covered import restrictions with respect to forestry products to be reduced by 50 percent if an importer of such product can demonstrate that such product contains 100 percent raw wood material originating in the United States, and for other purposes.
bills.plain_title: Tax Cut for Importers Using American-Grown Wood
bills.deck: Importers of wood products made entirely from U.S. timber would get a 50% cut in import duties.
bills.plain_summary: This bill would cut import tariffs and trade restrictions in half for companies that import wood products made entirely from raw timber originating in the United States. The idea is to encourage the use of American-grown wood even when products are manufactured abroad. The bill was referred to the House Committee on Ways and Means and has not yet advanced further.

impacts.text: Domestic wood product manufacturers could face cheaper competition from goods processed overseas using American timber, potentially hurting U.S. factory jobs.
American timber harvesters and forestry workers could see increased demand for U.S. raw wood from foreign manufacturers seeking the tariff discount.
Verifying that imported products contain '100 percent' U.S.-origin wood would require new supply-chain tracking, raising questions about enforcement costs and effectiveness.

bills.who_affected: U.S. timber farmers and loggers, domestic lumber mill workers, wood product importers, furniture and construction industries, U.S. Customs and Border Protection
bills.ticker_alert: 🪵 Bill would halve tariffs on wood imports made from 100% American timber

bills.title: Helping Ensure Reliable Opportunities in Child Care for Military Families Act
bills.plain_title: Military Family Child Care Access and Reliability Act
bills.deck: This bill aims to improve access to affordable, reliable child care for military families.
bills.plain_summary: This bill seeks to strengthen child care options for families of active-duty military service members, addressing shortages and affordability challenges at or near military installations. It was referred to the House Committee on Armed Services, where it awaits further review and action. The bill reflects ongoing congressional concern about child care as a readiness and retention issue for the U.S. military.

impacts.text: May require additional federal spending, potentially drawing criticism over budget priorities or implementation costs.
Military families could gain better access to affordable, reliable child care, reducing stress and supporting service member retention and readiness.
The bill's effectiveness depends on how child care programs are structured and funded, which remains subject to debate and future rulemaking.

bills.who_affected: Active-duty military service members, military spouses, military children, Department of Defense child care providers, military installation communities
bills.ticker_alert: 🪖 Bill introduced to boost child care access for military families

bills.title: Fresh Starts for Foster Youth Act
bills.plain_title: Financial Aid for Young Adults Aging Out of Foster Care
bills.deck: Helps former foster youth access federal financial assistance and support services after leaving the foster care system.
bills.plain_summary: The Fresh Starts for Foster Youth Act would expand financial support and services for young people who have aged out of the foster care system, helping them transition to independent adulthood. It passed committee with a unanimous 42-0 vote, suggesting broad bipartisan support. The bill is now eligible for a full House vote.

impacts.text: Expanded benefits programs increase federal spending, potentially adding to the national deficit without guaranteed long-term outcomes.
Former foster youth gain better access to housing, education, and financial resources, reducing homelessness and poverty among this vulnerable group.
The bill expands the role of federal government in supporting adults who were in foster care, raising questions about program administration and oversight.

bills.who_affected: Young adults aging out of foster care, foster care agencies, social workers, state child welfare departments, federal program administrators
bills.ticker_alert: 🌱 Bipartisan bill passes committee to expand support for former foster youth

bills.title: To direct the Secretary of Housing and Urban Development and the Director of the Bureau of the Census to conduct a study and submit a report about how Federal agencies identify and record cases of housing loss in the United States, and for other purposes.
bills.plain_title: Study How Government Tracks Americans Losing Their Homes
bills.deck: This bill orders federal agencies to study and improve how the government records and tracks Americans who lose their housing.
bills.plain_summary: This bill directs the Department of Housing and Urban Development (HUD) and the Census Bureau to work together on a study examining how federal agencies currently track and record housing loss — such as evictions, foreclosures, and homelessness — across the United States. The goal is to identify gaps in data collection and improve how the government measures this problem. The bill was referred to two House committees and has not yet advanced to a vote.

impacts.text: The bill only mandates a study, not actual policy changes, meaning people currently losing housing see no immediate help.
Better national data on housing loss could help lawmakers craft more effective and targeted housing assistance programs.
Federal agencies may need to coordinate new data-sharing systems, which could take time and administrative resources.

bills.who_affected: Renters facing eviction, homeowners in foreclosure, homeless individuals, housing policy researchers, local government planners, federal housing agencies
bills.ticker_alert: 📊 Congress proposes study to better track Americans losing their homes

bills.title: A resolution recognizing and supporting the goals and ideals of National Sexual Assault Awareness and Prevention Month.
bills.plain_title: Senate Support for Sexual Assault Awareness Month
bills.deck: The Senate formally recognizes National Sexual Assault Awareness and Prevention Month and its goals.
bills.plain_summary: This resolution officially expresses the Senate's support for National Sexual Assault Awareness and Prevention Month, which is observed each April. It does not create new laws, spend money, or establish programs — it is a symbolic statement of values and recognition. The resolution passed the Senate unanimously without any changes.

impacts.text: Critics note symbolic resolutions consume legislative time without allocating funding or creating enforceable protections for survivors.
Raises public awareness of sexual assault prevention resources and signals bipartisan Senate commitment to addressing the issue.
As a non-binding resolution, it carries no legal weight, creates no obligations, and does not change existing law or policy.

bills.who_affected: Sexual assault survivors, prevention advocates, nonprofit organizations, law enforcement, healthcare providers, the general public
bills.ticker_alert: 📢 Senate unanimously backs Sexual Assault Awareness Month in symbolic resolution

bills.title: A resolution supporting the designation of April 2026 as the "Month of the Military Child".
bills.plain_title: Designating April 2026 as Month of the Military Child
bills.deck: This resolution officially recognizes April 2026 as a month to honor and support children of U.S. military families.
bills.plain_summary: This Senate resolution asks the U.S. government to formally recognize April 2026 as 'Month of the Military Child,' honoring the unique sacrifices and challenges faced by children of active-duty and veteran military families. It is a symbolic resolution with no binding legal force or spending attached. The bill has been referred to the Senate Committee on Armed Services, where it awaits further action.

impacts.text: As a symbolic resolution, it allocates no funding or resources to actually support military children's needs.
Raises national awareness of the unique hardships military children face, such as frequent moves, school changes, and parental deployments.
The designation is non-binding and ceremonial, meaning its real-world effect depends entirely on how schools, communities, and agencies choose to observe it.

bills.who_affected: Children of active-duty military members, military families, veterans' families, military-connected school communities
bills.ticker_alert: 🎖️ Senate resolution would honor military kids with April 2026 awareness month

bills.title: To amend the Small Business Act to waive the accrual of interest and payments for certain disaster loans for a year, and for other purposes.
bills.plain_title: Disaster Loan Interest Pause for Small Businesses
bills.deck: This bill would pause interest and payments on certain SBA disaster loans for one year.
bills.plain_summary: This bill would temporarily waive the accumulation of interest and require no payments on certain Small Business Administration (SBA) disaster loans for a period of one year. It is designed to give small business owners extra financial breathing room after they've been hit by natural disasters or other qualifying emergencies. The bill has been referred to the House Committee on Small Business, meaning it is in the early stages of the legislative process.

impacts.text: The federal government would forgo loan interest revenue, potentially adding to program costs or national debt.
Small business owners recovering from disasters would have one full year without loan payments, freeing up cash to rebuild and reopen.
It is unclear whether the interest waived would be forgiven entirely or added to the loan balance after the deferral period ends.

bills.who_affected: Small business owners with SBA disaster loans, disaster-affected communities, SBA program administrators, federal taxpayers
bills.ticker_alert: 💼 Bill proposes 1-year payment pause on SBA disaster loans for small businesses

bills.title: Tribal Conservation Priorities Inclusion Act
bills.plain_title: Including Tribal Voices in Federal Conservation Planning
bills.deck: This bill requires federal agencies to include tribal conservation priorities when developing national land and resource management plans.
bills.plain_summary: The Tribal Conservation Priorities Inclusion Act would require federal agencies to formally consider and incorporate the conservation priorities of Native American tribes when creating or updating federal land, water, and natural resource management plans. This bill was referred to the House Committee on Agriculture, meaning it is still in early stages and has not yet been voted on. It aims to give tribal nations a stronger, more formalized role in shaping how federal lands and resources near or connected to their communities are managed.

impacts.text: Could slow federal land management decisions or create new bureaucratic requirements as agencies work to gather and integrate tribal input.
Native American tribes would gain a formal, legally recognized role in conservation planning that protects culturally and spiritually significant lands.
The extent to which tribal priorities would legally bind agency decisions versus serve as advisory input remains unclear without full bill text.

bills.who_affected: Native American tribal nations, federal land managers, ranchers and farmers near tribal lands, conservation and environmental groups, rural communities near federal lands
bills.ticker_alert: 🌿 Bill would give Native tribes formal say in federal conservation plans

bills.title: Farmer to Farmer Education Act of 2025
bills.plain_title: Peer Learning Programs for American Farmers
bills.deck: This bill funds programs where experienced farmers teach new and struggling farmers practical skills.
bills.plain_summary: The Farmer to Farmer Education Act of 2025 would create or expand federal funding for peer-to-peer agricultural education programs, where experienced farmers mentor and train other farmers in sustainable and profitable practices. The bill was introduced in the Senate and has been referred to the Senate Committee on Agriculture, Nutrition, and Forestry, meaning it is in early stages and has not yet received a vote. It aims to strengthen the farming community by spreading practical, real-world knowledge from one farmer to another.

impacts.text: Taxpayer dollars would fund a new federal program whose effectiveness and oversight remain unproven, potentially duplicating existing agricultural extension services.
Beginning and struggling farmers could gain hands-on, practical guidance from experienced peers, potentially improving farm profitability and reducing early farm failures.
The program would work alongside or potentially compete with existing USDA and land-grant university extension programs, raising questions about coordination and overlap.

bills.who_affected: Farmers and ranchers, beginning farmers, agricultural workers, rural communities, USDA staff, taxpayers
bills.ticker_alert: 🌾 Senate bill proposes federally funded farmer-to-farmer mentorship education programs

bills.title: Tim’s Act
bills.plain_title: Forest Worker Safety and Benefits Protection Act
bills.deck: This bill aims to improve safety standards and workers' compensation protections for federal forestry workers.
bills.plain_summary: Tim's Act is a bill designed to strengthen workplace safety rules and ensure fair workers' compensation benefits for federal forestry and forest service employees. It is named in honor of a worker who died on the job, reflecting concerns about dangerous conditions in federal forest work. The bill has been referred to the House Subcommittee on Forestry and Horticulture, meaning it is in early stages and has not yet been voted on.

impacts.text: Could increase federal agency costs for expanded workers' comp coverage, potentially straining forestry program budgets.
Forest Service and federal forestry workers would gain stronger safety protections and more reliable compensation if injured on the job.
Implementation would require new agency regulations and oversight processes, whose effectiveness and cost depend on final bill details.

bills.who_affected: Federal forestry workers, U.S. Forest Service employees, federal contractors in forestry, families of injured or deceased forest workers
bills.ticker_alert: 🌲 Tim's Act seeks stronger safety protections for federal forestry workers

bills.title: No Capital Gains Tax on Family Farms Act
bills.plain_title: No Capital Gains Tax When Family Farms Are Inherited
bills.deck: This bill would eliminate capital gains taxes when family farms are passed down to heirs.
bills.plain_summary: The No Capital Gains Tax on Family Farms Act would exempt inherited family farms from federal capital gains taxes, meaning heirs would not owe taxes on the increased value of farmland when they inherit it. This addresses concerns that high land values force farm families to sell or borrow heavily just to pay an inherited tax bill. The bill has been referred to the House Committee on Ways and Means, where most tax legislation begins its review.

impacts.text: The federal government loses tax revenue on appreciated farmland assets, potentially shifting the tax burden to other Americans or adding to the deficit.
Farm families can inherit and continue operating family farms without being forced to sell land just to cover a large tax bill.
Critics argue existing stepped-up basis rules already reduce capital gains exposure at inheritance, questioning how much additional relief this bill provides.

bills.who_affected: Farm families, agricultural landowners, rural communities, federal taxpayers, estate attorneys, agricultural lenders
bills.ticker_alert: 🌾 Bill would eliminate capital gains taxes on inherited family farmland

bills.title: Logan's Law
bills.plain_title: Federal Protection for Pets from Domestic Abusers
bills.deck: This bill makes it a federal crime for domestic abusers to harm or kill their victim's pets.
bills.plain_summary: Logan's Law would make it a federal offense for someone to harm, kill, or threaten a pet as a way to intimidate or control a domestic violence victim. The bill is named after a pet harmed during a domestic abuse situation and targets abusers who use animals as leverage over their partners or family members. It was referred to the House Committee on the Judiciary, where it awaits further review before any vote.

impacts.text: Adds new federal criminal charges, potentially increasing prison sentences and federal caseloads for what some argue should remain state-level offenses
Gives domestic violence victims stronger legal protection by criminalizing a common abuse tactic that often escalates to violence against people
All 50 states already have animal cruelty laws, raising debate over whether federal involvement duplicates existing protections or meaningfully strengthens them

bills.who_affected: Domestic violence survivors, pet owners in abusive households, animal welfare advocates, federal prosecutors, domestic abusers
bills.ticker_alert: 🐾 New bill would make harming a domestic violence victim's pet a federal crime

bills.title: To providing access to certain memorials on Federal property during Government shutdown, and for other purposes.
bills.plain_title: Keep War Memorials Open During Government Shutdowns
bills.deck: This bill requires that outdoor war memorials on federal property stay accessible to the public even when the government shuts down.
bills.plain_summary: This bill would ensure that open-air memorials on federal land — such as the World War II Memorial or Vietnam Veterans Memorial in Washington, D.C. — remain accessible to visitors during a government shutdown, rather than being barricaded or closed. It was introduced in the House of Representatives and has been referred to the House Committee on Oversight and Government Reform, meaning it has not yet been voted on. The bill responds to past shutdowns where veterans and tourists were turned away from outdoor monuments.

impacts.text: Critics argue the bill could create confusion about which federal sites are essential, potentially straining the limited staff available during a shutdown to maintain safety and cleanliness.
Veterans, tourists, and families — including those on once-in-a-lifetime trips — would no longer be blocked from visiting open-air memorials during a government shutdown.
The bill raises a broader debate about which federal services and sites should be deemed essential during shutdowns, a question Congress has not fully resolved.

bills.who_affected: Veterans, military families, tourists visiting Washington D.C., National Park Service employees, federal workers
bills.ticker_alert: 🏛️ Bill would keep war memorials open for visitors during government shutdowns

bills.title: A resolution expressing support for the staff of public, school, academic, and special libraries in the United States and the essential services those libraries provide to communities, recognizing the need for funding commensurate with the broad scope of social service and community supports provided by libraries, preserving the right of all citizens of the United States to freely access information and resources in their communities, supporting a strong union voice for library workers, and defending the civil rights of library staff.
bills.plain_title: Resolution Supporting Public Libraries and Library Workers
bills.deck: This resolution expresses congressional support for libraries, their staff, funding, and free public access to information.
bills.plain_summary: This is a Senate resolution that declares support for public, school, academic, and special library staff across the United States. It calls for adequate funding for libraries, defends the right of all Americans to access information freely, supports union rights for library workers, and backs the civil rights of library employees. Resolutions like this are symbolic statements of congressional intent and do not carry the force of law or directly allocate money. It has been referred to the Senate Health, Education, Labor, and Pensions Committee.

impacts.text: The resolution is non-binding and allocates no actual funding, so libraries facing budget cuts receive no direct financial relief.
Signals congressional backing for library funding and free information access, which can strengthen advocacy efforts at the state and local level.
The resolution's support for union organizing for library workers is welcomed by labor advocates but viewed skeptically by those who prefer local control over public employee bargaining.

bills.who_affected: Public library patrons, library workers and staff, school librarians, academic librarians, labor unions representing public employees, local governments funding libraries
bills.ticker_alert: 📚 Senate resolution backs libraries, free info access, and worker rights

bills.title: Expressing the sense of the House of Representatives in support of the integration of Kosovo into the North Atlantic Treaty Organization.
bills.plain_title: House Support for Kosovo Joining NATO
bills.deck: This resolution expresses Congress's support for Kosovo becoming a full member of NATO.
bills.plain_summary: This is a non-binding resolution where the House of Representatives formally states its support for Kosovo joining NATO, the military alliance that includes the United States and 31 other countries. It does not create law, spend money, or require any government action — it simply expresses an official opinion of the House. The resolution has been referred to the House Committee on Foreign Affairs, where it awaits further review.

impacts.text: NATO membership for Kosovo could obligate U.S. military defense of a small, disputed territory, potentially drawing American troops into future Balkan conflicts.
Kosovo's NATO integration could stabilize a volatile region of Europe, reducing the likelihood of costly U.S. humanitarian or military interventions down the road.
This resolution carries no legal force and does not commit the U.S. to any action — it is a symbolic political statement that may or may not influence actual NATO policy.

bills.who_affected: U.S. military personnel, foreign policy professionals, defense contractors, American taxpayers, Balkan-American communities
bills.ticker_alert: 🌍 House resolution backs Kosovo joining NATO military alliance

bills.title: Condemning antisemitic hate-filled rhetoric and content disseminated by prominent online personalities, and urging social media platforms and public leaders to denounce and address such conduct.
bills.plain_title: Resolution Condemning Online Antisemitic Hate Speech
bills.deck: This resolution condemns antisemitic content by online influencers and urges social media platforms and leaders to take action against it.
bills.plain_summary: This is a House Resolution — a symbolic statement, not a binding law — that condemns antisemitic rhetoric spread by prominent social media personalities. It urges social media companies and public figures to publicly denounce and address such content. The resolution has been referred to the House Judiciary and Energy and Commerce Committees, where most resolutions of this type await further consideration.

impacts.text: Critics warn it could set a precedent for government signaling pressure on platforms to restrict speech, raising First Amendment concerns.
Jewish Americans and other targeted communities may benefit from a clear, official congressional condemnation of hate speech directed at them.
As a non-binding resolution, it carries no legal enforcement power and creates no penalties, making its real-world impact largely symbolic.

bills.who_affected: Jewish Americans, social media users, online content creators, social media platform companies, civil liberties advocates
bills.ticker_alert: 📢 Congress condemns antisemitic online influencers in new symbolic resolution

bills.title: Calling on elected officials and civil society leaders to counter antisemitism and educate the public on the contributions of the Jewish-American community.
bills.plain_title: Resolution to Combat Antisemitism and Honor Jewish Americans
bills.deck: This resolution urges elected officials and community leaders to fight antisemitism and promote Jewish-American contributions.
bills.plain_summary: This is a House Resolution calling on elected officials and civil society leaders to actively counter antisemitism and educate the public about the history and contributions of Jewish Americans. It is a non-binding resolution, meaning it does not create new laws, mandates, or funding — it simply expresses the sense of Congress. The resolution has been referred to the House Judiciary and Armed Services Committees for review.

impacts.text: Critics argue non-binding resolutions like this can be used selectively to shield one group from criticism while other communities facing hate crimes receive less congressional attention.
The resolution sends a clear public signal against antisemitism, which has risen sharply in recent years, and may encourage local leaders to take concrete educational and protective actions.
As a symbolic, non-binding resolution, it carries no legal force, creates no new programs, and allocates no funding — its real-world effect depends entirely on voluntary action by leaders.

bills.who_affected: Jewish Americans, educators, civil society organizations, religious institutions, military communities, local elected officials
bills.ticker_alert: ✊ Congress urges leaders to fight antisemitism and honor Jewish-American heritage

bills.title: Expressing support for United States forces to remain as a part of the Kosovo Force.
bills.plain_title: Keep U.S. Troops in Kosovo Peacekeeping Mission
bills.deck: This resolution expresses Congressional support for keeping American soldiers in the NATO-led Kosovo peacekeeping force.
bills.plain_summary: This resolution urges that U.S. military personnel remain part of KFOR, the NATO-led peacekeeping mission in Kosovo that has operated since 1999. It does not create new law or spending but signals Congressional support for continued American military presence in the Balkans region. The resolution was referred to the House Committee on Foreign Affairs, where most resolutions like this begin their review process.

impacts.text: U.S. troops and their families continue bearing the cost and risk of an overseas deployment with no clear end date
Continued U.S. presence may help prevent renewed ethnic conflict in the Balkans, reducing risk of a broader European crisis
This is a non-binding resolution with no legal force, so it expresses an opinion but does not compel any executive action

bills.who_affected: U.S. military service members deployed to Kosovo, military families, NATO allies, Balkans regional stakeholders, U.S. taxpayers funding the mission
bills.ticker_alert: 🪖 Congress signals support for keeping U.S. troops in Kosovo peacekeeping mission

bills.title: Air Permitting Improvements to Protect National Security Act of 2025
bills.plain_title: Speed Up Air Permits Near Military Bases
bills.deck: This bill fast-tracks air quality permits for projects near military facilities to protect national security operations.
bills.plain_summary: This bill would streamline the EPA air permitting process for construction and industrial projects located near military installations, aiming to prevent permit delays from disrupting defense activities. It was introduced in the House and has been placed on the Union Calendar, meaning it is eligible for floor consideration. Supporters say it protects military readiness, while critics worry it could weaken environmental oversight near communities that already live close to defense sites.

impacts.text: Communities near military bases could face reduced environmental review of air pollution from nearby industrial projects
Defense contractors and developers near military sites would face fewer permit delays, potentially supporting national security projects
Changes how EPA balances environmental review timelines against national security considerations, a tradeoff that experts debate

bills.who_affected: Residents near military bases, defense contractors, industrial developers, EPA regulators, local environmental agencies
bills.ticker_alert: ⚡ Bill would speed up air permits near military bases, raising community concerns

bills.title: NFA SBS Act
bills.plain_title: Remove Short-Barreled Shotguns from Strict Federal Gun Registry
bills.deck: This bill would take short-barreled shotguns off the National Firearms Act's heavily regulated list of weapons.
bills.plain_summary: The NFA SBS Act would remove short-barreled shotguns (shotguns with barrels shorter than 18 inches) from the National Firearms Act, eliminating the current requirement for owners to pay a $200 tax, register the weapon with the federal government, and undergo an extensive background check process. This would make purchasing and owning short-barreled shotguns as straightforward as buying a standard rifle or handgun. The bill has been referred to the House Committees on Ways and Means and the Judiciary, meaning it is in early stages and has not yet been voted on.

impacts.text: Critics warn removing regulatory barriers could make it easier for dangerous individuals to obtain short-barreled shotguns, which are concealable and powerful weapons.
Law-abiding gun owners would no longer face months-long wait times, $200 taxes, and burdensome federal registration to legally own a short-barreled shotgun for home defense or sport.
The federal government would lose NFA tax revenue from short-barreled shotgun registrations, while state-level regulations on these firearms would remain in effect and vary widely.

bills.who_affected: Licensed firearms owners, gun dealers and manufacturers, hunters and sport shooters, home defense gun owners, federal law enforcement agencies, gun control advocates
bills.ticker_alert: 🔫 Bill would deregulate short-barreled shotguns, removing federal NFA restrictions

bills.title: Rural Depositories Revitalization Study Act
bills.plain_title: Study on Reviving Rural Banks and Credit Unions
bills.deck: This bill orders a government study on how to help rural banks and credit unions survive and grow.
bills.plain_summary: This bill directs federal regulators to conduct a study examining why rural banks and credit unions are declining and what policies could help revitalize them. It focuses on the challenges rural communities face in accessing banking services as local financial institutions close. The bill has been placed on the Union Calendar, meaning it has passed committee review and is awaiting a full House vote.

impacts.text: The bill only mandates a study with no guaranteed action, meaning rural communities could wait years for real banking relief while closures continue.
Rural residents who already struggle to access loans, mortgages, and basic banking could benefit if study findings lead to policies that keep local banks open.
The study's recommendations could favor either deregulation of small banks or increased federal support programs, depending on findings — both are contested policy approaches.

bills.who_affected: Rural residents, small-town farmers, rural small business owners, community bank employees, rural credit union members
bills.ticker_alert: 📋 Congress considers study to help save rural banks across America

bills.title: CDFI Fund Transparency Act
bills.plain_title: Make Community Development Finance Fund More Transparent
bills.deck: Requires the CDFI Fund to publicly report more details about how it awards grants and loans to community lenders.
bills.plain_summary: This bill would require the Treasury Department's CDFI Fund — which provides money to lenders that serve low-income and underserved communities — to publicly disclose more information about how it awards funding. The goal is to increase accountability and let taxpayers see exactly where federal dollars are going. The bill has been referred to the House Committee on Financial Services, meaning it is in the early stages of the legislative process.

impacts.text: Increased reporting requirements could burden smaller community lenders with added administrative costs and paperwork.
Taxpayers and communities gain clearer visibility into how federal funds are awarded to local lenders serving low-income neighborhoods.
Greater transparency could shift how the CDFI Fund prioritizes applicants, potentially changing which organizations receive awards.

bills.who_affected: Low-income and rural community residents, Community Development Financial Institutions (CDFIs), small business owners in underserved areas, taxpayers, Treasury Department officials
bills.ticker_alert: 📋 Bill would require greater public disclosure of community lending federal awards

bills.title: Small Bank Holding Company Relief Act
bills.plain_title: Easing Rules for Small Bank Holding Companies
bills.deck: This bill raises the asset threshold allowing small bank holding companies to carry more debt with less federal oversight.
bills.plain_summary: The Small Bank Holding Company Relief Act raises the asset limit under the Federal Reserve's Small Bank Holding Company Policy Statement, making it easier for smaller community banks to take on debt when buying other banks or expanding. This reduces some federal regulatory requirements for these institutions. The bill has been placed on the Union Calendar, meaning it has passed committee and is awaiting a full House vote.

impacts.text: Reduced oversight of small bank holding companies could increase financial risk if leveraged banks face downturns, potentially threatening depositors and local economies.
Community banks gain more flexibility to expand and merge, which could improve banking access and loan availability in rural or underserved areas.
The policy shifts regulatory burden from small banks to broader market forces, a trade-off that economists debate in terms of safety versus economic growth.

bills.who_affected: Community bank customers, small and regional bank holding companies, rural residents relying on local banks, bank investors and shareholders, small business borrowers
bills.ticker_alert: 🏦 House bill would loosen rules for small community bank holding companies

bills.title: HUMPS Act of 2025
bills.plain_title: Federal Hiring Freeze Exception for Military Spouses
bills.deck: This bill would exempt military spouses from federal civilian hiring freezes, making it easier for them to get government jobs.
bills.plain_summary: The HUMPS Act of 2025 (Helping Underemployed Military Partners Succeed Act) would require that military spouses be excluded from any federal civilian hiring freezes, giving them continued access to federal employment opportunities. Military spouses often face chronic unemployment and underemployment due to frequent relocations following their service member's orders. The bill has been placed on the Union Calendar, meaning it has passed committee review and is awaiting a full House vote.

impacts.text: Critics argue it creates a preference class that could be seen as unfair to other qualified civilian job seekers during hiring freezes.
Military spouses, who face an unemployment rate roughly 3 times the national average due to frequent moves, would gain more stable federal job access.
The practical impact depends on how often and broadly future administrations implement federal hiring freezes, which vary widely between administrations.

bills.who_affected: Military spouses, active-duty service members, federal job applicants, Department of Defense families, federal agency HR departments
bills.ticker_alert: ⚖️ Bill would protect military spouses from federal civilian hiring freezes

bills.title: Merchant Banking Modernization Act
bills.plain_title: Bank Investment Rules Update for Large Banks
bills.deck: This bill expands what types of companies large banks can invest in using their own money.
bills.plain_summary: The Merchant Banking Modernization Act updates rules that govern when and how large financial holding companies can make investments in commercial businesses. It loosens restrictions originally set by the Bank Holding Company Act, giving big banks more flexibility to invest in non-financial companies. The bill has been placed on the Union Calendar, meaning it has passed committee and is awaiting a full House vote.

impacts.text: Critics warn it could blur the line between banking and commerce, increasing systemic financial risk if banks make bad bets with federally insured funds.
Supporters say it helps U.S. banks compete globally and could channel more capital into startups and growing businesses, potentially creating jobs.
The bill primarily changes how long banks can hold merchant banking investments and updates procedural rules, with effects largely felt in financial markets rather than by most consumers directly.

bills.who_affected: Large bank holding companies, private equity investors, startup businesses seeking investment, bank regulators, Wall Street financial firms
bills.ticker_alert: 🏦 Congress considers loosening investment rules for large U.S. banks

bills.title: Electing a Member to a certain standing committee of the House of Representatives.
bills.plain_title: House Committee Seat Assignment Resolution
bills.deck: This resolution assigns a specific House member to fill a vacant seat on a standing committee.
bills.plain_summary: This resolution formally elects a member of the House of Representatives to serve on a specific standing committee. Committee assignments are routine procedural actions that determine which lawmakers oversee particular policy areas like defense, finance, or education. The resolution has already passed, as the motion to reconsider was laid on the table without objection.

impacts.text: Reshuffling committee membership can disrupt ongoing legislative work or shift a committee's ideological balance in ways voters may not prefer.
Filling a committee vacancy ensures full representation and working capacity on oversight and legislative duties that affect Americans.
Committee assignments reflect internal House power dynamics and party negotiations, which can influence which bills get hearings or votes.

bills.who_affected: Members of Congress, constituents of the assigned representative, stakeholders in the policy areas the committee oversees
bills.ticker_alert: 🏛️ House assigns member to standing committee in routine procedural vote

bills.plain_title: Update Address for Specialist Jeffrey L. White Jr. Post Office

White Jr., a U.S. military servicemember. It is a housekeeping measure to ensure official records accurately reflect the facility's current address. The bill was referred to the House Committee on Oversight and Government Reform and has not yet advanced further.

impacts.text: Requires administrative work and minor government resources to update official records and signage.
Ensures the memorial dedication to a fallen soldier is accurately recorded and properly honored in official documents.
This is a largely procedural change with no policy implications beyond correcting a government record.

bills.who_affected: U.S. Postal Service staff at the affected facility, residents of the local community, family of Specialist Jeffrey L. White Jr.
